title: EXPOSÉ XV COMPLEMENTS ON THE SUBTORI OF A GROUP PRESCHEME. APPLICATION TO SMOOTH GROUPS.

pages.title: EXPOSÉ XV COMPLEMENTS ON THE SUBTORI OF A GROUP PRESCHEME. APPLICATION TO SMOOTH GROUPS.

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XV
COMPLEMENTS ON THE SUBTORI OF A GROUP PRESCHEME. APPLICATION TO SMOOTH GROUPS.by M. Raynaud0∗

footnotes.html: This exposé and the two following ones (Exp. XVI and XVII) do not correspond to oral exposés of the seminar. They develop, with some complements, the substance of (succinct) handwritten notes of A. Grothendieck, written in 1964, on the occasion of the present seminar.

blocks.html: 0. Introduction

This exposé completes and partially takes up again exposés XI and XII; familiarity with exposés XIII and XIV is not indispensable. Pursuing the effort undertaken in XII, we shall work with S-group preschemes that are not necessarily affine and not necessarily separated over S.

Sections 1, 2, 3, 4 are devoted to the study of the subtori of a group prescheme. We obtain infinitesimal (§ 2) and global (§ 4) lifting theorems, in which an essential role is played by the points of finite order (§ 1).

Sections 5, 6 and 7 are independent of the preceding sections. The consideration of infinitesimal neighbourhoods leads to the representability of the functor of smooth subgroups equal to their connected normalizer (§ 5). In §§ 6 and 7, we take a closer interest in Cartan subgroups.
Finally, in § 8 we give a necessary and sufficient condition for the functor of subtori of a smooth group, or that of maximal tori, to be representable.
1. Lifting of finite subgroups
1. Finite, smooth and central subgroups of multiplicative type

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.1

Let S be an affine scheme, \(\mathrm{S}_0\) a closed subscheme of S defined by an ideal of square zero, G an S-group prescheme, \(\mathrm{H}_0\) a subgroup scheme of \(\mathrm{G}_0 = \mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0\), which is smooth over \(\mathrm{S}_0\), of finite type, and of multiplicative type. Then, for there to exist a subgroup scheme of G, of multiplicative type, which lifts \(\mathrm{H}_0\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist a subscheme \(\mathrm{H}'\) of G, flat over S, which lifts \(\mathrm{H}_0\).

The necessity of the condition is quite clear; let us prove the sufficiency. The group of multiplicative type \(\mathrm{H}_0\) is quasi-isotrivial (X 4.5); by Exp. X 2.1, there exist an S-group of multiplicative type H and an \(\mathrm{S}_0\)-isomorphism of groups: \[ u_0 : \mathrm{H} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{S}_0 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{H}_0. \] Since \(\mathrm{H}'\) is flat over S and \(\mathrm{H}' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0\) is of finite presentation over \(\mathrm{S}_0\) (Exp. IX 2.1 b)), \(\mathrm{H}'\) is of finite presentation over S; moreover, its fibres are smooth, so \(\mathrm{H}'\) is smooth over S (EGA IV 17.5.1). The scheme S being affine, \(u_0\) therefore lifts to an S-morphism of preschemes: \[ u : \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}'. \] It then follows from Exp. III 2.1 and from Exp. IX 3.1 that the composite morphism \(v_0 : \mathrm{H} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0 \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{H}_0 \to \mathrm{G}_0\) also lifts to an S-morphism of groups: \[ v : \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}. \] Since \(v_0\) is an immersion, so is \(v\). The image of H under \(v\) is therefore a subgroup scheme of G, of multiplicative type, which lifts \(\mathrm{H}_0\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.2

Let S be a prescheme, \(\mathrm{S}_0\) a subprescheme of S defined by a locally nilpotent sheaf of ideals, G an S-group prescheme, flat and of finite presentation over S, and \(\mathrm{H}_0\) a subgroup scheme of \(\mathrm{G}_0 = \mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0\) which is smooth, finite over \(\mathrm{S}_0\), of multiplicative type and central. Then there exists a unique subgroup scheme H of G, of multiplicative type, which lifts \(\mathrm{H}_0\). Moreover H is central. (See XVII App. III, 1).

blocks.label: 1.2 bis
blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.2 bis

Let S, G, \(\mathrm{S}_0\), \(\mathrm{G}_0\) be as above, H an S-group scheme, of multiplicative type, smooth and finite over S, and \(u_0 : \mathrm{H}_0 = \mathrm{H} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0 \to \mathrm{G}_0\) a central homomorphism. Then \(u_0\) lifts in a unique way to a homomorphism \(u : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\). Moreover \(u\) is central.
The existence of the lifting \(u\) in 1.2 bis is easily deduced from 1.2 by considering the graph of \(u_0\). The lifting \(u\) is unique and central by Exp. IX 3.4 and Exp. IX 5.1.

Proof of 1.2. The uniqueness of H and the fact that H is central follow from Exp. IX 5.6 b) and from Exp. IX 3.4 bis. Taking the uniqueness into account, to prove the existence of H we may assume S affine and \(\mathrm{S}_0\) defined by an ideal of square zero, and it suffices (1.1) to find a subscheme of G, flat over S, which lifts \(\mathrm{H}_0\).

Since \(\mathrm{H}_0\) is smooth and finite over \(\mathrm{S}_0\), we may assume, after restricting S if necessary, that there exists an integer \(n > 0\), invertible on S, such that \(\mathrm{H}_0 = {}_n\mathrm{H}_0\). Consider the \(n^{\text{th}}\)-power morphism in G: \[ u : \mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}, \qquad x \mapsto x^n. \] Denote again by \({}_n\mathrm{G}\) the "kernel of \(u\)", i.e. the inverse image under \(u\) of the unit section of G (N.B. \(u\) is not in general a group morphism). Let us grant for a moment the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.3

Let \(k\) be a field, G a group scheme locally of finite type over \(k\), \(n\) an integer prime to the characteristic of \(k\), \(u\) the \(n^{\text{th}}\)-power morphism in G. Then \(u\) is étale at every point \(x\) of G belonging to the centre of G.

Since G is flat and of finite presentation over S, it follows from the preceding lemma and from EGA IV 17.8.2 that if \(x\) is a point of G projecting to \(s\) in S and belonging to the centre of \(\mathrm{G}_s\), the morphism \(u\) is étale at \(x\). If moreover \(x\) is a point of \({}_n\mathrm{G}\), then \({}_n\mathrm{G}\) is étale over S at \(x\). By hypothesis, the group \(\mathrm{H}_0\) is central and contained in \({}_n\mathrm{G}_0\), so it is in fact contained in the largest open subset V of \({}_n\mathrm{G}\) which is étale over S. Since \(\mathrm{H}_0\) and \(\mathrm{V} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0 = \mathrm{V}_0\) are étale over \(\mathrm{S}_0\), \(\mathrm{H}_0\) is an open subset of \(\mathrm{V}_0\) (EGA IV 17.8.7 and 17.9.1). But then the open subprescheme of V having the same underlying space as \(\mathrm{H}_0\) is a subscheme of G, flat over S, which lifts \(\mathrm{H}_0\).

It remains for us to prove Lemma 1.3. For this, note that the largest open subset of G on which \(u\) is étale is invariant under extension of the base field (EGA IV 17.8.2); this allows us to reduce to the case where \(x\) is rational over \(k\). Denote by \(t_x\) the translation by \(x\), which is a \(k\)-automorphism of the scheme G. Since \(x\) is in the centre of G, we have the commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{G} \ar[r]^u \ar[d]_{t_x} & \mathrm{G} \ar[d]^{t_x^n} \\ \mathrm{G} \ar[r]_u & \mathrm{G} } \] It therefore suffices to show that \(u\) is étale at the origin, but this was seen in VIIA 8.4.
2. Global lifting of finite groups

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.4

Let A be a local ring, separated and complete for the topology defined by its maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\), \(\mathrm{S} = \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{A})\), \(\mathrm{S}_n = \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{A}/\mathfrak{m}^n)\). Then for every prescheme X (resp. for every S-prescheme X), the canonical map: \[ (\mathrm{x}) \qquad \operatorname{Hom}(\mathrm{S}, \mathrm{X}) \longrightarrow \varprojlim_n \operatorname{Hom}(\mathrm{S}_n, \mathrm{X}) \] (resp.: \[ (\mathrm{xx}) \qquad \Gamma(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S}) \longrightarrow \varprojlim_n \Gamma(\mathrm{X}_n/\mathrm{S}_n), \quad \text{where} \quad \mathrm{X}_n = \mathrm{X} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{S}_n) \] is bijective.
\((\mathrm{xx})\) is an easy consequence of \((\mathrm{x})\). Let us prove \((\mathrm{x})\).

Let \(u_n : \mathrm{S}_n \to \mathrm{X}\) \((n \in \mathbb{N})\) be a coherent system of morphisms. The image \(y\) of the closed point of \(\mathrm{S}_n\) is thus independent of \(n\), and \(u_n\) factors through \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathscr{O}_y\). The morphisms \(u_n\) define, by passage to the projective limit, a ring morphism: \[ \tilde{u} : \mathscr{O}_y \longrightarrow \varprojlim_n (\mathrm{A}/\mathfrak{m}^n) = \mathrm{A}. \] This shows that \((\mathrm{x})\) is surjective; it is injective as soon as A is separated for the \(\mathfrak{m}\)-adic topology.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.5

Let A be a complete noetherian local ring, \(\mathfrak{m}\) its maximal ideal, \(\mathrm{S} = \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{A})\), \(\mathrm{S}_n = \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{A}/\mathfrak{m}^n)\), X a scheme finite over S and Y an S-prescheme. Then the canonical map: \[ \operatorname{Hom}_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}) \longrightarrow \varprojlim_n \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}_n}(\mathrm{X}_n, \mathrm{Y}_n) \] (where \(\mathrm{X}_n = \mathrm{X} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_n\) and likewise \(\mathrm{Y}_n = \cdots\)) is bijective.

Indeed, it follows from EGA II 6.2.5 that X is a finite sum of local S-schemes finite over S. This reduces us to the case where X itself is the spectrum of a complete noetherian local ring. But \(\operatorname{Hom}_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}) = \Gamma(\mathrm{Z}/\mathrm{X})\) where Z is the X-prescheme \(\mathrm{Y} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{X}\), and we apply the preceding proposition.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.6

Let A, S, \(\mathrm{S}_n\) be as above and let G and M be two S-group preschemes, M being finite over S. Then:

a) The canonical map: \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{M}, \mathrm{G}) \longrightarrow \varprojlim_n \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}_n\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{M}_n, \mathrm{G}_n) \] is bijective.

b) If M is of multiplicative type and if G is smooth over S, the canonical map: \[ \varphi : \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{M}, \mathrm{G}) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}_0\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{M}_0, \mathrm{G}_0) \] is surjective. Moreover, if \(\varphi(u) = \varphi(u') = u_0\), then \(u\) and \(u'\) are conjugate by an element of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\) reducing to the unit element of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}_0)\).

c) If M is of multiplicative type and smooth over S, if G is flat of finite type over S, and if \(u_0 : \mathrm{M}_0 \to \mathrm{G}_0\) is a central homomorphism, then \(u_0\) lifts in a unique way to a homomorphism \[ u : \mathrm{M} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}. \] Moreover \(u\) is central if G has connected fibres.

Proof. a) Follows from 1.5, from the fact that \(\mathrm{M} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{M}\) is finite over S, and from the characterization of group morphisms as those making commutative the well-known diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{M}\underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times}\mathrm{M} \ar[r]^{u\times u} \ar[d] & \mathrm{G}\underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times}\mathrm{G} \ar[d] \\ \mathrm{M} \ar[r]_u & \mathrm{G} } \]

b) By Exp IX 3.6, one can construct a coherent system of homomorphisms \(u_n : \mathrm{M}_n \to \mathrm{G}_n\) lifting a homomorphism \(u_0 : \mathrm{M}_0 \to \mathrm{G}_0\). Whence the first assertion of b), taking a) into account.

If now \(u\) and \(u'\) are two liftings of \(u_0\), then \(u_n\) and \(u'_n\) are conjugate by an element \(g_n\) of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}_n)\) which lifts the unit element of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}_0)\) (Exp. IX 3.6); loc. cit. also implies that one can choose the \(g_n\) coherently, hence (1.4) coming from a section \(g\) of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\). The morphisms \(u\) and \(\operatorname{int}(g)u'\) then coincide modulo \(\mathfrak{m}^n\) for every \(n\), hence coincide (1.5).
c) The existence and uniqueness of \(u\) follow from a) and from 1.2 bis. If G has connected fibres, \(u\) is central by Exp. IX 5.6 a).
2. Infinitesimal lifting of subtori
1. Statement of the theorem

We are going to give an infinitesimal lifting theorem for the subtori of a group prescheme which does not appeal to smoothness hypotheses (contrary to Exp. IX 3.6 bis) and which, moreover, answers a very natural question1: does the ability to lift "sufficiently many" points of finite order of a subtorus \(\mathrm{T}_0\) suffice to guarantee that \(\mathrm{T}_0\) can be lifted (infinitesimally, of course)?

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1

Let S be a noetherian affine scheme, \(\mathrm{S}_0\) a closed subscheme of S defined by an ideal J of square zero, G an S-group prescheme of finite type, \(\mathrm{G}_0 = \mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0\), \(\mathrm{T}_0\) a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_0\), \(q\) an integer \(> 0\) invertible on S. Suppose that for every integer \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\), there exists a subscheme \(\mathrm{M}_n\) of G, flat over S, such that \(\mathrm{M}_n \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0 = {}_n\mathrm{T}_0\). Then there exists a subtorus T of G such that \(\mathrm{T} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0 = \mathrm{T}_0\).
Theorem 2.1 will be useful to us through the two following corollaries:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.2

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(\mathrm{S}_0\) a closed subprescheme of S defined by a locally nilpotent sheaf of ideals, G an S-group prescheme of finite type, \(\mathrm{T}_0\) a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_0 = \mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0\), \(q\) an integer \(> 0\) invertible on S; finally, the integer \(n\) running over the powers of \(q\), let \((\mathrm{M}_n)\) be a coherent system of S-subgroup schemes of G, of multiplicative type, lifting the \({}_n\mathrm{T}_0\) (N.B. The system of subgroups of multiplicative type \((\mathrm{M}_n)\) is said to be coherent if \(\mathrm{M}_m = {}_m(\mathrm{M}_n)\) whenever the integer \(m\) divides \(n\).) Then there exists one and only one subtorus T of G such that \(\mathrm{T} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0 = \mathrm{T}_0\) and \({}_n\mathrm{T} = \mathrm{M}_n\) for every \(n\).

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3

Let G be an S-group prescheme, flat and of finite presentation over S, \(\mathrm{S}_0\) a closed subprescheme of S defined by a sheaf of ideals of finite type and locally nilpotent, \(\mathrm{T}_0\) a central torus of \(\mathrm{G}_0 = \mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0\). Then there exists one and only one subtorus T of G which lifts \(\mathrm{T}_0\). Moreover T is central.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 2.4

We leave to the reader the task of formulating the analogue of the statements 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, where instead of lifting a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_0\), one is given a torus T over S and one proposes to lift a morphism \[ u_0 : \mathrm{T}_0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}_0 \] (one reduces to the preceding cases by considering the graph of \(u_0\)).
Let us show how corollaries 2.2 and 2.3 are deduced from 2.1.

Proof of corollary 2.2. The uniqueness of T follows from Exp. IX 4.8 b) and from Exp. IX 4.10. To prove the existence of T, we may therefore reduce to the case where S is affine, hence noetherian, and to the case where \(\mathrm{S}_0\) is defined by an ideal of square zero.

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.5

Let G be an S-group prescheme of finite presentation, and H a subgroup scheme of G, of multiplicative type, smooth over S. Then \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H})\) is representable by a subgroup prescheme of G, of finite presentation.

The lemma follows from Exp. VIII 6.5 e), without any smoothness hypothesis on H, when G is separated over S. In the present case, we remark that the assertion to be proved is local for the fpqc topology, which allows us to assume H diagonalizable, hence of the form \(\mathrm{D}_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{M})\). We may also assume S affine, then S noetherian thanks to EGA IV 8. Since H is smooth over S and of finite type, the order \(q\) of the torsion subgroup of M is invertible on S (Exp VIII 2.1 e)). It is then immediate (cf. Exp. IX 4.10) that the subgroups \({}_n\mathrm{H}\), where \(n\) runs over the powers of \(q\), are schematically dense in H (Exp. IX 4.1). But \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) is a completely decomposed covering of S (that is to say, is isomorphic to a finite direct sum of copies of S), so \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}({}_n\mathrm{H}) = \mathrm{Z}_n\) is representable by the intersection of the centralizers in G of the sections of \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) over S. It then suffices to apply the lemma:

blocks.label: 2.5 bis
blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.5 bis

Let S be a noetherian prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite type, H a subgroup of multiplicative type of G, \(\mathrm{H}_i\) an increasing filtered family of subgroups of multiplicative type of G, and suppose that \(\mathrm{Z}_i = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H}_i)\) is representable by a subgroup prescheme of G. Then the family of the \(\mathrm{Z}_i\) is stationary. If moreover the \(\mathrm{H}_i\) are schematically dense in H, we have \(\mathrm{Z}_i = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H})\) for \(i\) large enough.

To see that the family of the \(\mathrm{Z}_i\) is stationary, it suffices to show that the family of underlying sets \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{Z}_i)\) is stationary. Indeed, the stationary value will be a closed part of an open subset U of G, and after replacing G by U, we are reduced to studying a decreasing filtered family of closed subpreschemes of a noetherian prescheme. An easy constructibility argument reduces us to the case where S is integral. We must then show that the family of the \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{Z}_i)\) is stationary over a non-empty open subset of S. Now the generic fibre of G is separated (Exp. VIA 0.3), so, after restricting S if necessary, we may assume G separated over S (EGA IV 8). But then \(\mathrm{Z}_i\) is closed in G (Exp. VIII 6.5 e)).

To establish the last assertion of the lemma, denote by Z the stationary value of the family \(\mathrm{Z}_i\). It is clear that \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H})\) is a subfunctor of Z; let us show that Z centralizes H. Now let E be the subprescheme of \(\mathrm{H} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{Z}\) that is the kernel of the pair of morphisms: \[ \mathrm{H} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{Z} \rightrightarrows \mathrm{G}, \qquad \begin{aligned} (h, c) &\longmapsto c \\ (h, c) &\longmapsto hch^{-1}. \end{aligned} \] The prescheme E dominates \(\mathrm{H}_i \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{Z}\) for every \(i\). On the other hand the \(\mathrm{H}_i\) are flat over S, so (EGA IV 11.10.9) for every point \(s\) of S, the \((\mathrm{H}_i)_s\) are schematically dense in \(\mathrm{H}_s\) and the \(\mathrm{H}_i \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{Z}\) are schematically dense in \(\mathrm{H} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{Z}\). Since \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is separated, \(\mathrm{E}_s\) is closed in \((\mathrm{H} \times_s \mathrm{Z})_s\) and consequently is equal to it. But then E is closed in \(\mathrm{H} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{Z}\), hence is equal to \(\mathrm{H} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{Z}\). That is to say, Z centralizes H.

Let us return to the proof of 2.2. By 2.5, \(\mathrm{Z}_n = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{M}_n)\) is representable, and by 2.5 bis, the decreasing family of subpreschemes \(\mathrm{Z}_n\) is stationary; let Z be the stationary value. The group Z dominates \(\mathrm{T}_0\) and the \(\mathrm{M}_n\). After replacing G by Z, we may therefore assume the \(\mathrm{M}_n\) central. We are then within the conditions of application of theorem 2.1, and there exists a subtorus T of G lifting \(\mathrm{T}_0\). The groups \({}_n\mathrm{T}\) and \(\mathrm{M}_n\) are then two liftings of \({}_n\mathrm{T}_0\), hence are conjugate (Exp. IX 3.2 bis) and consequently coincide, \(\mathrm{M}_n\) being central. The torus T answers the question.

Proof of corollary 2.3. The uniqueness of T follows from Exp. IX 5.1 bis, and the fact that T is central follows from IX 5.6 b). This remark allows us to reduce, by the usual procedure, to the case where S is affine (hence \(\mathrm{S}_0\) defined by a nilpotent ideal of finite type), then to the case where S is noetherian. Restricting S if need be, we may assume that there exists an integer \(q\) invertible on S. Corollary 2.3 is then a consequence of 2.2 and of 1.2.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 2.6

One easily shows that corollary 2.3 remains true if one replaces the torus \(\mathrm{T}_0\) by a smooth and central subgroup of multiplicative type of \(\mathrm{G}_0\).
2. Proof of 2.1
a) Reduction to the case where \(\mathrm{T}_0 = \mathbb{G}_{m\,\mathrm{S}_0}\).

Thanks to 1.1, we may assume that \(\mathrm{M}_n\) is a subgroup of multiplicative type. Using Exp. IX 3.2 bis, we may assume that the family of the \(\mathrm{M}_n\) is coherent (2.2). The centralizer \(\mathrm{Z}_n\) of \(\mathrm{M}_n\) in G is representable (2.5) and the filtered family of the \(\mathrm{Z}_n\) is stationary (2.5 bis). After replacing G by \(\mathrm{Z}_n\) for \(n\) large enough, we may therefore assume \(\mathrm{T}_0\) and the \(\mathrm{M}_n\) central. The uniqueness of the lifting of \(\mathrm{T}_0\) is then guaranteed (Exp. IX 5.1 bis).

Proceeding as in the proof of 1.1, we may assume that there exist an S-torus T and an \(\mathrm{S}_0\)-isomorphism: \[ u_0 : \mathrm{T} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{S}_0 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{T}_0 \] and it is equivalent to lift \(u_0\) or to lift \(\mathrm{T}_0\). In view of the uniqueness, it suffices to prove the existence of a lifting of \(u_0\) after making a faithfully flat affine extension \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) of finite type (fpqc descent), which allows us to assume \(\mathrm{T} = \mathbb{G}^r_{m\,\mathrm{S}}\) (Exp. X 4.5). If the restriction of \(u_0\) to each factor \(\mathbb{G}\) lifts to an S-morphism, necessarily central, we immediately deduce from this a lifting of \(u_0\). In short, we may assume \(\mathrm{T}_0 = \mathbb{G}_{m,\,\mathrm{S}_0}\).
b) Definition of the obstruction to the existence of a lifting of \(\mathrm{T}_0\).

To prove 2.1, it suffices by 1.1 to find a subscheme of G, flat over S, which lifts \(\mathrm{T}_0\). We are going to see that one can define the obstruction to the existence of such a lifting as an element of a certain \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(\ ,\ )\) of sheaves of modules.

Let U be an open subset of G such that \(\mathrm{T}_0\) is closed in U, and denote again by U (resp. \(\mathrm{U}_0\)) the open subscheme of G (resp. \(\mathrm{G}_0\)) having U as underlying space. The sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0}\), considered as a sheaf on U, is thus a quotient of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{U}_0}\). Let \(h\) be the canonical epimorphism: \[ h : \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{U}_0} \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0}. \]

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.7

The canonical map: \[ h' = \operatorname{id}_\mathrm{J} \otimes h : \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{U}_0} \longrightarrow \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0} \] factors (obviously in a unique way) as \(i \circ j_\mathrm{U}\) where \(j_\mathrm{U}\) is the canonical epimorphism: \[ \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{U}_0} \longrightarrow \mathrm{J}\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{U} \simeq \mathrm{J}\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{U}_0}. \]

We must show that \(h'(\mathrm{K}) = 0\), where K is the kernel of \(j_\mathrm{U}\). Now for every integer \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\), we have an epimorphism: \[ h_n : \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0} \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_{{}_n\mathrm{T}_0} \] and since \({}_n\mathrm{T}_0\) lifts to a scheme \(\mathrm{M}_n\) flat over S, the canonical morphism: \[ j_n : \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{{}_n\mathrm{T}_0} \longrightarrow \mathrm{J}\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{M}_n} \] is an isomorphism. The commutative diagram below: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{K} \ar[r] & \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{U}_0} \ar[r]^-{j_\mathrm{U}} \ar[d]_{h'} & \mathrm{J}\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{U} \subset \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{U} \\ & \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0} \ar[ur]_i \ar[d]_{h'_n} & \\ & \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{{}_n\mathrm{T}_0} \ar[r]^-{\sim}_-{j_n} & \mathrm{J}\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{M}_n} \subset \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{M}_n} } \] proves that \(h'(\mathrm{K})\) is contained in \(\operatorname{Ker} h'_n\) for every \(n\), hence is contained in \(\bigcap_n \operatorname{Ker} h'_n\), and it suffices to show that the latter is zero. Now the sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0}\) equals the sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}_0}[\mathbb{Z}]\), the algebra of the group \(\mathbb{Z}\) with coefficients in \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}_0}\), while \(\mathscr{O}_{{}_n\mathrm{T}_0}\) is the quotient algebra \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}_0}[\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}]\).

Let \(a = \sum_{m \in \mathbb{Z}} a_m \otimes m\) be an element of \(\mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0}\). The \(a_m\) are thus sections of J, almost all zero. Take \(n\) large enough so that the indices \(m\) for which \(a_m\) is non-zero have distinct images in \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\). Then if \(a \in \operatorname{Ker} h'_n\), we necessarily have \(a = 0\). This proves that \(\bigcap_n \operatorname{Ker} h'_n = 0\), and proves 2.7.

Now let \(\mathrm{K}_0\) be the kernel of \(h\); consider the following diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ & & & 0 \ar[d] & \\ & \mathrm{J}\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{U} \ar[d]_l & & \mathrm{K}_0 \ar[d] & \\ 0 \ar[r] & \mathrm{J}\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{U}_0} \ar[r]^-i \ar[d] & \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{U} \ar[r] & \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{U}_0} \ar[r] \ar[d]^h & 0 \\ & \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0} & & \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0} \ar[d] & \\ & & & 0 & } \] The sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{U}\) defines an element \(\Phi\) of the group \(\operatorname{Ext}^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{U}}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{U}_0}, \mathrm{J}\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{U}_0})\). Let \(\Psi\) be the element of \(\operatorname{Ext}^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{U}}(\mathrm{K}_0, \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0})\) deduced from \(\Phi\) by the bifunctoriality of \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(\ ,\ )\) thanks to the morphisms \(\mathrm{K}_0 \to \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{U}_0}\) and \(i : \mathrm{J}\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{U}_0} \to \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0}\). It follows from Exp. III 4.1 and from the infinitesimal criterion of flatness (see Exp. III 4.3) that there exists a subscheme of U, flat over S, which lifts \(\mathrm{T}_0\), if and only if \(\Psi\) is zero.

But note that \(\mathrm{T}_0\) is affine; it therefore suffices (Exp. III 4.5 and 4.6) to show that there exists, locally on U, a subscheme flat over S which lifts \(\mathrm{T}_0\). In short, it suffices to show that the image of \(\Psi\) in the sheaf: \[ \mathscr{E} = \mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{U}}(\mathrm{K}_0, \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0}) \] is zero. We shall again denote by \(\Psi\) this element of \(\Gamma(\mathrm{U}, \mathscr{E})\).
c) Reduction to the case of S local artinian, with algebraically closed residue field.

Since \(\mathrm{K}_0\) and \(\mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0}\) are coherent sheaves, so is the sheaf \(\mathscr{E}\); moreover \(\mathscr{E}\) has its support in \(\mathrm{T}_0\), since this is the case for \(\mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0}\). To show that the section \(f\) of \(\mathscr{E}\) is zero, it suffices to see that for every point \(u\) of \(\mathrm{T}_0\), the image of \(f\) in the fibre of \(\mathscr{E}\) at the point \(u\) is zero. But the formation of the \(\mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^i(\ ,\ )\) of coherent sheaves commutes with flat extensions of the base2, so that we are reduced to proving the existence of a lifting of \(\mathrm{T}_0 \cap \operatorname{Spec} \mathscr{O}_u\) which is flat over S. Let \(s\) be the projection of \(u\) onto S; we may then replace S by \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathscr{O}_s\) and G by \(\mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \operatorname{Spec} \mathscr{O}_s\). After making, again, a faithfully flat extension, we may assume that \(\mathscr{O}_s\) has an algebraically closed residue field (EGA \(0_{\text{IV}}\) 10.3.1).

Let \(\mathfrak{m}\) be the maximal ideal of \(\mathscr{O}_s\) and \(\mathrm{S}_n = \operatorname{Spec} \mathscr{O}_s/\mathfrak{m}^n\). Suppose we have shown that for every \(n > 0\), the obstruction to lifting \(\mathrm{T}_0 \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_n = (\mathrm{T}_0)_n\) to a subscheme of \(\mathrm{G}_n = \mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_n\), flat over \(\mathrm{S}_n\), is zero, and let \(\mathrm{T}_n\) be the unique lifting of \((\mathrm{T}_0)_n\), flat over \(\mathrm{S}_n\), which is a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_n\). It is clear that if \(n > n'\), we have: \[ (\mathrm{T}_n) \underset{\mathrm{S}_{n'}}{\times} \mathrm{S}_{n'} = \mathrm{T}_{n'}. \]

If then \(u\) is a point of \(\mathrm{T}_0\) projecting onto \(s\), it follows from the lemma below, applied to the coherent system of liftings \((\mathrm{T}_n \cap \operatorname{Spec} \mathscr{O}_u)\) of \((\mathrm{T}_0)_n \cap \operatorname{Spec} \mathscr{O}_u\), that there does exist a lifting of \(\mathrm{T}_0 \cap \operatorname{Spec} \mathscr{O}_u\) flat over \(\mathscr{O}_s\). We are therefore reduced to proving that \(\Psi\) is zero when \(\mathrm{S} = \mathrm{S}_n\) is the spectrum of a local artinian ring with algebraically closed residue field, and to proving the:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.8

Let \(\mathrm{A} \to \mathrm{B}\) be a local homomorphism of noetherian local rings, \(\mathfrak{m}\) the maximal ideal of A, J an ideal of square zero of A, M a B-module of finite type, \(\mathrm{A}_0 = \mathrm{A}/\mathrm{J}\), \(\mathrm{B}_0 = \mathrm{B}/\mathrm{JB}\), \(\mathrm{M}_0 = \mathrm{M}/\mathrm{JM}\), \(\mathrm{N}_0\) a \(\mathrm{B}_0\)-module quotient of \(\mathrm{M}_0\) which is flat over \(\mathrm{A}_0\). For every integer \(n > 0\) denote by \(\mathrm{A}_n\), \(\mathrm{A}_{0,n}\), etc. the objects obtained by the base extension \(\mathrm{A} \to \mathrm{A}/\mathfrak{m}^n = \mathrm{A}_n\), and let \(\overline{\mathrm{J}}_n\) be the image of J in \(\mathrm{A}_n\). For every integer \(n > 0\), let \(\mathrm{N}_n\) be a B-module quotient of \(\mathrm{M}_n\), flat over \(\mathrm{A}_n\), which lifts \(\mathrm{N}_{0,n}\), and suppose that for \(n \geqslant n'\), \(\mathrm{N}_{n'}\) is obtained from \(\mathrm{N}_n\) by the base extension \(\mathrm{A}_n \to \mathrm{A}_{n'}\). Then there exists a B-module N, quotient of M, flat over A, which lifts \(\mathrm{N}_0\).

Proof of 2.8. Denote by \(\mathrm{P}_0\) the kernel of the epimorphism \(\mathrm{M}_0 \to \mathrm{N}_0\). For every \(n > 0\), we thus have the following commutative diagram, in which the horizontal rows are exact: \[ \xymatrix@C=1.4em@R=1.6em{ & & & 0 \ar[d] & & \\ & & & \mathrm{P}_{0,n} & & \\ (\ast)_n & 0 \ar[r] & \overline{\mathrm{J}}_n\mathrm{M}_n \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{M}_n \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{M}_{0,n} \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ & & \overline{\mathrm{J}}_n \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_{0,n}} \mathrm{M}_{0,n} \ar[u] \ar[d] & & & \\ & 0 \ar[r] & \overline{\mathrm{J}}_n \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_{0,n}} \mathrm{N}_{0,n} \ar[r] & \mathrm{N}_n \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{N}_{0,n} \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ & & & 0 & 0 & } \] Moreover, by hypothesis, the diagram \((\ast)_{n+1}\) reduces to \((\ast)_n\) modulo \(\mathfrak{m}^n\).

The Artin-Rees lemma (Bourbaki, Alg. comm., Chap. 3 §3 cor. 1) shows that the filtration defined on JM (resp. \(\mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_0} \mathrm{M}_0\) and \(\mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_0} \mathrm{N}_0\)) by the kernels of the morphisms: \[ \mathrm{JM} \longrightarrow \overline{\mathrm{J}}_n\mathrm{M}_n, \quad (\text{resp. } \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_0} \mathrm{M}_0 \longrightarrow \overline{\mathrm{J}}_n \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_{0,n}} \mathrm{M}_{0,n} \text{ and } \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_0} \mathrm{N}_0 \longrightarrow \overline{\mathrm{J}}_n \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_{0,n}} \mathrm{N}_{0,n}) \] is \(\mathfrak{m}\mathrm{B}\)-good, so that, passing to the projective limit over the diagrams \((\ast)_n\) and denoting by \(\widehat{\mathrm{Q}}\) the separated completion of a B-module Q for the \(\mathfrak{m}\mathrm{B}\)-adic topology, we obtain the following commutative diagram in which the two horizontal rows are still exact: \[ \xymatrix@C=1.4em@R=1.6em{ & & & 0 \ar[d] & & \\ & & & \widehat{\mathrm{P}}_0 & & \\ \widehat{(\ast)} & 0 \ar[r] & \widehat{\mathrm{JM}} \ar[r] \ar[d] & \widehat{\mathrm{M}} \ar[r] \ar[d] & \widehat{\mathrm{M}}_0 \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ & & \mathrm{J}\widehat{\otimes}_{\mathrm{A}_0}\mathrm{M}_0 \ar[u] \ar[d] & & & \\ & 0 \ar[r] & \mathrm{J}\widehat{\otimes}_{\mathrm{A}_0}\mathrm{N}_0 \ar[r] & \varprojlim_n \mathrm{N}_n \ar[r] \ar[d] & \widehat{\mathrm{N}}_0 \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ & & & 0 & 0 & } \] Now \((\mathrm{B}, \mathfrak{m}\mathrm{B})\) is a Zariski ring and \(\mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_0} \mathrm{N}_0\) is of finite type over B, hence is separated for the \(\mathfrak{m}\mathrm{B}\)-adic topology. The diagram \(\widehat{(\ast)}\) then shows that the morphism: \[ \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_0} \mathrm{M}_0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_0} \mathrm{N}_0 \] deduced from the epimorphism \(\mathrm{M}_0 \to \mathrm{N}_0\) factors through JM: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_0} \mathrm{M}_0 \ar[r]^-{\text{can.}} \ar[dr] & \mathrm{JM} \ar[d] \\ & \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_0} \mathrm{N}_0 } \]

Under these conditions, it follows from Exp. III 4.1 and Exp. III 4.3 that there exists a B-module quotient N of M, flat over A, which lifts \(\mathrm{N}_0\), if and only if a certain element \(g\) of \(\mathrm{E} = \operatorname{Ext}^1_\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{P}_0, \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_0} \mathrm{N}_0)\) is zero. More precisely, the exact sequence: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{JM} \longrightarrow \mathrm{M} \longrightarrow \mathrm{M}_0 \longrightarrow 0 \] defines an element \(f\) of F, where F is the B-module \(\operatorname{Ext}^1_\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{M}_0, \mathrm{JM})\), and \(g\) is the image of \(f\) under the natural morphism \(\mathrm{F} \to \mathrm{E}\) coming, by bifunctoriality, from the morphisms: \[ \mathrm{P}_0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{M}_0 \qquad \text{and} \qquad \mathrm{JM} \longrightarrow \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{A}_0} \mathrm{N}_0. \] It follows from the diagram \(\widehat{(\ast)}\) and from Exp. III 4.1 that the image of \(g\) in \(\widehat{\mathrm{E}}\), canonically identified with \(\operatorname{Ext}^1_{\widehat{\mathrm{B}}}(\widehat{\mathrm{P}}_0, \mathrm{J}\widehat{\otimes}_{\mathrm{A}_0}\mathrm{N}_0)\), is zero. But E is of finite type over B, hence is separated for the \(\mathfrak{m}\mathrm{B}\)-adic topology, and consequently \(g\) is indeed equal to 0, which completes the proof of 2.8.

d) Study of \(\mathscr{E}\). We therefore suppose that S is the spectrum of a local artinian ring A, with algebraically closed residue field \(k\). Let \(\mathrm{A}_0\) be the ring of \(\mathrm{S}_0\), \(\mathfrak{m}_0\) the maximal ideal of \(\mathrm{A}_0\). Since \(\mathrm{S}_0\) is artinian, \(\mathrm{T}_0\) is closed in G (Exp. VIB 1.4.2); we may therefore take the open subset U equal to G, so that: \[ \mathscr{E} = \mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{K}_0, \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0}). \]

Let \(\mathrm{B}_0\) be the ring of the affine \(\mathrm{S}_0\)-scheme \(\mathrm{T}_0 = \mathbb{G}_{m,\,\mathrm{S}_0}\) and \(\overline{\mathrm{B}}_0\) the ring of the special fibre \(\mathrm{T}_0 \times_{\mathrm{S}_0} \operatorname{Spec}(k) = \mathbb{G}_{m,\,k}\) of \(\mathrm{T}_0\). The sheaf \(\mathscr{E}\) is a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0}\)-module, hence is defined by a \(\mathrm{B}_0\)-module of finite type which we shall denote by E.

Consider the graded module associated to E and to the \(\mathfrak{m}_0\mathrm{B}_0\)-adic filtration: \[ \mathrm{E}_r = \mathfrak{m}_0^r\mathrm{E}/\mathfrak{m}_0^{r+1}\mathrm{E}. \] Each \(\mathrm{E}_r\) is thus a \(\overline{\mathrm{B}}_0\)-module of finite type, and \(\mathrm{E}_r = 0\) for \(r\) large enough, \(\mathrm{S}_0\) being artinian.

Now let \(g\) be a section of G over S which over \(\mathrm{S}_0\) induces a section \(g_0\) of \(\mathrm{T}_0\). Translation (on the left, to fix ideas) by the element \(g\) defines an "automorphism of the situation" from the point of view of the obstruction problem under consideration. In particular, to \(g\) corresponds an \(\mathrm{S}_0\)-automorphism of the sheaf \(\mathscr{E}\) which leaves the obstruction \(\Psi\) fixed. More precisely, \(g\) defines a semi-linear automorphism of the \(\mathrm{B}_0\)-module E (relative to the \(\mathrm{A}_0\)-automorphism of \(\mathrm{B}_0\) defined by the translation by \(g_0\) in the group \(\mathrm{T}_0\)). By reduction modulo \(\mathfrak{m}_0^{r+1}\), \(g\) thus defines a semi-linear automorphism of \(\mathrm{E}_r\) (relative to the \(k\)-automorphism of \(\overline{\mathrm{B}}_0\) defined by the translation by \(g_0 \times_{\mathrm{S}_0} \operatorname{Spec}(k)\) in \(\mathrm{T}_0 \times_{\mathrm{S}_0} \operatorname{Spec}(k)\)).

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.9

For every integer \(r \geqslant 0\), \(\mathrm{E}_r\) is a locally free \(\mathrm{B}_0\)-module.

Let \(x\) be a point of \(\mathrm{T}_0\), \(\kappa(x)\) its residue field, \((\mathrm{E}_r)_x\) "the fibre" of \(\mathrm{E}_r\) at \(x\), equal to \(\mathrm{E}_r \otimes_{\overline{\mathrm{B}}_0} \kappa(x)\), \(\ell(x)\) the rank of \((\mathrm{E}_r)_x\) over \(\kappa(x)\), \(\ell\) the maximum value of \(\ell(x)\) as \(x\) runs over the points of \(\mathrm{T}_0\). Denote by L the largest closed subscheme of \(\operatorname{Spec} \overline{\mathrm{B}}_0 = \mathbb{G}_{m,\,k}\) over which \(\mathrm{E}_r\) is locally free of rank \(\ell\) (TDTE IV Lemma 3.6). Let \(\beta\) be a point of \(\mathrm{L}(k)\) (one exists, L being of finite type over \(k\) algebraically closed) and let \(\alpha\) be a point of \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\,k}(k)\) of order equal to a power \(n\) of \(q\). The point \(\alpha\) is thus rational over \(k\), and since, by hypothesis, \({}_n\mathrm{T}_0\) lifts to a subscheme \(\mathrm{M}_n\) étale over S, there exists a section \(a\) of G over S which lifts \(\alpha\) and which, over \(\mathrm{S}_0\), is a section of \(\mathrm{T}_0\). The remarks made above then show that the fibres of \(\mathrm{E}_r\) are \(k\)-isomorphic at the points \(\beta\) and \(\alpha\beta\) of \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\,k}(k)\). But the points of order a power of \(q\) are dense in \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\,k}\), and the same holds for their translates by \(\beta\). Since L is closed in \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\,k}\) and \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\,k}\) is reduced, L is equal to \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\,k}\) and \(\mathrm{E}_r\) is locally free over \(\overline{\mathrm{B}}_0\), of rank \(\ell\).
e) End of the proof of theorem 2.1.

Let \(\mathrm{K}_0(n)\) be the sheaf of ideals of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G}_0}\) defining the closed subscheme \({}_n\mathrm{T}_0\). The sheaf \(\mathrm{K}_0\)3 is thus a subsheaf of \(\mathrm{K}_0(n)\). Let us set, for simplicity: \[ \mathrm{R} = \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \mathrm{R}(n) = \mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathscr{O}_{{}_n\mathrm{T}_0}. \] The sheaf \(\mathrm{R}(n)\) is thus a quotient of R, and we have the diagram of maps: \[ \xymatrix{ & \mathrm{K}_0 \ar[d] & & \\ & \mathrm{K}_0(n) \ar[d] & & \\ 0 \ar[r] & \mathrm{J}\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G} \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G} \ar[r] & \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G}_0} \ar[r] & 0 \\ & \mathrm{R} \ar[d] & & \\ & \mathrm{R}(n) & & } \] Using then the bifunctoriality of \(\mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\ ,\ )\), we obtain the following commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix@C=1.4em{ \mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G}_0}, \mathrm{J}\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}) \ar[dr] & & \\ & \mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{K}_0(n), \mathrm{R}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{K}_0, \mathrm{R}) = \mathscr{E} \ar[d] \\ & \mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{K}_0(n), \mathrm{R}(n)) \ar[r] & \mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{K}_0, \mathrm{R}(n)) } \] Denote again by \(\Phi\) the element of \(\mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G}_0}, \mathrm{J}\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G})\) defined by the exact sequence: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{J}\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G}_0} \longrightarrow 0 \] so that \(\Psi\) is the image of \(\Phi\) in \(\mathscr{E}\). Since \({}_n\mathrm{T}_0\) lifts to a subscheme \(\mathrm{M}_n\) of G, flat over S, the image of \(\Phi\) in the sheaf \(\mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{K}_0(n), \mathrm{R}(n))\) is zero (Exp. III 4.1); a fortiori the image of \(\Phi\) in \(\mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{K}_0, \mathrm{R}(n))\), which is also the image of \(\Psi\), is zero.

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.10

The canonical morphism: \[ \mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{K}_0, \mathrm{R}) \otimes_{\mathrm{B}_0} \mathscr{O}_{{}_n\mathrm{T}_0} \longrightarrow \mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{K}_0, \mathrm{R}(n)) \] is injective for every integer \(n\).

Indeed, the affine scheme \(\mathrm{T}_0 = \mathbb{G}_{m,\,\mathrm{S}_0}\) has ring: \[ \mathrm{B}_0 = \mathrm{A}_0[\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{T}^{-1}]. \] The subscheme \({}_n\mathrm{T}_0\) is defined by the vanishing of the following section of \(\mathrm{B}_0\): \[ h(n) = \mathrm{T}^n - 1, \] which is regular (EGA \(0_{\text{IV}}\) 15.2.2) and remains regular after any base change \(\mathrm{S}'_0 \to \mathrm{S}_0\). We therefore have an exact sequence of sheaves: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0} \xrightarrow{\ h(n)\ } \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{T}_0} \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_{{}_n\mathrm{T}_0} \longrightarrow 0. \] Since \({}_n\mathrm{T}_0\) is flat over S, we obtain, by tensoring with J over \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}_0}\), the exact sequence: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{R} \xrightarrow{\ h(n)\ } \mathrm{R} \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}(n) \longrightarrow 0. \] Then the exact sequence of the \(\mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt\): \[ \cdots \longrightarrow \mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{K}_0, \mathrm{R}) \xrightarrow{\ h(n)\ } \mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{K}_0, \mathrm{R}) \longrightarrow \mathscr{E}\mkern-2mu xt^1_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{K}_0, \mathrm{R}(n)), \] which completes the proof of the lemma.

The above shows that for every integer \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\), the image of \(\Psi\) in \(\mathrm{E}/h(n)\mathrm{E}\) is zero. To show that \(\Psi\) is zero, it suffices to see that if \(\Psi \in \mathfrak{m}_0^r\mathrm{E}\), then \(\Psi \in \mathfrak{m}_0^{r+1}\mathrm{E}\). Let \(\overline{\Psi}\) be the image of \(\Psi\) in \(\mathrm{E}_r\). There exists an element \(\Psi(n)\) of E such that we have: \[ \Psi = \Psi(n)h(n) \qquad (n \text{ equal to a power of } q). \] We have remarked that the image \(\overline{h(n)}\) of \(h(n)\) in \(\overline{\mathrm{B}}_0\) is still \(\overline{\mathrm{B}}_0\)-regular. Since \(\mathrm{E}_s\) is locally free over \(\overline{\mathrm{B}}_0\) for every \(s\) (2.9), multiplication by \(\overline{h(n)}\) in \(\mathrm{E}_s\) is injective. One immediately deduces that \(\Psi(n)\) is in \(\mathfrak{m}_0^r\mathrm{E}\). Let \(\overline{\Psi(n)}\) be its image in \(\mathrm{E}_r\), so that we have the relation: \[ \overline{\Psi} = \overline{h(n)}\,\overline{\Psi(n)} \qquad (n \text{ equal to a power of } q). \] This shows that the set F of points of \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\,k}\) at which \(\overline{\Psi}\) takes the value 0 contains the dense set of points of order a power of \(q\). Moreover F is closed (since \(\mathrm{E}_r\) is locally free over \(\overline{\mathrm{B}}_0\)) and \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\,k}\) is reduced, so \(\overline{\Psi}\) is zero.
This completes the proof of theorem 2.1.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: The idea of approximating a torus by its finite subgroups appears in Grothendieck's proof of the connectedness of centralizers of tori, see § 4.6 of BIBLE.
N.D.E.: introduced after lemma 2.7.

pages.title: 3. Characterization of a subtorus by its underlying set

blocks.html: 3. Characterization of a subtorus by its underlying set
1. Statement of the theorem

blocks.title: Notations
blocks.html:
Notations

If X is a prescheme, \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{X})\) denotes the set underlying X. If X and \(\mathrm{S}'\) are two S-preschemas, \(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{S}'} = \mathrm{X} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}'\), \(u : \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{S}'} \to \mathrm{X}\) the canonical morphism, E a subset of \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{X})\), we denote by \(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) (or \(\mathrm{E} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}'\)) the subset of \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{S}'})\) equal to \(u^{-1}(\mathrm{E})\).

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.1

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite type, \(q\) an integer \(> 0\) invertible on S, E a subset of \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{G})\). Consider the following assertions concerning E:

(i) The set E is the set underlying a subtorus T of G.

(ii) a) For every point \(s\) of S, there exists a subtorus \(\mathrm{T}_s\) of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) such that \(\mathrm{E} \cap \operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{G}_s) = \mathrm{E}_s\) is the set underlying \(\mathrm{T}_s\).

b) The integer \(n\) running over the powers of \(q\), there exists a coherent family (cf. 2.2) \(\mathrm{M}_n\) of subgroup schemes of G, of multiplicative type, such that for every point \(s\) of S, we have: \[ (\mathrm{M}_n)_s = {}_n\mathrm{T}_s. \]

(iii) a) as (ii) a).

b) The set E is locally closed in \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{G})\) and the dimension of the fibres of E, relative to S, is locally constant.

(iv) a) as (ii) a).

b) For every S-scheme \(\mathrm{S}'\) which is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field, \(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is the set underlying a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\).

Then we have the following implications:

A) (i) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (iv).

B) If G is separated over S, we have (iii) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (iv), and moreover E is closed.

C) The conditions (i), (ii), (iii) (and also (iv), if G is separated over S) are equivalent in the two following cases:

1st case: a) The prescheme S is reduced or else G is flat over S, and
b) For every point \(s\) of S, \(\mathrm{T}_s\) is a central torus of \(\mathrm{G}_s\).
2nd case: S is normal.

Moreover, in the two cases above, the torus T with underlying space E is unique.

blocks.title: Remarques
blocks.html:
Remarks 3.2

a) When S is reduced, it is unnecessary in (ii) to assume the family \(\mathrm{M}_n\) coherent, this condition being implied by the other hypotheses. Indeed, if the integer \(m\) divides \(n\), the subgroups \({}_m\mathrm{M}_n\) and \(\mathrm{M}_m\) are étale over S, hence reduced, and have the same underlying space, so they coincide.

b) If S is no longer assumed normal, it is no longer true in general that (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i), even when S is reduced, geometrically unibranch and G is a group scheme smooth over S. Indeed, consider the Borel subgroup of \(\mathrm{SL}_{2,\,\mathrm{S}}\) formed of the matrices of the form: \[ \begin{pmatrix} t & u \\ 0 & t^{-1} \end{pmatrix}, \] where S is the affine curve over a field \(k\) with ring: \[ k[x, y]/(y^2 - x^3). \] Consider then the set E obtained in the following way: Above the "cusp point of S" \((x = y = 0)\) we take the diagonal torus \((u = 0)\). Above the complementary open set \((x \neq 0)\) we take the torus deduced from the diagonal torus by conjugation by the element: \[ \begin{pmatrix} 1 & y/x \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}. \] The set E obtained satisfies (iii) a); on the other hand it is closed in G, and the reduced subscheme having E as underlying set has equations: \[ \begin{cases} xu + y(t - t^{-1}) = 0 \\ u^2 - x(t - t^{-1})^2 = 0. \end{cases} \] It is therefore not a subtorus of G, since the fibre above the cusp point is not reduced.
Plan of the proof of 3.1.

In a first part, we shall establish the following "easy" implications: \[ \xymatrix{ & (\mathrm{ii}) \ar[dr] & \\ (\mathrm{i}) \ar[ur] \ar[dr] & & (\mathrm{iv}) \\ & (\mathrm{iii}) \ar[ur] & } \] and (iv) \(\Rightarrow\) [(iii) and E closed] if G is separated over S.
The proof of the more delicate implications will be carried out in three steps:
I) Reduction of the implication (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i) (under the hypotheses of C), 1st case), to the case where S is normal.
II) (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii) if S is normal.
III) (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i).
2. Proof of the "easy" assertions contained in 3.1
(i) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii) and (iii) is trivial.
(iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (iv) After replacing S by \(\mathrm{S}'\), we may assume that S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring. Let \(t\) be the generic point of S and \(s\) the closed point.

Since E is locally closed, there exists a subprescheme of G which is reduced and whose underlying space is E; we shall denote it by \(\overline{\mathrm{E}}\). The generic fibre \(\overline{\mathrm{E}}_t\) of \(\overline{\mathrm{E}}\) is thus equal to the subtorus \(\mathrm{T}_t\) of rank \(r\) of \(\mathrm{G}_t\) (by (iii) a)). Let \(\mathrm{E}'\) be the schematic closure of \(\overline{\mathrm{E}}_t\) in G. The prescheme \(\mathrm{E}'\) is irreducible and its closed fibre \(\mathrm{E}'_s\) is not empty (it contains the unit section of \(\mathrm{G}_s\)), so \(\mathrm{E}'_s\) is of dimension \(r\) (EGA IV 14.3.10). But \(\mathrm{E}'_s\) is then a closed subscheme of \(\overline{\mathrm{E}}_s\), and the latter is of dimension \(r\) (by (iii) b)) and irreducible (since it has the same underlying space as \(\mathrm{T}_s\)), so \(\mathrm{E}'_s\) has the same underlying space as \(\overline{\mathrm{E}}_s\) and consequently \(\mathrm{E}' = \overline{\mathrm{E}}\), which proves that \(\overline{\mathrm{E}}\) is flat over S.

Now let \(\mathrm{E}''\) be the schematic closure of \(\overline{\mathrm{E}}_t\) in G. Then \(\mathrm{E}''\) is a subgroup prescheme of G flat over S (Exp. VIII 7.1) which dominates \(\mathrm{E}'\), hence \(\overline{\mathrm{E}}\). The closed fibre \(\mathrm{E}''_s\) is an algebraic subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\), of dimension \(r\) (loc. cit.). Since \(\mathrm{T}_s\) is an irreducible closed subset of \(\mathrm{G}_s\), of dimension \(r\), \(\overline{\mathrm{E}}_s\) has the same underlying set as the identity component \((\mathrm{E}''_s)^0\) of \(\mathrm{E}''_s\). Let \((\mathrm{E}'')^0\) be the "identity component" of \(\mathrm{E}''\), i.e. the open subgroup of \(\mathrm{E}''\) complementary to the union of the irreducible components of \(\mathrm{E}''_s\) not containing the origin. We thus have: \[ \mathrm{E} = \operatorname{ens}[(\mathrm{E}'')^0]. \] Since \(\overline{\mathrm{E}}\) and \(\mathrm{E}''\) are reduced, we even have \(\overline{\mathrm{E}} = (\mathrm{E}'')^0\). Finally, \(\overline{\mathrm{E}}\) is a subgroup prescheme of G, flat and of finite type over S, with connected fibres, hence separated (Exp. VIB 5.2), whose generic fibre is a torus \(\mathrm{T}_t\), and whose reduced closed fibre is a torus \(\mathrm{T}_s\); but then \(\overline{\mathrm{E}}\) is a torus (Exp. X 8.8).

(ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (iv). We may again assume that S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, and we keep the notation introduced above. The schematic closure in G of \(\mathrm{T}_t\) is a subgroup prescheme \(\overline{\mathrm{T}}\) of G, flat over S, which dominates \(\mathrm{M}_n\) for every integer \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\). Consequently the closed fibre of \(\overline{\mathrm{T}}\) is a closed subscheme of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) which dominates \((\mathrm{M}_n)_s\) for every \(n\), hence dominates \(\mathrm{T}_s\). For dimension reasons, the "identity component" \(\overline{\mathrm{T}}^0\) of \(\overline{\mathrm{T}}\) admits E as underlying set, and one concludes as above that \(\overline{\mathrm{T}}^0\) is a subtorus of G.
(iv) \(\Rightarrow\) [(iii) and E closed, if G is separated over S. Let us show that E is closed.]4 Let us first prove the lemma:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.3

If the conditions stated in 3.1 iv) are satisfied, E is a locally constructible part of \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{G})\).

By the usual method, we are reduced to studying the case where S is noetherian, integral, with generic point \(\eta\). After restricting S if necessary, we may assume (Exp. VIB 10.10) that there exists a subgroup scheme of G, say H, flat over S, with connected fibres, whose generic fibre \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) equals \(\mathrm{T}_\eta\). To prove 3.3, it then suffices to show that \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{H}) = \mathrm{E}\). Now if \(s\) is a point of S, there exists, by EGA II 7.1.9, an S-scheme \(\mathrm{S}'\), spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field, whose generic point \(t'\) projects onto \(\eta\) and whose closed point \(s'\) projects onto \(s\). By (iv) b), there exists a subtorus \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) having \(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) as underlying space. The two subgroup preschemes \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) and \(\mathrm{H}'_{\mathrm{S}'} = \mathrm{H} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}'\) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) are flat over \(\mathrm{S}'\), with connected fibres, and have the same generic fibre \(\mathrm{T}_{t'}\), so they coincide with the identity component of the schematic closure of \(\mathrm{T}_{t'}\) in \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\). Consequently \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{H}_s) = \mathrm{E}_s\), hence \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{H}) = \mathrm{E}\), which proves the lemma.

This being so, knowing that E is locally constructible, to see that E is closed it suffices to show that every specialization in G of a point of E is a point of E. By the usual technique, we are reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field. But then the subtorus of G with underlying space E ((iv) b)) is closed in G, since G is separated (Exp. VIII 7.12).
3. Continuation of the proof of 3.1
I) Reduction of (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i) (C, 1st case) to the case where S is normal.
a) Reduction to the case of S affine and reduced.

We therefore assume that for every point \(s\) of S, \(\mathrm{E}_s\) is the space underlying a central subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_s\). The uniqueness of T then follows from Exp. IX 5.1 bis, and moreover T will necessarily be central (loc. cit.). In view of the uniqueness, to prove the existence of T we may assume S noetherian, affine with ring A. If S is not reduced, then by hypothesis G is flat over S. By 2.3, it then suffices to solve the problem for \(\mathrm{S}_{\text{réd}}\) and \(\mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_{\text{réd}}\). We may therefore further assume S reduced.
b) Reduction to the case where the ring A is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\).
Let us first prove two lemmas:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.4

Let \(k\) be a field, G a \(k\)-algebraic group, E a subset of G, \(k'\) an extension of \(k\), \(\mathrm{T}_{k'}\) a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_{k'}\) having \(\mathrm{E}_{k'}\) as underlying space. Then if \(\mathrm{T}_{k'}\) is central or if \(k\) is perfect, E is the set underlying a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_k\).

Indeed, by fpqc descent, it suffices to show that the two inverse images of \(\mathrm{T}_{k'}\) in \(k'' = k' \otimes_k k'\) coincide. Now they have the same underlying space, namely the inverse image of E. If \(\mathrm{T}_{k'}\) is central, the lemma is then a consequence of Exp. IX 5.1 bis. If \(k\) is perfect, \(k''\) is reduced, and the two inverse images of \(\mathrm{T}_{k'}\), being smooth over \(k''\), are reduced, hence coincide.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 3.5

It follows from the preceding lemma that in the statement of 3.1 (iv), property (iv) a) is a consequence of (iv) b) in the two following cases:

(1) One assumes that the residue fields of the points of S are perfect.

(2) For every \(\mathrm{S}'\) as in (iv) b), one assumes that the torus with underlying space equal to \(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is central in \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\).

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.6

Let S be a prescheme that is the projective limit of affine schemes \(\mathrm{S}_i\) (cf. EGA IV 8), H an S-group scheme of multiplicative type and of finite type. Then there exist an index \(i\), an \(\mathrm{S}_i\)-group scheme \(\mathrm{H}_i\) of multiplicative type and of finite type, and an S-isomorphism: \[ \mathrm{H}_i \underset{\mathrm{S}_i}{\times} \mathrm{S} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{H}. \] If moreover H is isotrivial, one may assume \(\mathrm{H}_i\) isotrivial.

Since H is of finite type over S, H is even of finite presentation over S (Exp. IX 2.1 b)), so there exist an index \(\ell\) and an \(\mathrm{S}_\ell\)-group scheme \(\mathrm{H}_\ell\) such that \(\mathrm{H}_\ell \times_{\mathrm{S}_\ell} \mathrm{S}\) is isomorphic to H (Exp. VIB 10). Setting \(\mathrm{H}_i = \mathrm{H}_\ell \times_{\mathrm{S}_\ell} \mathrm{S}_i\), we thus have \(\mathrm{H} \cong \mathrm{H}_i \times_{\mathrm{S}_i} \mathrm{S}\) for every \(i \geqslant \ell\).

Since H is of finite type over S, H is quasi-isotrivial (Exp. X 4.5), hence trivialized by an étale surjective morphism \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\). Using the quasi-compactness of S, one easily sees that there exist a covering of S by a finite number of affine open sets \(\mathrm{S}_\alpha\) and, for each \(\alpha\), an étale, surjective morphism of finite presentation \(\mathrm{S}'_\alpha \to \mathrm{S}_\alpha\) which trivializes \(\mathrm{H}|_{\mathrm{S}_\alpha}\). This covering \((\mathrm{S}_\alpha)\) of S then comes from a covering \((\mathrm{S}_{i,\alpha})\) of \(\mathrm{S}_i\) for \(i\) large enough (EGA IV 8). After replacing \(\mathrm{S}_i\) by \(\mathrm{S}_{i,\alpha}\) and S by \(\mathrm{S}_\alpha\), we may therefore assume that H is trivialized by an étale surjective morphism \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) of finite presentation. For \(i\) large enough, there then exist a prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'_i\), an étale surjective morphism \(\mathrm{S}'_i \to \mathrm{S}_i\) of finite presentation, and an S-isomorphism \(\mathrm{S}'_i \times_{\mathrm{S}_i} \mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{S}'\) (EGA IV 17.16). Then set, for \(j\) large enough: \[ \mathrm{S}'_j = \mathrm{S}'_i \underset{\mathrm{S}_i}{\times} \mathrm{S}_j, \qquad \mathrm{H}'_j = \mathrm{H}_j \underset{\mathrm{S}_j}{\times} \mathrm{S}'_j, \qquad \mathrm{H}' = \mathrm{H} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{S}'. \] In view of the choice of \(\mathrm{S}'\), there exist an abelian group M of finite type and an S-isomorphism of group schemes \(\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{S}'}(\mathrm{M}) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{H}'\). Since the \(\mathrm{S}'_i\) are quasi-compact and \(\mathrm{S}' = \varprojlim \mathrm{S}'_i\), it follows from Exp. VIB 10 that there exist an index \(j\) and an \(\mathrm{S}'_j\)-isomorphism of group schemes: \[ \mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{S}'_j}(\mathrm{M}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{H}'_j. \] But that is to say that \(\mathrm{H}_j\) is a quasi-isotrivial group of multiplicative type.
When H is isotrivial, one proceeds analogously, using a trivialization of H by a finite étale morphism \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\).

This being so, we can carry out the announced reduction b). The ring A of S is a filtered inductive limit of its subrings \(\mathrm{A}_i\) of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Let \(\mathrm{S}_i = \operatorname{Spec} \mathrm{A}_i\), \(u_j : \mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{S}_j\) and \(u_{jk} : \mathrm{S}_k \to \mathrm{S}_j\) \((k \geqslant j)\) the transition morphisms. Since S is noetherian and G of finite presentation over S, there exist an index \(i\) and an \(\mathrm{S}_i\)-group prescheme \(\mathrm{G}_i\), of finite type over \(\mathrm{S}_i\), such that G is S-isomorphic to \(\mathrm{G}_i \times_{\mathrm{S}_i} \mathrm{S}\). Likewise, E being locally closed in G, we may assume that there exists a locally closed subset \(\mathrm{E}_i\) of \(\mathrm{G}_i\) such that \(\mathrm{E} = \mathrm{E}_i \times_{\mathrm{S}_i} \mathrm{S}\) (EGA IV 8.3.11). For every \(j > i\), write \(\mathrm{G}_j = \mathrm{G}_i \times_{\mathrm{S}_i} \mathrm{S}_j\) and \(\mathrm{E}_j = \mathrm{E}_i \times_{\mathrm{S}_i} \mathrm{S}_j\), and let \(\mathrm{Q}_j\) be the set of points \(s\) of \(\mathrm{S}_j\) such that \((\mathrm{E}_j)_s\) is the set underlying a central subtorus of \((\mathrm{G}_j)_s\).

It follows from 3.4 that \(\mathrm{Q}_k = u_{jk}^{-1}(\mathrm{Q}_j)\) for \(k \geqslant j\), and by hypothesis \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{S}) = u_j^{-1}(\mathrm{Q}_j)\) for \(j \geqslant i\). Moreover, I claim that \(\mathrm{Q}_j\) is ind-constructible (EGA IV 1.9.4). Indeed, since \(\mathrm{S}_j\) is noetherian, it suffices (EGA IV 1.9.10) to see that if S is an integral noetherian scheme, with generic point \(\eta\), and if \(\mathrm{E}_\eta\) is the set underlying a central subtorus \(\mathrm{T}_\eta\) of \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\), there exists a neighbourhood U of \(\eta\) such that for every point \(s\) of U, \(\mathrm{E}_s\) has the same property. Now, after restricting S if necessary, we may assume that the centre Z of G is representable and that there exists a subgroup scheme T of G whose generic fibre is \(\mathrm{T}_\eta\) (VIB §10). But then \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{T})\) and E are two locally closed subsets of \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{G})\) which coincide on the generic fibre, so we can find a neighbourhood U of \(\eta\) such that, above U, \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{T}) = \mathrm{E}\) (EGA IV 8.3.11). For the same reasons, we may assume that above U, T is central, since T is a torus (3.6).

Knowing now that \(\mathrm{Q}_j\) is ind-constructible, it follows from EGA IV 8.3.4 that \(\mathrm{Q}_j = \operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{S}_j)\) for \(j\) large enough. We may then replace S, G, E by \(\mathrm{S}_j\), \(\mathrm{G}_j\), \(\mathrm{E}_j\), which reduces us to the case where S is a reduced affine scheme, of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\).
c) Reduction to the case where S is the spectrum of a noetherian local ring, reduced and complete.

Because of the uniqueness of the torus with underlying space E, the usual technique (EGA IV 8) and lemma 3.6 allow us to replace S by the spectrum of the local ring A of a point of S. Let \(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}\) be the spectrum of the completion \(\widehat{\mathrm{A}}\) of A for the topology defined by the maximal ideal. Since A is the localization of an algebra of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\), \(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}\) is still reduced (EGA IV 7.6.5). I claim that it suffices to solve the problem after the base extension \(\widehat{\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{S}\). Indeed, if \(\widehat{\mathrm{T}}\) denotes the subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_{\widehat{\mathrm{S}}}\) with underlying space \(\mathrm{E}_{\widehat{\mathrm{S}}}\), its two inverse images in \(\mathrm{G}_{\widehat{\mathrm{S}} \times_\mathrm{S} \widehat{\mathrm{S}}}\) are two central subtori having the same underlying space, hence they coincide (Exp. IX 5.1 bis), and by fpqc descent, \(\widehat{\mathrm{T}}\) comes from a torus T of G which answers the question (cf. 3.4).
d) A descent lemma.

Let us recall the following properties of finite morphisms, which were pointed out in TDTE I: Let S and \(\mathrm{S}'\) be two preschemas and \(u : \mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) a finite morphism. Then:
(1) The morphism \(u\) is an epimorphism if and only if the canonical morphism of sheaves: \[ \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S} \longrightarrow u_*(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}) \] is injective.

(2) The morphism \(u\) is an effective epimorphism (Exp. IV 1.3) if and only if the canonical diagram: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S} \longrightarrow u_*(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}) \rightrightarrows u_*(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}) \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}} u_*(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}) \] is exact.
(3) If moreover S is noetherian and \(u\) is an epimorphism, then \(u\) is the composite of a finite sequence of finite effective epimorphisms.
We are then in a position to prove the following lemma, whose proof uses a technique of non-flat descent:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.7

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite type, \(\mathrm{S}'\) a prescheme and \(u : \mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) a finite morphism. For every S-prescheme T, denote by \(\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{T})\) the set of subgroups of multiplicative type of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{T}\) (resp. the set of subgroups of multiplicative type and central of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{T}\)), so that M is naturally a contravariant functor defined on \(\mathbf{Sch}/\mathrm{S}\). Then if \(u\) is an effective epimorphism (resp. an epimorphism), the diagram of sets: \[ (\ast) \qquad \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}') \rightrightarrows \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}' \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{S}') \] is exact.

Proof. i) Reduction to the case where \(u\) is an effective epimorphism. We are thus interested in the functor of subgroups of multiplicative type and central of G. The injectivity of \(\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}) \to \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}')\) is a question of a local nature on S, and this injectivity being granted, the exactness of \((\ast)\) becomes a problem of a local nature on S. We may therefore assume S affine and noetherian.

Let us study the case where \(u : \mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) is the composite of two finite epimorphisms: \[ \mathrm{S}' \xrightarrow{\ v\ } \mathrm{S}'' \xrightarrow{\ w\ } \mathrm{S}. \] I claim that if the two diagrams: \[ (\ast)' \qquad \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}'') \longrightarrow \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}') \rightrightarrows \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}' \underset{\mathrm{S}''}{\times} \mathrm{S}') \] \[ (\ast)'' \qquad \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}'') \rightrightarrows \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}'' \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{S}'') \] are exact, then so is \((\ast)\).

Indeed, the injectivity of \(\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}) \to \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}')\) is clear. If now \(\mathrm{T}'\) is an element of \(\operatorname{Ker} \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}') \rightrightarrows \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}')\), then a fortiori \(\mathrm{T}'\) belongs to \(\operatorname{Ker} \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}') \rightrightarrows \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}' \times_{\mathrm{S}''} \mathrm{S}')\), hence, by the exactness of \((\ast)'\), comes from a unique \(\mathrm{T}''\) of \(\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}'')\). It suffices for us to show that \(\mathrm{T}''\) belongs to \(\operatorname{Ker} \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}'') \rightrightarrows \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}'' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}'')\), for, in view of the exactness of \((\ast)''\), \(\mathrm{T}''\) will then descend to S. So let \(\mathrm{T}''_1\) and \(\mathrm{T}''_2\) be the two inverse images of \(\mathrm{T}''\) in \(\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}'' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}'')\). Since these are two central subgroups of multiplicative type of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}''}\), to show that \(\mathrm{T}''_1 = \mathrm{T}''_2\) it suffices to see that they have the same fibres (Exp. IX 5.1 bis). Consider the commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{S}' \ar[d]_v & \mathrm{S}' \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{S}' \ar@<.4ex>[l] \ar@<-.4ex>[l] \ar[d]^{v \times v} \\ \mathrm{S}'' & \mathrm{S}'' \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{S}'' \ar@<.4ex>[l] \ar@<-.4ex>[l] } \] The morphism \(v\) is a finite epimorphism, hence is dominant (property (a) above) and closed, hence is surjective, and the same holds for \(v \times v\). Now let \(x''\) be a point of \(\mathrm{S}'' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}''\) and \(x'\) a point of \(\mathrm{S}' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}'\) above \(x''\). It follows from the commutativity of the above diagram that the two inverse images of \((\mathrm{T}''_1)_{x''}\) and \((\mathrm{T}''_2)_{x''}\) in \(\mathrm{G}_{x'}\) coincide, hence \((\mathrm{T}''_1)_{x''} = (\mathrm{T}''_2)_{x''}\) (EGA IV 2.2.15).

The above, together with an immediate induction on the number of factors of a factorization of \(u : \mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) into effective epimorphisms (property c) recalled above), shows that to prove the exactness of \((\ast)\) in the case of central subgroups of multiplicative type, we may confine ourselves to the case where \(u\) is an effective epimorphism. Finally, using Exp. IX 5.1 bis once more, we see that it suffices to prove 3.7 when M is the functor of subgroups of multiplicative type of G and \(u\) an effective epimorphism.

ii) Injectivity of \(\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}) \to \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}')\). Since \(u : \mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) is an epimorphism, the canonical morphism: \[ \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S} \longrightarrow u_*(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}) \] is injective; moreover, an S-group of multiplicative type is flat over S; the injectivity of \(\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}) \to \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}')\) is therefore a consequence of the following more general lemma:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.8

Let \(f : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{S}\) and \(g : \mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) be two morphisms of preschemas, \(\mathscr{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}\)-module, \(\mathrm{X}' = \mathrm{X} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}'\), \(\mathscr{F}' = \mathscr{F} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), \(\mathrm{Q}(\mathscr{F})\) the set of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}\)-module quotients \(\mathscr{G}\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) which are quasi-coherent and flat over S, \(\mathrm{Q}(\mathscr{F}')\) the analogue relative to \(\mathscr{F}'\), \(\mathrm{X}'\) and \(\mathrm{S}'\). Suppose \(g\) quasi-compact and \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S} \to g_*(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'})\) injective; then the canonical map: \[ \mathrm{Q}(\mathscr{F}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Q}(\mathscr{F}'), \qquad \mathscr{G} \longmapsto \mathscr{G} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'} \] is injective.

Indeed, we may assume S affine, then X affine. The morphism \(g\) being quasi-compact, \(\mathrm{S}'\) is then a union of a finite number of affine open sets \(\mathrm{S}'_i\). After replacing \(\mathrm{S}'\) by \(\coprod \mathrm{S}'_i\) (an operation which preserves the injectivity of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S} \to g_*(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'})\)), we may assume \(\mathrm{S}'\) affine. We are then reduced to proving the following lemma, whose proof is immediate:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.9

Let \(\mathrm{A} \to \mathrm{A}'\) be an injective ring homomorphism, M an A-module, \(\mathrm{N} = \mathrm{M}/\mathrm{P}\) an A-module quotient flat over A, \(\mathrm{M}' = \mathrm{M} \otimes_\mathrm{A} \mathrm{A}'\), \(\mathrm{N}' = \mathrm{N} \otimes_\mathrm{A} \mathrm{A}' = \mathrm{M}'/\mathrm{P}'\). Then P is the inverse image of \(\mathrm{P}'\) under the canonical homomorphism \(\mathrm{M} \to \mathrm{M}'\) (hence N and P are known once \(\mathrm{N}'\) and \(\mathrm{P}'\) are known).

iii) Exactness of 3.7 \((\ast)\) at \(\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}')\). Let \(\mathrm{T}'\) be an element of \(\operatorname{Ker} \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}') \rightrightarrows \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}')\). Suppose we have proved iii) when S is the spectrum of a noetherian local ring. For every point \(s\) of S, there thus exists a subgroup of multiplicative type \(\mathrm{T}_s\) of \(\mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S},s})\) which comes by descent from \(\mathrm{T}' \times_{\mathrm{S}'} u^{-1} \operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S},s})\). By (Exp. VIB §10 and 3.6), there exist a neighbourhood U of \(s\) in S and a subgroup scheme T of G above U which is of multiplicative type and which extends \(\mathrm{T}_s\). Let \(\mathrm{U}'\) be the inverse image of U in \(\mathrm{S}'\). We then know two subschemes of \(\mathrm{G}'|_{\mathrm{U}'}\): \(\mathrm{T}'|_{\mathrm{U}'}\) and \(\mathrm{T} \times_\mathrm{U} \mathrm{U}'\), which coincide on \(u^{-1}(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S},s}))\). If we regard \(u^{-1}(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S},s}))\) as the projective limit of the schemes \(u^{-1}(\mathrm{V})\) where V runs over the open neighbourhoods of \(s\) in U, it follows from EGA IV 8 that there exists an open set V of U containing \(s\) such that \(\mathrm{T} \times_\mathrm{U} \mathrm{U}'\) and \(\mathrm{T}'\) coincide above \(u^{-1}(\mathrm{V})\). So under the hypotheses made, \(\mathrm{T}'\) descends locally on S; but because of the uniqueness proved in ii), \(\mathrm{T}'\) then descends globally on S. In short, it suffices to prove iii) when S is the spectrum of a noetherian local ring.

Denote then by \(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}\) the spectrum of the completion of the ring of S, and let \(\mathrm{S}'' = \mathrm{S}' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}'\), \(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}' = \widehat{\mathrm{S}} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}'\), \(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}'' = \widehat{\mathrm{S}}' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}'\). I claim that it suffices to show that the diagram: \[ (\ast) \qquad \mathrm{M}(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{M}(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}') \rightrightarrows \mathrm{M}(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}'') \] is exact at \(\mathrm{M}(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}')\). This follows from the commutative diagram below, in which the second row is exact at \(\mathrm{M}(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}')\) by hypothesis, the first two columns are exact (fpqc descent), and the map \(f\) is injective, as follows from ii) applied to the finite epimorphism: \[ \widehat{\mathrm{S}}' \underset{\mathrm{S}'}{\times} \widehat{\mathrm{S}}' \longrightarrow \widehat{\mathrm{S}} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \widehat{\mathrm{S}} \] deduced from \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) by the flat base change \(\widehat{\mathrm{S}} \times_\mathrm{S} \widehat{\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{S}\): \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}') \ar@<.4ex>[r] \ar@<-.4ex>[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}'') \ar[d] \\ \mathrm{M}(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}) \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \mathrm{M}(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}') \ar@<.4ex>[r] \ar@<-.4ex>[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \mathrm{M}(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}'') \\ \mathrm{M}(\widehat{\mathrm{S}} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \widehat{\mathrm{S}}) \ar[r]^-f & \mathrm{M}(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}' \underset{\mathrm{S}'}{\times} \widehat{\mathrm{S}}') & } \] (the diagram-chasing is left to the reader). It follows from the characterization b) of finite effective epimorphisms that the morphism \(\widehat{\mathrm{S}}' \to \widehat{\mathrm{S}}\), deduced from \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) by the flat base change \(\widehat{\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{S}\), is again a finite effective epimorphism. We are thus reduced to the case where, in addition, S is the spectrum of a complete noetherian local ring.

Denote by \(\mathrm{S}_0\) the reduced subscheme of S whose underlying space is the closed point of S. Let \(\mathrm{T}'\) be an element of \(\operatorname{Ker} \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}') \rightrightarrows \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}'')\), and \(\mathrm{T}''\) its image in \(\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}'')\). Since \(\mathrm{S}'_0 = \mathrm{S}' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0\) is faithfully flat over \(\mathrm{S}_0\), there exists an S-subgroup of multiplicative type \(\mathrm{T}_0\) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}_0}\) whose inverse image in \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'_0}\) is \(\mathrm{T}'_{\mathrm{S}'_0}\) (fpqc descent). But S is local noetherian complete, so there exist an S-group of multiplicative type T and an \(\mathrm{S}_0\)-morphism \(u_0 : \mathrm{T} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0 \to \mathrm{T}_0\) (Exp. X 3.3). The inverse image \(u'_0\) of \(u_0\) above \(\mathrm{S}'_0\) extends in a unique way to an \(\mathrm{S}'\)-isomorphism \(u' : \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}'} \to \mathrm{T}'\), again by Exp. X 3.3 (note that \(\mathrm{S}'\), being finite over S local complete, is the sum of a finite number of complete local schemes). The two inverse images of \(u'\) above \(\mathrm{S}''\) are two morphisms from \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}''}\) to \(\mathrm{T}''\) which coincide on \(\mathrm{S}_0 \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}''\), hence they coincide (loc. cit.). Since T is flat over S and \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) is a finite effective epimorphism, it follows from TDTE I page 8 that the diagram: \[ \operatorname{Hom}_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G}) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}'}(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}'}, \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}) \rightrightarrows \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}''}(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}''}, \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}''}) \] is exact. Hence \(u'\) comes from a morphism of preschemas \(u : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{G}\). Likewise \(\mathrm{T} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{T}\) is flat over S, and consequently the map: \[ \operatorname{Hom}_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{T} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G}) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}'}(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}'} \underset{\mathrm{S}'}{\times} \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}'}, \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}) \] is injective. One deduces immediately that \(u\) is a group morphism, since \(u'\) is one. Moreover \(u\) is a monomorphism. Indeed, note first that \(\operatorname{Ker} u\) is flat over S, for to establish this fact we may assume S artinian local (EGA \(0_{\text{III}}\) 10.2.2), hence G separated (Exp. VIA 0.3); but then \(\operatorname{Ker} u\) is of multiplicative type (Exp. IX 6.8), hence flat over S. Since \(\operatorname{Ker}(u) \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}' = \operatorname{Ker}(u') = 0\), we have \(\operatorname{Ker}(u) = 0\) (3.8). But \(u\), being a monomorphism, is an immersion (Exp. VIII, remarks 7.13), and the image group \(u(\mathrm{T})\) in \(\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{S}')\) is \(\mathrm{T}'\), which completes the proof of 3.7.
e) End of the proof of I).

We are reduced by reduction c) to the case where S is the spectrum of a reduced and complete noetherian local ring A. Let \(\mathrm{S}'\) be the spectrum of the normalization \(\mathrm{A}'\) of A, which is finite over A by Nagata (EGA \(0_{\text{IV}}\) 23.1.5); \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) is an epimorphism since A maps injectively into \(\mathrm{A}'\). Suppose then that there exists a torus \(\mathrm{T}'\) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) having \(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) as underlying space. The two inverse images of \(\mathrm{T}'\) in \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}'}\) are two central subtori having the same underlying space, hence they coincide (Exp. IX 5.1 bis); therefore, by 3.7, \(\mathrm{T}'\) comes from a central subtorus T of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{S}\) which obviously admits E as underlying space. It thus suffices to prove the existence of \(\mathrm{T}'\), which reduces us to the case where S is normal and completes the proof of I).
II) Proof of (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii) when S is normal.
