We may restrict ourselves to the case where S is integral; let \(t\) be its generic point. For every integer \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\), let \({}_n\mathrm{G}\) be the subprescheme of G that is the "kernel" of raising to the \(n^{\text{th}}\) power in G. Since E is locally closed in G (by (iii) b)), the intersection of \(\operatorname{ens}({}_n\mathrm{G})\) and E is locally closed in \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{G})\); denote then by \(\mathrm{E}(n)\) the reduced subprescheme of G having \(\operatorname{ens}({}_n\mathrm{G}) \cap \mathrm{E}\) as underlying space.

Let us show that the structural morphism \(\mathrm{E}(n) \to \mathrm{S}\) is separated and universally open. For these two properties, we have a valuative criterion available (EGA II 7.2.3 and EGA IV 14.5.8). So let \(\mathrm{S}'\) be an S-scheme which is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field. We have shown that 3.1 (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) 3.1 (iv), so there exists a subtorus \(\mathrm{T}'\) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) having \(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) as underlying space. Now \({}_n\mathrm{T}'\) is finite and étale over \(\mathrm{S}'\), hence is separated and universally open over \(\mathrm{S}'\), and the same holds for \(\mathrm{E}(n) \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}'\), which has the same underlying space as \({}_n\mathrm{T}'\).

Moreover, the fibres of \(\mathrm{E}(n)\) have the same number of geometric points, namely \(r^n\) if \(r\) is the rank of the torus \(\mathrm{T}_t\). Finally, the generic fibre \(\mathrm{E}(n)_t\), being reduced, equals \({}_n\mathrm{T}_t\), hence is étale over \(\operatorname{Spec}(t)\). Since S is normal, it then follows from SGA I 10.11 that \(\mathrm{E}(n)\) is an étale covering of S.

If \(s\) is a point of S, \(\mathrm{E}(n)_s\) is étale over \(\operatorname{Spec} \kappa(s)\), hence is reduced, and consequently coincides with the group of multiplicative type \({}_n\mathrm{T}_s\). Let us show that \(\mathrm{E}(n)\) is a subgroup prescheme of G. Indeed, let \(m\) be the morphism \[ \mathrm{E}(n) \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{E}(n) \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] induced by multiplication in G. The map \(\operatorname{ens}(m)\) underlying \(m\) factors through \(\mathrm{E}(n)\), so \(m\) factors through the prescheme \(\mathrm{E}(n)\), since the source \(\mathrm{E}(n) \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{E}(n)\) is étale over S, hence reduced. It then follows from Exp. X 4.8 a) that \(\mathrm{E}(n)\) is a subgroup of multiplicative type. As already remarked (3.2 a)), the family of subgroups \(\mathrm{E}(n)\) is necessarily coherent. We have thus proved that (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii) when S is normal.
III) Proof of (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i).

In fact we are going to show that there exists a unique subtorus T of G with underlying space equal to E and such that \({}_n\mathrm{T} = \mathrm{M}_n\) for every \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\). The uniqueness of T follows simply from Exp. IX 4.8 b). To establish the existence of T, taking the uniqueness into account, we may successively assume that:
a) S is noetherian.

b) The \(\mathrm{M}_n\) are central subgroups. Indeed, it suffices to replace G by \(\mathrm{Z}_n = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{M}_n)\) for \(n\) large enough (2.5 and 2.5 bis).

c) S is the spectrum of a local ring. Suppose indeed the problem solved after every base change \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S},s}) \to \mathrm{S}\), where \(s\) runs over the points of S. Let \(\mathrm{T}_s\) be the subtorus of \(\mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S},s})\) thus obtained. For every \(s\) there then exist an open neighbourhood U of \(s\) and a subtorus T of \(\mathrm{G}|_\mathrm{U}\) which extends \(\mathrm{T}_s\) (Exp. VIB §10 and 3.6). We have shown 3.1 (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) 3.1 (iv); since S is noetherian, E is therefore constructible (3.3). Consequently, after restricting U if necessary, we may assume that \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{T}) = \mathrm{E} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{U}\) (EGA IV 9.5.2). But then, for every integer \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\), \({}_n\mathrm{T}\) and \(\mathrm{M}_n \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{U}\) are two subgroups of multiplicative type of \(\mathrm{G}|_\mathrm{U}\) which have the same fibres, hence coincide, \(\mathrm{M}_n\) being central (Exp. IX 5.3 bis). In short, under the hypotheses made, there exists a solution locally on S, hence, because of the uniqueness, there exists a global solution.
d) S is the spectrum of a complete noetherian local ring, and if \(s\) is the closed point, \(\mathrm{T}_s\) is trivial. This follows from EGA \(0_{\text{III}}\) 10.3.1 and fpqc descent.
e) S is reduced. One applies 2.2.
f) S is normal. One applies Nagata's finiteness theorem (EGA \(0_{\text{IV}}\) 23.1.5) and lemma 3.7 in the case of central subtori.

These reductions being made, since \(\mathrm{T}_s\) is trivial, \((\mathrm{M}_n)_s\) is trivial, hence \(\mathrm{M}_n\) is trivial (Exp. X 3.3). If \(t\) is a point of S, the subgroups \({}_n\mathrm{T}_t\) of \(\mathrm{T}_t\) are therefore trivial for every \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\), and it follows easily from Exp. X 1.4 that \(\mathrm{T}_t\) itself is trivial. It then suffices for us to prove the lemma:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.10

Under the hypotheses of 3.1 (ii), suppose further that S is noetherian and normal and that for every point \(s\) of S, \(\mathrm{T}_s\) is a trivial torus. Then there exists a unique subtorus T of G with underlying space equal to E, such that \({}_n\mathrm{T} = \mathrm{M}_n\) for every \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\). Moreover T is trivial.

The uniqueness of T follows from the fact that, T being smooth over S, T is reduced. To prove the existence, we may assume S irreducible with generic point \(\eta\). Let \(r\) be the rank of \(\mathrm{T}_\eta\), \(\mathrm{T}' = \mathbb{G}^r_{m,\,\mathrm{S}}\), \(u_\eta\) an isomorphism from \(\mathrm{T}'_\eta\) onto \(\mathrm{T}_\eta\), \({}_n u_\eta\) the restriction of \(u_\eta\) to \({}_n\mathrm{T}'_\eta\). Since \({}_n\mathrm{T}'\) and \(\mathrm{M}_n\) are trivial, there exists a unique extension \({}_n u\) of \({}_n u_\eta\) to an S-isomorphism from \({}_n\mathrm{T}'\) onto \(\mathrm{M}_n\). I claim that for every point \(s\) of S, there exists a group isomorphism, necessarily unique: \[ u_s : \mathrm{T}'_s \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{T}_s \] which extends \({}_n u_s\) for every \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\). Indeed, let \(\mathrm{S}_1\) be an S-scheme, spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field, whose generic point \(t_1\) projects onto \(\eta\) and whose closed point \(s_1\) projects onto \(s\) (EGA II 7.1.9). It follows from the proof of 3.1 (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) 3.1 (iv) that there exists a subtorus \(\mathrm{T}^1\) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}_1}\) such that \((\mathrm{M}_n)_{\mathrm{S}_1} = {}_n\mathrm{T}^1\) for every \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\). Since \(\mathrm{S}_1\) is normal, \(\mathrm{T}^1\) is isotrivial (Exp. X 4.5), and it follows from the classification of isotrivial tori (Exp. X 1.2) and from SGA1 V 8.2 that \(\mathrm{T}^1\), having a trivial generic fibre, is trivial. We may therefore extend the isomorphism \(u_\eta \times_\eta t_1\) to an \(\mathrm{S}_1\)-isomorphism of group schemes: \[ u^1 : \mathrm{T}' \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{S}_1 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{T}^1. \] The restriction of \(u^1\) to \({}_n\mathrm{T}' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_1\) on the one hand, and \({}_n u \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_1\) on the other hand, coincide on the generic fibre by construction, hence coincide. The restriction of \(u^1\) to the closed fibre therefore realizes the desired extension after the residue field extension \(\kappa(s) \to \kappa(s_1)\). It then follows from Exp. IX 4.8 a) and from fpqc descent that \(u^1_{s_1}\) descends to \(\kappa(s)\) as a group isomorphism \(u_s : \mathrm{T}'_s \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{T}_s\) which answers the question.

Moreover, \(\mathrm{T}'\) is smooth over S, which is normal, hence is normal. It then follows from an easy criterion for extending rational maps (EGA IV\(_4\) 20.4.6) that, for there to exist an S-morphism \(u : \mathrm{T}' \to \mathrm{G}\) whose restriction to \(\mathrm{T}'_s\), for every point \(s\) of S, is the composite morphism: \[ \mathrm{T}'_s \xrightarrow{\ u_s\ } \mathrm{T}_s \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}_s \] it is necessary and sufficient that this be the case after every base change \(\mathrm{S}_1 \to \mathrm{S}\), where \(\mathrm{S}_1\) is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field.

Now in the present case, an argument analogous to the one just made shows that the extension condition is indeed satisfied. Denote by \(u\) the S-morphism \(\mathrm{T}' \to \mathrm{G}\) thus obtained.

Let us show that \(u\) is indeed a group morphism. Let \(m_{\mathrm{T}'}\) (resp. \(m_\mathrm{G}\)) be the morphism defining multiplication in \(\mathrm{T}'\) (resp. G). We must verify that \(m_\mathrm{G} \circ (u \times_\mathrm{S} u) = u \circ m_{\mathrm{T}'}\). Now the coincidence subprescheme of these two morphisms is a subprescheme of \(\mathrm{T}' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{T}'\) which dominates the fibres (since \(u_s\) is a group morphism), hence has the same underlying space as \(\mathrm{T}' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{T}'\), hence is equal to the latter, since \(\mathrm{T}' \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{T}'\) is smooth over S, hence reduced.

Finally, note that \(u\) is a monomorphism (since it is one on the fibres), hence is an immersion (Exp. VIII 7.9). The image of \(\mathrm{T}'\) under \(u\) is then a subtorus of G having E as underlying set.
This completes the proof of theorem 3.1.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: The fact that (iv) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii) will appear in the course of the proof.

pages.title: 4. Characterization of a subtorus T by the subgroups \({}_n\mathrm{T}\)

blocks.html: 4. Characterization of a subtorus T by the subgroups \({}_n\mathrm{T}\)
1. Statement of the main theorem

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.1

Let S be a connected locally noetherian prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite type over S, \(q\) an integer \(> 1\) invertible on S, \(r\) a positive integer. For every integer \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\), let \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) be a subgroup scheme of G, of multiplicative type and of type \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^r\). We assume that:

a) The family of subgroups \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) is coherent, that is to say, if the integer \(m\) divides \(n\), we have: \[ {}_m\mathrm{M}(n) = \mathrm{M}(m). \]

b) There exist a point \(s\) of S and a subtorus \(\mathrm{T}_s\) of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) such that: \[ \mathrm{M}(n)_s = {}_n\mathrm{T}_s \quad \text{for every } n. \]

c) For every point \(t\) of S, there exists a closed and affine subscheme \(\mathrm{F}_{\bar{t}}\) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{t}}\) which dominates \(\mathrm{M}(n)_{\bar{t}}\) for every \(n\).

Then there exists one and only one subtorus T of G such that \({}_n\mathrm{T} = \mathrm{M}(n)\) for every \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\).
There is an analogous theorem concerning the lifting of morphisms:

blocks.label: 4.1 bis
blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.1 bis

Let S, G and \(q\) be as above, T an S-torus, and for every integer \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\), let \(u(n)\) be an S-morphism of groups \({}_n\mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{G}\). We assume that:

a) The family of morphisms \(u(n)\) is coherent, i.e., if \(m\) divides \(n\), we have: \[ u(m) = u(n)|_{{}_m\mathrm{T}}. \]

b) There exist a point \(s\) of S and a group morphism: \[ u_s : \mathrm{T}_s \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}_s \] such that \(u_s|_{{}_n\mathrm{T}_s} = u(n)_s\) for every \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\).

c) For every point \(t\) of S, there exists a closed and affine subscheme \(\mathrm{F}_{\bar{t}}\) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{t}}\) which dominates \(u(n)_{\bar{t}}({}_n\mathrm{T}_{\bar{t}})\) for every \(n\).

Then there exists a unique group morphism \(u : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{G}\) such that for every \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\), the restriction of \(u\) to \({}_n\mathrm{T}\) equals \(u(n)\).

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 4.2

Using the lower semicontinuity of the abelian rank of a flat group prescheme of finite type over the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring (see Exp. X 8.7), one can, in the statement of 4.1 and of 4.1 bis, weaken condition c) by simply requiring that the closed and affine subscheme \(\mathrm{F}_{\bar{t}}\) demanded exist for every maximal point \(t\) of S.

Let us show how 4.1 bis follows from 4.1. Let \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{T}\). For every integer \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\), consider the group morphism: \[ v(n) : {}_n\mathrm{T} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}' \] whose projections onto G and T are respectively \(u(n)\) and the canonical immersion \({}_n\mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{T}\). The morphism \(v(n)\) is thus an immersion; let \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) be the image subgroup. It is clear that the family of subgroups \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) is coherent in the sense of 4.1, that the group \(\mathrm{M}(n)_s\) equals \({}_n\mathrm{T}'_s\), where \(\mathrm{T}'_s\) is the subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}'_s\) that is the graph of \(u_s\), and that for every point \(t\) of S, the closed and affine subscheme \(\mathrm{F}_{\bar{t}} \times_{\bar{t}} \mathrm{T}_{\bar{t}}\) of \(\mathrm{G}'_{\bar{t}}\) dominates the subgroups \(\mathrm{M}(n)_{\bar{t}}\) for every \(n\). By 4.1 there thus exists a subtorus \(\mathrm{T}'\) of \(\mathrm{G}'\) such that \({}_n\mathrm{T}' = \mathrm{M}(n)\) for every \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\). Let \(f\) be the restriction to \(\mathrm{T}'\) of the projection of \(\mathrm{G}'\) onto T, and \(f(n)\) the restriction of \(f\) to \(\mathrm{M}(n)\). We have: \[ f(n) \circ v(n) = \operatorname{id}_{{}_n\mathrm{T}}. \] The fibre at \(s\) of \(\mathrm{T}'\) is the torus already denoted \(\mathrm{T}'_s\), equal to the graph of \(u_s\) (this follows from Exp. IX 4.8 b)), so \(f_s\) is an isomorphism. But \(\operatorname{Ker} f\) and \(\operatorname{Coker} f\) are groups of multiplicative type (Exp. IX 2.7) of constant type, S being connected, hence reduced to the unit group, and \(f\) is an isomorphism. Let \(v\) be the inverse isomorphism of \(f\). We have: \[ v|_{{}_n\mathrm{T}} = v(n). \] Consequently, the composite of \(v\) and the projection of \(\mathrm{G}'\) onto G is a morphism \(u : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{G}\) which answers the question. The above proves the existence of the morphism \(u\); as for the uniqueness, it follows in any case from Exp. IX 4.8 a).
2. Application
We propose to generalize theorem 7.1 of Exp. IX.

Let A be a complete noetherian local ring, I its maximal ideal, \(\mathrm{S} = \operatorname{Spec} \mathrm{A}\), \(\mathrm{S}_m = \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{A}/\mathrm{I}^m)\). For every prescheme X, set \(\mathrm{X}_m = \mathrm{X} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_m\).

Now let G be an S-group prescheme of finite type, T an S-torus, \(q\) an integer invertible on S, and \(u_m : \mathrm{T}_m \to \mathrm{G}_m\) \((m \in \mathbb{N})\) a coherent family of group morphisms. The integer \(n\) running over the powers of \(q\), denote by \(u_m(n)\) the restriction of \(u_m\) to \({}_n\mathrm{T}_m\), and by \(u(n)\) the unique group morphism: \[ u(n) : {}_n\mathrm{T} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] which extends the morphisms \(u_m(n)\) for every \(m\) (1.6 a)). We shall say that the family \((u_m)\), \(m \in \mathbb{N}\), is admissible if for every point \(t\) of S, there exists a closed affine subprescheme \(\mathrm{F}_{\bar{t}}\) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{t}}\) which dominates \(u(n)_{\bar{t}}({}_n\mathrm{T}_{\bar{t}})\) for every \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\) (this property is independent of \(q\), as we shall see).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.3

With the above notation, the canonical map: \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G}) \longrightarrow \varprojlim_m \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}_m\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}_m, \mathrm{G}_m) \] induces an isomorphism of the left-hand side onto the subset of the right-hand side formed by the "admissible" coherent families.
Indeed, it suffices to apply 4.1 bis, taking for \(s\) the closed point of S.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4

With the above notation, suppose moreover that G has affine fibres; then the canonical map: \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G}) \longrightarrow \varprojlim_m \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}_m\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}_m, \mathrm{G}_m) \] is an isomorphism.
Indeed, if G has affine fibres, every coherent family is "admissible".

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 4.5

When G is separated, one may in 4.3 replace the ring A by a noetherian I-adic ring; indeed, one can use EGA III 5.4.1 instead of 1.6 a).
3. Proof of 4.1

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.6

Let \(k\) be a field, G a \(k\)-algebraic group, \(q\) an integer prime to the residue characteristic of \(k\), \(r\) an integer \(> 0\), \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) (\(n\) running over the powers of \(q\)) a coherent family of subgroups of G, of multiplicative type and of type \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^r\). Then there exists a smallest closed subscheme H of G which dominates the \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) for every \(n\). Moreover, H is an algebraic subgroup of G, smooth, connected and commutative, "whose formation is compatible with extension of the base field".

Let M be the subset of \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{G})\) that is the union of the sets underlying the subgroups \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) for every \(n\), and H the reduced closed subscheme of G having the closure of M as underlying space. Since \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) is étale over \(k\), hence reduced, \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) is contained in H, and consequently H is the smallest closed subscheme of G which dominates \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) for every \(n\). Now let \(\bar{k}\) be an algebraically closed extension of \(k\). By construction, the subschemes \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) are schematically dense in H (Exp. IX 4.1), so the \(\mathrm{M}(n)_{\bar{k}}\) are schematically dense in \(\mathrm{H}_{\bar{k}}\) (Exp. IX 4.5); moreover \(\mathrm{M}(n)_{\bar{k}}\) is reduced, and it follows that \(\mathrm{H}_{\bar{k}}\) is necessarily equal to the reduced closed subscheme of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{k}}\) having the closure of \(\mathrm{M}_{\bar{k}}\) as underlying space. This proves that H is geometrically reduced (EGA IV 4.6.1). The family \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) being coherent, M is stable under the group law; moreover \(\mathrm{M} \times_k \mathrm{M}\) is dense in \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{H} \times_k \mathrm{H})\) and \(\mathrm{H} \times_k \mathrm{H}\) is reduced (EGA IV 4.6.5); one immediately deduces that H is an algebraic subgroup of G. Moreover H is smooth over S, since it is geometrically reduced (Exp. VIA 1.3.1), and H is commutative since the \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) are commutative. It remains to see that H is connected. Let \(\mathrm{H}^0\) be the identity component of H, \(m\) the number of geometric points of \(\mathrm{H}/\mathrm{H}^0\), \(q^s\) the exponent of \(q\) in the prime factorization of \(m\). For every integer \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\), \(q^s\mathrm{M}(n)\) is then contained in \(\mathrm{H}^0\). But the family \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) is coherent and \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) is of type \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^r\); consequently \(q^s\mathrm{M}(nq^s) = \mathrm{M}(n)\). Hence \(\mathrm{H}^0\) dominates \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) for every \(n\), and consequently equals H.

This being so, we shall make condition c) of 4.1 more precise. Indeed, by the above, we may consider the smallest closed subscheme \(\mathrm{H}_t\) of \(\mathrm{G}_t\) which dominates \(\mathrm{M}(n)_t\) for every \(n\). The formation of \(\mathrm{H}_t\) commuting with extension of the base field (4.6), \(\mathrm{H}_{\bar{t}} = \mathrm{H}_t \times_t \bar{t}\) is contained in the affine closed subset \(\mathrm{F}_{\bar{t}}\), hence is affine. In short, \(\mathrm{H}_t\) is affine. On the other hand, we know (4.6) that \(\mathrm{H}_t\) is a smooth, connected, commutative algebraic group. It then follows from the structure of smooth commutative connected affine algebraic groups over an algebraically closed field (BIBLE 4 Th. 4) that \(\mathrm{H}_{\bar{t}}\) is the direct product of a torus \(\mathrm{T}_{\bar{t}}\) and a unipotent group \(\mathrm{U}_{\bar{t}}\). But then \(\mathrm{M}(n)_{\bar{t}}\) is necessarily contained in \(\mathrm{T}_{\bar{t}}\), so \(\mathrm{H}_{\bar{t}} = \mathrm{T}_{\bar{t}}\), and consequently \(\mathrm{H}_t\) is a torus.
We can now embark on the proof of 4.1.
a) Uniqueness of the solution: it suffices to apply Exp. IX 4.8 b).

b) Case where S is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring A with algebraically closed residue field \(k\). Denote by K the fraction field of A, \(s\) the closed point of S, \(t\) the generic point, J the maximal ideal of A, \(\mathrm{S}_m = \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{A}/\mathrm{J}^m)\), \(\mathrm{X}_m = \mathrm{X} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_m\).
Let us distinguish two cases:

1st case: The point \(s\) of 4.1 b) is the generic point \(t\) of S. Then let \(\mathrm{T}'\) be the schematic closure in G of the torus \(\mathrm{T}_t\). The closed fibre \(\mathrm{T}'_s\) is thus an algebraic subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\), of dimension \(r\), which dominates \(\mathrm{M}(n)_s\) for every \(n\), so \(\mathrm{T}'_s\) dominates \(\mathrm{H}_s\). But \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is a torus containing \(\mathrm{M}(n)_s\), so \(\mathrm{H}_s\) has rank at least \(r\). Consequently \(\mathrm{H}_s\) has the same underlying space as the identity component of \(\mathrm{T}'_s\). The "identity component" \((\mathrm{T}')^0\) of \(\mathrm{T}'\) is then a subgroup prescheme of G, flat, separated (VIB 5.2), whose generic fibre \(\mathrm{T}_t\) is a torus and whose reduced closed fibre \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is a torus. But then \((\mathrm{T}')^0\) is a torus (Exp. X 8.8), which we denote by T. The groups \({}_n\mathrm{T}\) and \(\mathrm{M}_n\) are smooth over S, hence reduced, and since they have the same underlying space, they coincide. So the torus T is the solution of the problem.

2nd case: The point \(s\) of 4.1 b) is the closed point \(s\) of S. After replacing G by the schematic closure in G of the smallest algebraic subgroup \(\mathrm{H}_t\) which dominates the family \(\mathrm{M}(n)_t\) (4.6), we may assume \(\mathrm{G}_t\) affine.

For every integer \(m \geqslant 0\), it follows from 2.2 that there exists a unique subtorus \(\mathrm{T}_m\) of \(\mathrm{G}_m\) which lifts \(\mathrm{T}_s\) and such that for every integer \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\), we have \({}_n\mathrm{T}_m = \mathrm{M}(n)_m\). Moreover, let \(\mathrm{T}' = \mathbb{G}^r_{m,\,\mathrm{S}}\). Since \(k\) is algebraically closed, \(\mathrm{T}_s\) is trivial and there exists a \(k\)-isomorphism \(u_s : \mathrm{T}'_s \to \mathrm{T}_s\). The morphism \(u_s\) lifts in a unique way to an \(\mathrm{S}_m\)-isomorphism \(u_m : \mathrm{T}'_m \to \mathrm{T}_m\) (Exp. IX 3.3). The family of morphisms \(u_m\), \(m \in \mathbb{N}\), defines in the limit a morphism \(\tilde{u}\) of the formal completions \(\widehat{\mathrm{T}'}\) and \(\widehat{\mathrm{G}}\) of \(\mathrm{T}'\) and G along their closed fibre: \[ \xymatrix{ \widehat{\mathrm{T}'} \ar[r]^{\tilde{u}} \ar[d]_i & \widehat{\mathrm{G}} \ar[d]^j \\ \mathrm{T}' & \mathrm{G} } \] where \(i\) and \(j\) denote the canonical morphisms.
We are going to show that the morphism \(\tilde{u}\) is algebrizable. For this, we shall reduce to the case where the group G is affine.

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.7

Let S be the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring A, X and Y two S-preschemas, quasi-compact, quasi-separated and flat over S. Then the canonical map: \[ \Gamma(\mathrm{X}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}) \otimes_\mathrm{A} \Gamma(\mathrm{Y}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{Y}) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\mathrm{X} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{Y}, \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{X} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{Y}}) \] is an isomorphism.

Let \(f : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{S}\) (resp. \(g : \mathrm{Y} \to \mathrm{S}\)) be the structural morphism. Since X (resp. Y) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated, it follows from EGA I 9.2.2 and EGA IV 1.7.4 that \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X})\) (resp. \(g_*(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{Y})\)) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-algebra, which therefore corresponds to an affine S-scheme \(\widetilde{\mathrm{X}}\) (resp. \(\widetilde{\mathrm{Y}}\)). By hypothesis, Y is flat over S; it then follows from EGA III 1.4.15, taking EGA IV 1.7.21 into account, that the canonical map (deduced from the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{X} \to \widetilde{\mathrm{X}}\)): \[ \Gamma(\widetilde{\mathrm{X}} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{Y}, \mathscr{O}_{\widetilde{\mathrm{X}} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{Y}}) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\mathrm{X} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{Y}, \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{X} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{Y}}) \] is an isomorphism. But X being flat over S, \(\widetilde{\mathrm{X}}\) is flat over S (for flat over A is equivalent to torsion-free). Applying EGA III 1.4.15 again, interchanging the roles of \(\widetilde{\mathrm{X}}\) and Y, we obtain an isomorphism: \[ \Gamma(\widetilde{\mathrm{X}}, \mathscr{O}_{\widetilde{\mathrm{X}}}) \otimes_\mathrm{A} \Gamma(\widetilde{\mathrm{Y}}, \mathscr{O}_{\widetilde{\mathrm{Y}}}) \simeq \Gamma(\widetilde{\mathrm{X}} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \widetilde{\mathrm{Y}}, \mathscr{O}_{\widetilde{\mathrm{X}} \times_\mathrm{S} \widetilde{\mathrm{Y}}}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Gamma(\widetilde{\mathrm{X}} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{Y}, \mathscr{O}_{\widetilde{\mathrm{X}} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{Y}}) \] whence the lemma.

In the present case, the S-group G is flat over S and of finite type, hence quasi-compact and quasi-separated. We may therefore apply the lemma to \(\mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{G}\): \[ \Gamma(\mathrm{G}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}) \otimes_\mathrm{A} \Gamma(\mathrm{G}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Gamma(\mathrm{G} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{G}, \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{G}}). \] To the morphism \(m_g : \mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}\) defining the multiplication in G there thus corresponds a morphism: \[ \Gamma(\mathrm{G}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\mathrm{G} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{G}, \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{G}}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Gamma(\mathrm{G}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}) \otimes_\mathrm{A} \Gamma(\mathrm{G}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G}) \] hence an S-morphism: \[ m_{\widetilde{\mathrm{G}}} : \widetilde{\mathrm{G}} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \widetilde{\mathrm{G}} \longrightarrow \widetilde{\mathrm{G}}, \] where \(\widetilde{\mathrm{G}}\) denotes the affine S-scheme having \(\Gamma(\mathrm{G}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G})\) as A-algebra. It is a formality, from there, to verify that \(m_{\widetilde{\mathrm{G}}}\) endows \(\widetilde{\mathrm{G}}\) with a structure of S-group scheme such that the canonical morphism \(v : \mathrm{G} \to \widetilde{\mathrm{G}}\) is a morphism of S-groups.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 4.8

\(\widetilde{\mathrm{G}}\) plays the role of a largest "affine quotient" of G; in fact, one can show that \(\widetilde{\mathrm{G}}\) is indeed a quotient of G for fpqc, hence is of finite type over S (XVII App. III, 2).

In the case at hand, the generic fibre \(\mathrm{G}_t\) of G is affine; it then follows from EGA I 9.3.3 that \(v_t\) is an isomorphism. Since \(\widetilde{\mathrm{G}}\) is affine, the coherent family of morphisms: \[ w_m = v_m u_m : \mathrm{T}'_m \longrightarrow \widetilde{\mathrm{G}}_m \quad (m \in \mathbb{N}) \] comes from a unique morphism of S-groups (Exp. IX 7.1) \[ w : \mathrm{T}' \longrightarrow \widetilde{\mathrm{G}}. \] Then let \(\mathrm{T}_t\) be the subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_t\) equal to \(v_t^{-1} w_t(\mathrm{T}'_t)\). The torus \(\mathrm{T}_t\) is thus of rank at most \(r\) (being the image of a torus of rank \(r\)). Let us show that \(\mathrm{T}_t\) dominates \(\mathrm{M}(n)_t\) for every \(n\). Indeed, let \(u(n)_m\) be the \(\mathrm{S}_m\)-isomorphism \(({}_n\mathrm{T}')_m \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{M}(n)_m\) obtained by restricting \(u_m\) to \(({}_n\mathrm{T}')_m\). The coherent family of morphisms \(u(n)_m\) comes from a unique S-isomorphism \(u(n) : {}_n\mathrm{T}' \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{M}(n)\) (since \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) is finite over S). For every integer \(m \geqslant 0\), we then have the equalities: \[ w_m|_{({}_n\mathrm{T}')_m} = (v_m u_m)|_{({}_n\mathrm{T}')_m} = (v \circ u(n))_m. \] Consequently, \(w|_{{}_n\mathrm{T}'} = v \circ u(n)\) (1.6 a)). In particular, we have: \[ w_t|_{({}_n\mathrm{T}')_t} = v_t \circ u(n)_t, \] hence \(v_t^{-1} w_t ({}_n\mathrm{T}')_t = u(n)_t ({}_n\mathrm{T}')_t = \mathrm{M}(n)_t\).

This indeed proves that \(\mathrm{T}_t\) dominates \(\mathrm{M}(n)_t\), and implies that \(\mathrm{T}_t\) is of rank \(r\). One finishes as in the first case already studied, by considering the schematic closure in G of \(\mathrm{T}_t\), say \(\mathrm{T}''\). Since \(\mathrm{T}_t\) dominates \(\mathrm{M}(n)_t\), \(\mathrm{T}''_s\) dominates \(\mathrm{M}(n)_s\), hence dominates \(\mathrm{T}_s\) (density theorem). On the other hand, \(\mathrm{T}''\) being flat over S and \(\mathrm{T}_t\) of dimension \(r\), \(\mathrm{T}''_s\) is of dimension \(r\) (Exp. VIB 4). In short, \(\mathrm{T}_s\) has the same underlying space as the identity component of \(\mathrm{T}''_s\), and one concludes as in the first case that the identity component of \(\mathrm{T}''\) is a subtorus of G which answers the question.
c) End of the proof of 4.1.

To prove the existence of the subtorus T, we may assume S reduced (2.2). Taking into account 3.1 (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i), it then suffices to prove that the set U of points \(x\) of S such that there exists a subtorus \(\mathrm{T}_x\) of \(\mathrm{G}_x\) with \({}_n\mathrm{T}_x = \mathrm{M}(n)_x\) for every \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\), is equal to \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{S})\). The torus \(\mathrm{T}_x\) in question is necessarily unique, and by fpqc descent it suffices to prove its existence after extension of the residue field of \(x\) (Exp. IX 4.8 b)).

This being so, since S is locally noetherian and connected and since U is not empty (it contains the point \(s\) of 4.1 b)), we are reduced, by an immediate argument, to proving that if \(x\) and \(x'\) are two points of S, \(x\) being a specialization of \(x'\), and if one of the two points belongs to U, then both points are in U. By the usual technique (EGA II 7.1.9) we reduce to the case where S is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field, a case which was treated in b).
This completes the proof of theorem 4.1.

pages.title: 5. Representability of the functor: smooth subgroups identical to their connected normalizer

blocks.html: 5. Representability of the functor: smooth subgroups identical to their connected normalizer

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.1

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite presentation, H a subgroup prescheme of G, smooth with connected fibres. Then:

a) The normalizer N of H in G is representable by a closed subprescheme of G, of finite presentation over S.

b) The following conditions are equivalent:

i) The canonical immersion \(\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{N}\) is an open immersion.

ii) The group N is smooth along the unit section and its identity component, which is then representable (Exp. VIB 3.10), is equal to H.

iii) For every point \(s\) of S, we have \(\mathrm{H}_s = (\mathrm{N}_s)^0\).

Proof. The group prescheme H is locally of finite presentation over S (H is smooth over S) and has connected fibres, hence is of finite presentation over S (Exp. VIB 5.3.3). Assertion a) is then a consequence of Exp. XI 6.11. The equivalence of the conditions appearing in b) is included here for the record and was proved in VIB 6.5.1.
This being so, we can state the main theorem of this section:

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.2

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S, \(\mathscr{L}_\mathrm{G}\) (or simply \(\mathscr{L}\) if there is no ambiguity) the S-functor such that for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\) we have:

\(\mathscr{L}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}') =\) the set of subgroup preschemes of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), smooth over \(\mathrm{S}'\), with connected fibres, which are identical to their connected normalizer.

Then the functor \(\mathscr{L}\) is representable by an S-prescheme, a union of an increasing family \((\mathrm{U}_i)_{i \in \mathbb{N}}\) of open subpreschemes, quasi-projective, of finite presentation over S, hence a fortiori separated over S.
First reductions.
For every integer \(r \geqslant 0\), let \(\mathscr{L}_r\) be the subfunctor of \(\mathscr{L}\) such that for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\) we have:

\(\mathscr{L}_r(\mathrm{S}') =\) the set of subgroup preschemes of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), smooth, with connected fibres, identical to their connected normalizer and of relative dimension \(r\).

Since the dimension of the fibres of a smooth group is a locally constant function, the canonical monomorphism \(\mathscr{L}_r \to \mathscr{L}\) is representable by an immersion that is both open and closed. It therefore suffices to show that for every \(r\), \(\mathscr{L}_r\) is representable by an S-prescheme having the above properties, for \(\mathscr{L}\) will then be representable by the sum S-prescheme \(\coprod_{r \in \mathbb{N}} \mathscr{L}_r\).

For every integer \(n \geqslant 0\), every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\) and every subgroup prescheme H of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), we shall denote by \(\mathrm{H}^{(n)}\) the \(n^{\text{th}}\) normal invariant of the unit section \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{H}\) of H (EGA IV 16.1.2), so that \(\mathrm{H}^{(n)}\) is a sheaf of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}\)-modules, of finite type, corresponding to the \(n^{\text{th}}\) infinitesimal neighbourhood of the unit section of H. If H is smooth over \(\mathrm{S}'\) of relative dimension \(r\), \(\mathrm{H}^{(n)}\) is a locally free \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-module whose rank \(\varphi(n, r)\) depends only on \(n\) and \(r\). Moreover, since H is a subprescheme of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), we have a canonical epimorphism, compatible with base extension: \[ \mathrm{G}^{(n)} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'} \simeq \mathrm{G}^{(n)}_{\mathrm{S}'} \to \mathrm{H}^{(n)}. \] Let us then introduce the projective S-scheme: \[ \mathrm{P}_{\varphi(n,r)} = \operatorname{Grass}_{\varphi(n,r)}\big((\mathrm{G})^{(n)}\big) \] (EGA I 2nd ed. 9.7; cf. also Séminaire Cartan, 1960/61, Exp. No. 14 by A. Grothendieck). It then follows from the preceding remarks that the map: \[ \mathrm{H} \longmapsto \mathrm{H}^{(n)} \] defines a canonical morphism: \[ u_{n,r} : \mathscr{L}_r \longrightarrow \mathrm{P}_{\varphi(n,r)}. \] The group G acts naturally on \(\mathrm{G}^{(n)}\), hence on \(\mathrm{P}_{\varphi(n,r)}\), via the representation: \[ \operatorname{int} : \mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Aut}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{G}), \qquad g \mapsto \operatorname{int}(g). \] Moreover, if \(\mathrm{S}'\) is a quasi-compact S-prescheme and H an element of \(\mathscr{L}_r(\mathrm{S}')\), we know (Exp. XI 6.11) that for \(n\) large enough we have: \[ \mathrm{N} = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H}) = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H}^{(n)}). \] For each integer \(n \geqslant 0\), let us introduce the subfunctor \(\mathscr{L}^n_r\) of \(\mathscr{L}_r\) such that for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\) we have:

\(\mathscr{L}^n_r(\mathrm{S}') =\) the set of subgroups H of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) which belong to \(\mathscr{L}_r(\mathrm{S}')\) and such that \(\underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H}) = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H}^{(n)})\).
Representability of \(\mathscr{L}^n_r\).

Since the integer \(r\) is fixed until the end of the proof of 5.2, we shall omit recalling it in the notation. Thus we shall write \(\mathscr{L}\), \(\mathscr{L}^n\), \(\mathrm{P}_n\), \(u_n\) instead of \(\mathscr{L}_r\), \(\mathscr{L}^n_r\), \(\mathrm{P}_{\varphi(n,r)}\), \(u_{n,r}\).

Let \(v_n\) be the restriction of \(u_n\) to the subfunctor \(\mathscr{L}^n\) of \(\mathscr{L}\). It follows from the definition of \(\mathscr{L}^n\) and from 5.1 b) ii) that \(v_n\) is a monomorphism. In fact we have the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.3

The morphism \(v_n\) is an immersion of finite presentation. A fortiori, \(\mathscr{L}^n\) is representable by an S-prescheme, quasi-projective and of finite presentation over S.

After replacing S by \(\mathrm{P}_n\), we are reduced by the usual technique to proving the following assertion: Let \(\mathrm{Q} \in \mathrm{P}_n(\mathrm{S})\), and consider the subfunctor \(\mathscr{F}\) of the functor \(h_\mathrm{S}\) represented by the final object S of \(\mathbf{Sch}/\mathrm{S}\), such that for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\) we have:

\(\mathscr{F}(\mathrm{S}') = h_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{S}')\) (a set reduced to one element) if there exists \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{L}^n(\mathrm{S}')\) such that \(\mathrm{H}^{(n)} = \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{S}'}\),
\(\mathscr{F}(\mathrm{S}') = \varnothing\) otherwise.
Then the canonical monomorphism \(\mathscr{F} \to \mathrm{S}\) is an immersion of finite presentation.

Let us begin by transforming the definition of the functor \(\mathscr{F}\). For this, note that the normalizer of Q in G is representable by a subgroup prescheme N of finite presentation over S (namely, the inverse image of the point Q of \(\mathrm{P}_n(\mathrm{S})\) under the morphism: \[ \mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \mathrm{P}_n, \qquad g \mapsto g \cdot \mathrm{Q}. \] I claim that the functor \(\mathscr{F}\) coincides with the following subfunctor of \(h_\mathrm{S}\):

\(\mathscr{F}(\mathrm{S}') = h_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{S}')\) if we have:
(a) \(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is smooth along the unit section and of relative dimension \(r\).
(b) \((\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'})^{(n)}\) (which is then canonically an element of \(\mathrm{P}_n(\mathrm{S}')\)) is equal to \(\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{S}'}\).
\(\mathscr{F}(\mathrm{S}') = \varnothing\) otherwise.
Indeed, denote provisionally by \(\mathscr{F}_1\) the functor \(\mathscr{F}\) in the first version and by \(\mathscr{F}_2\) the functor \(\mathscr{F}\) in the second version. Then:
i) \(\mathscr{F}_1(\mathrm{S}') = h_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{S}') \Rightarrow \mathscr{F}_2(\mathrm{S}') = h_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{S}')\).

Indeed, let \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{F}^n(\mathrm{S}')\) be the subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) such that \(\mathrm{H}^{(n)} = \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{S}'}\). We thus have: \[ \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H}) = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H}^{(n)}) = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{S}'}) = \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'}. \] Hence by 5.1 b) ii), \(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is smooth along the unit section and its identity component is H. Consequently, \(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is of relative dimension \(r\), and since H is open in \(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) (by 5.1 b) i)), we have \((\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'})^{(n)} = \mathrm{H}^{(n)} = \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{S}'}\). In short, \(\mathscr{F}_2(\mathrm{S}') = h_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{S}')\).
ii) \(\mathscr{F}_2(\mathrm{S}') = h_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{S}') \Rightarrow \mathscr{F}_1(\mathrm{S}') = h_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{S}')\).

By hypothesis, \(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is smooth along the unit section, of relative dimension \(r\); its identity component is therefore representable (Exp. VIB 3.10) by a subgroup prescheme H, smooth over \(\mathrm{S}'\), with connected fibres of dimension \(r\). Since H is normal in \(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) and open in \(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), we have the following inclusions: \[ \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'} \subset \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H}) \subset \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H}^{(n)}) = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{N}^{(n)}_{\mathrm{S}'}) = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{S}'}) = \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'}. \] The above inclusions are therefore equalities. The first inclusion then shows that H is equal to its connected normalizer, and the second shows that H is an element of \(\mathscr{L}^n(\mathrm{S}')\). That is to say, \(\mathscr{F}_1(\mathrm{S}') = h_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{S}')\).

The implications i) and ii) entail \(\mathscr{F}_1 = \mathscr{F}_2\). We keep the second definition of the functor \(\mathscr{F}\), and we shall first "represent condition a)" by an immersion of finite presentation. For this, it suffices to apply the:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.4

Let S be a prescheme, X an S-prescheme locally of finite presentation over S, \(\sigma : \mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{X}\) a section of X, \(r\) an integer \(\geqslant 0\), and \(\mathrm{L} : (\mathbf{Sch}/\mathrm{S})^\circ \to (\mathbf{Ens})\) the subfunctor of S defined as follows:

\(\mathrm{L}(\mathrm{S}') = h_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{S}')\) if \(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is smooth along the section \(\sigma_{\mathrm{S}'}\) and of relative dimension \(r\) at the points of \(\sigma_{\mathrm{S}'}(\mathrm{S}')\).
\(\mathrm{L}(\mathrm{S}') = \varnothing\) otherwise.

Then:

a) The monomorphism \(\mathrm{L} \to \mathrm{S}\) is an immersion of finite presentation.

b) Let J be the conormal sheaf relative to the immersion \(\mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{X}\) (EGA IV 16.1.2); suppose that for every point \(s\) of S, \(\mathrm{J} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S},s}} \kappa(s)\) is of rank at most \(r\); then the immersion \(\mathrm{L} \to \mathrm{S}\) is a closed immersion.

Proof. The functor L is of a local nature on S, which reduces us to the case where S is affine. After replacing X by a neighbourhood of \(\sigma(\mathrm{S})\), we may assume X of finite presentation over S, then (EGA IV 8.9) S noetherian (note, in case b), that the formation of the conormal sheaf commutes with base extension (EGA IV 16.6.4) and that the rank of the fibres of J is a constructible function on S). This being so, for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\), denote by \(\mathrm{J}' = \mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) the conormal sheaf relative to the section \(\sigma_{\mathrm{S}'}\); let \(\mathrm{S}^\bullet(\mathrm{J}')\), canonically isomorphic to \(\mathrm{S}^\bullet(\mathrm{J})_{\mathrm{S}'}\), be the symmetric algebra of \(\mathrm{J}'\) over \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), \(\operatorname{Gr}_\bullet(\sigma_{\mathrm{S}'})\) the sheaf of graded \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}\)-algebras associated to \(\sigma_{\mathrm{S}'}\) (EGA IV 16), and for every integer \(n \geqslant 0\) let \(\sigma_{n,\mathrm{S}'} : \mathrm{S}^n(\mathrm{J}') \to \operatorname{Gr}_n(\sigma_{\mathrm{S}'})\) be the canonical epimorphism.

It follows from EGA IV 17.12.3 and from EGA \(0_{\text{IV}}\) 19.5.4 that, for \(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) to be smooth along the section \(\sigma_{\mathrm{S}'}\) and of relative dimension \(r\) at the points of \(\sigma_{\mathrm{S}'}(\mathrm{S}')\), it is necessary and sufficient that:
i) \(\mathrm{J}'\) be a locally free \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}\)-sheaf of rank \(r\).
ii) For every integer \(n \geqslant 1\), \(\sigma_{n,\mathrm{S}'}\) be an isomorphism.

Now it follows from TDTE IV, lemma 3.6, that "the functor which makes J locally free of rank \(r\)" is representable by a subprescheme \(\mathrm{S}_1\), closed in S in case b). Replacing S by \(\mathrm{S}_1\), we are reduced to the case where J is locally free of rank \(r\). Let us then proceed by induction on the integer \(n\). Suppose we have represented by a closed subprescheme \(\mathrm{S}_{n-1}\) of S the "functor which makes the morphisms \(\sigma_{q,\mathrm{S}'}\) injective for every integer \(q \leqslant n-1\)", and let us show that the "subfunctor5 which makes \(\sigma_{n,\mathrm{S}}\) injective" is representable by a closed subprescheme \(\mathrm{S}_n\) of \(\mathrm{S}_{n-1}\). Replacing S by \(\mathrm{S}_{n-1}\), we may assume that \(\sigma_{q,\mathrm{S}}\) is bijective for \(q \leqslant n-1\). But then the \((n-1)^{\text{th}}\) normal invariant \(\mathrm{X}^{(n-1)}\) relative to the section \(\sigma_s\) admits a composition series \(\mathrm{X}^{(0)}, \ldots, \mathrm{X}^{(n-1)}\) whose successive quotients \(\operatorname{Gr}_0(\sigma_s), \ldots, \operatorname{Gr}_{n-1}(\sigma_s)\) are locally free over \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), hence flat; consequently \(\mathrm{X}^{(n-1)}\) is flat over \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\). Since the formation of \(\mathrm{X}^{(n)}\) commutes with base extension (EGA IV 16), we have for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\): \[ \operatorname{Gr}_n(\sigma_{\mathrm{S}'}) = \operatorname{Ker} \mathrm{X}^{(n)}_{\mathrm{S}'} \to \mathrm{X}^{(n-1)}_{\mathrm{S}'} = (\operatorname{Gr}_n(\sigma))_{\mathrm{S}'} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_{n,\mathrm{S}'} = (\sigma_{n,\mathrm{S}})_{\mathrm{S}'}. \]

Hence the functor of interest to us is "the one which makes the morphism \(\sigma_{n,\mathrm{S}}\) injective". This functor is of a local nature on S, which allows us to assume S affine and \(\mathrm{S}^n(\mathrm{J})\) free over \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\). But it is then clear that the functor in question is representable by the closed subscheme of S defined by the ideal generated by the coordinates of \(\operatorname{Ker} \sigma_{n,\mathrm{S}}\) with respect to a basis of \(\mathrm{S}^n(\mathrm{J})\).

Since S is noetherian, the decreasing sequence \(\{\mathrm{S}_n\}\) of closed subpreschemes of \(\mathrm{S}_1\) is stationary, and the stationary value represents the functor L, which completes the proof of 5.4.

Let us return to the question of the representability of \(\mathscr{L}^n\). Replacing S by a suitable subprescheme \(\mathrm{S}_1\), we may thus assume N smooth along the unit section and of relative dimension \(r\). The functor \(\mathscr{L}^n\) is then the "functor of coincidences" of two sections of \(\mathrm{P}_n\) over S, \(h\) and \(g\), corresponding to the sheaves Q and \(\mathrm{N}^{(n)}\) (condition b) occurring in the definition of the functor \(\mathscr{F}_2\) above). It is therefore representable by the closed subprescheme of S that is the inverse image of the diagonal of \(\mathrm{P}_n \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{P}_n\) under the morphism of finite presentation \(h \times_\mathrm{S} g\). This completes the proof of 5.3.
Study of the transition morphisms \(\mathscr{L}^n \to \mathscr{L}^m\).

If a subgroup H of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) belongs to \(\mathscr{L}^n(\mathrm{S}')\), it belongs a fortiori to \(\mathscr{L}^m(\mathrm{S}')\) for every \(m \geqslant n\), whence natural monomorphisms: \[ u^m_n : \mathscr{L}^n \longrightarrow \mathscr{L}^m \quad \text{for} \quad m \geqslant n. \]

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5

The morphism \(u^m_n\) is an open immersion.

After changing S, we are reduced to the following problem: Let \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{L}^m(\mathrm{S})\), \[ \mathrm{N} = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H}) = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H}^{(m)}), \qquad \mathrm{N}' = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H}^{(n)}) \] and let \(\mathscr{D} : (\mathbf{Sch}/\mathrm{S})^\circ \to (\mathbf{Ens})\) be the functor of coincidences of N and \(\mathrm{N}'\), defined by \(\mathscr{D}(\mathrm{S}') = h_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{S}')\) if \(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'} = \mathrm{N}'_{\mathrm{S}'}\), and \(\mathscr{D}(\mathrm{S}') = \varnothing\) otherwise. We must show that \(\mathscr{D} \to \mathrm{S}\) is an open immersion. Now I claim that \(\mathscr{D}\) is also the subfunctor of S which "makes the immersion \(\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{N}'\) open". Indeed, if \(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'} = \mathrm{N}'_{\mathrm{S}'}\), then \(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'} \to \mathrm{N}'_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is indeed an open immersion since this is the case for \(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'} \to \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) (prop. 5.1). Conversely, if \(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'} \to \mathrm{N}'_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is an open immersion, H having connected fibres, \(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is the identity component of \(\mathrm{N}'_{\mathrm{S}'}\) (Exp. VIB 3.10) and consequently is normal in \(\mathrm{N}'_{\mathrm{S}'}\), hence \(\mathrm{N}'_{\mathrm{S}'} \subset \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'}\). Since in any case \(\mathrm{N}'\) dominates N, we indeed have \(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}'} = \mathrm{N}'_{\mathrm{S}'}\). The group preschemes H and \(\mathrm{N}'\) are of finite presentation over S and H is flat over S; the fact that \(\mathscr{D} \to \mathrm{S}\) is an open immersion then follows from Exp. VIB 2.6.
End of the proof of 5.2.

The functors \(\mathscr{L}^n\) being representable and the transition morphisms \(u^m_n\) being compatible with one another and representable by open immersions, there exists an S-prescheme X, a union of an increasing sequence of open sets \((\mathrm{X}_i)_{i \in \mathbb{N}}\), such that \(\mathrm{X}_i\) represents the functor \(\mathscr{L}^i\) and such that, if one identifies \(\mathscr{L}^i\) with \(\mathrm{X}_i\), the inclusion \(\mathrm{X}_i \to \mathrm{X}_j\) \((j \geqslant i)\) is identified with \(u^i_j\). To conclude that X represents the functor \(\mathscr{L}\), it then suffices to remark that, in the category of sheaves on \(\mathbf{Sch}/\mathrm{S}\) equipped with the Zariski topology (Exp. IV 6.1), we have: \[ \mathrm{X} = \varinjlim \mathrm{X}_i \qquad \text{and} \qquad \mathscr{L} = \varinjlim \mathscr{L}^i. \]

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 5.6

With the preceding notation, suppose moreover that S has all its residue characteristics zero; then for every integer \(r \geqslant 0\), the functor \(\mathscr{L}_r\) is equal to \(\mathscr{L}^1_r\), hence is representable by an S-prescheme of finite presentation and quasi-projective over S. Indeed, it suffices to show that if \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{L}_r(\mathrm{S}')\), the canonical immersion: \[ \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow \mathrm{N} = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H}^{(1)}) \] is an open immersion (for this entails \(\mathrm{N} = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H})\), hence \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{L}^1_r(\mathrm{S}')\)). Since H is flat over S, and H and N of finite presentation over S, it suffices (Exp. VIB 2.6) to show that for every point \(s\) of S, \(\mathrm{H}_s \to \mathrm{N}_s\) is an open immersion. Now it follows easily6 from Cartier's theorem (Exp. VIB 1.6) that if G is an algebraic group over a field of characteristic 0 and H is a connected algebraic subgroup, we have: \[ \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H}) = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}) = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H}^{(1)}). \] On the other hand, if S has non-zero residue characteristics, the subfunctors \(\mathscr{L}^n_r\) of \(\mathscr{L}_r\) may form a strictly increasing sequence (even when S is quasi-compact), and in that case \(\mathscr{L}_r\) is not representable by an S-prescheme quasi-compact over S. Take for example the algebraic group G, defined over a field \(k\) of characteristic \(p > 0\), equal to the semi-direct product of the torus \(\mathrm{T} = \mathbb{G}_m \times \mathbb{G}_m\) with the unipotent group \(\mathrm{U} = \mathbb{G}_a \times \mathbb{G}_a\), the action of T on U being defined by \((t, t', u, u') \to (tu, t'u')\). For every integer \(n > 0\), consider then the smooth connected subgroup \(\mathrm{U}_n\) of U with equation \(u' = u^{p^n}\), and the subtorus \(\mathrm{T}_n\) of T with equation \(t' = t^{p^n}\). It is immediate to verify that \(\mathrm{T}_n\) acts on \(\mathrm{U}_n\) and that the subgroup \(\mathrm{G}_n\) of G, equal to \(\mathrm{T}_n \cdot \mathrm{U}_n\), is smooth, connected and identical to its normalizer in G. Now all the groups \(\mathrm{G}_n\), for \(n \geqslant m\), are distinct and have the same infinitesimal neighbourhood of order \(p^m\).

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 5.7

There exists on \(\mathscr{L}\) a canonical invertible sheaf L whose restriction to every open subset U of \(\mathscr{L}\), quasi-compact over S, is S-ample. Indeed, consider the subgroup prescheme H of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathscr{L}\), smooth over \(\mathscr{L}\), with connected fibres, equal to its connected normalizer, and which is universal for these properties. I claim that one may take for L the sheaf \((\det(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}))^{-1}\) (recall that if F is a sheaf of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-modules on a prescheme S which is locally free of finite rank, \(\det(\mathrm{F})\) denotes the invertible \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-module whose restriction to the open and closed subprescheme \(\mathrm{S}_r\) of S \((r \geqslant 0)\) where F is of rank \(r\), equals \(\bigwedge^r(\mathrm{F})\)). We keep the notation of the proof of 5.2. To prove the assertion made about L, we may restrict ourselves to the functor \(\mathscr{L}^n_r\) and prove that \(\mathrm{L}|_{\mathscr{L}^n_r}\) is S-ample. Consider the canonical immersion \(v_n : \mathscr{L}^n_r \to \mathrm{P}_n\), and let Q be the locally free sheaf on \(\mathrm{P}_n\), universal for the Grassmannian \(\mathrm{P}_n\). By construction, we have \(v^*_n(\mathrm{Q}) = \mathrm{H}^{(n)}\) (where now H denotes the subgroup prescheme of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{L}^n_r}\), universal for the functor \(\mathscr{L}^n_r\)). Now \(\det(\mathrm{Q})\) is the canonical ample sheaf on \(\mathrm{P}_n\) (EGA I 2nd ed., 9.7), so \(\det \mathrm{H}^{(n)}\) is ample relative to \(\mathscr{L}^n_r\) (EGA II 4.6.13 i) bis). Denote again by J the conormal sheaf, equal to \(\mathrm{H}^{(1)}\), and by \(\mathrm{S}^q(\mathrm{J})\) the homogeneous part of degree \(q\) of the symmetric algebra of J over \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathscr{L}^n_r}\). Since H is smooth over \(\mathscr{L}^n_r\), it is immediate that we have a canonical isomorphism: \[ \det \mathrm{H}^{(n)} \simeq \prod_{1 \leqslant q \leqslant n} \det \mathrm{S}^q(\mathrm{J}). \] On the other hand, one shows that for every locally free sheaf J of rank \(r\) and every integer \(q > 0\), there exists a canonical isomorphism: \[ \det \mathrm{S}^q(\mathrm{J}) \simeq (\det \mathrm{J})^{\otimes s}, \] where \(s > 0\) is an integer depending only on \(r\) and \(q\). Finally, we obtain \(\det \mathrm{H}^{(n)} \simeq (\det \mathrm{J})^{\otimes s}\) for a suitable integer \(s > 0\), hence (EGA II 4.5.6), \(\det \mathrm{J} = (\det \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H})^{-1}\) is indeed S-ample.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 5.8

Let S be a prescheme, G and H two S-group preschemes of finite presentation over S, \(i : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) an S-homomorphism of groups which is a monomorphism. If H is smooth over S, with connected fibres, we know (Exp. XI 6.11) that \(\mathrm{N} = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H})\) is representable by a closed subgroup prescheme of G, of finite presentation over S. Suppose moreover that N is smooth along the unit section and has the same relative dimension over S as H. The identity component \(\mathrm{N}^0\) of N is then representable by an open subgroup prescheme of N, smooth over S (Exp. VIB 3.10). The monomorphism \(i\) obviously factors through \(\mathrm{N}^0\). In fact we have \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{N}^0\). Indeed, for every point \(s\) of S, we have \(\mathrm{H}_s = (\mathrm{N}^0)_s\), these two algebraic groups being connected, smooth, and of the same dimension. Since H is flat over S, one deduces that \(\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{N}^0\) is an isomorphism (EGA IV 17.9.5). Finally, H is a subgroup prescheme of G. We have thus shown that the functor \(\mathscr{L}\) introduced in this section is identical to the functor of subgroups H of G, smooth over S, with connected fibres and equal to their connected normalizer.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: "functor" has been replaced by "subfunctor". Should one write "which makes \(\sigma_{q,\mathrm{S}}\) injective for \(q \leqslant n\)"?
N.D.E.: One may apply, for example, proposition II.6.1 of the book by M. Demazure and P. Gabriel, Groupes algébriques I, Masson & North Holland (1970).

pages.title: 6. Functor of Cartan subgroups and functor of parabolic subgroups

blocks.html: 6. Functor of Cartan subgroups and functor of parabolic subgroups

When G is a smooth connected algebraic group defined over an algebraically closed field \(k\), one has defined the subtori of G, the maximal subtori, the Cartan subgroups (Exp. XII 1), the Borel subgroups (Exp. XIV 4.1), the parabolic subgroups (Exp. XIV 4.8 bis). We extend these notions to the case of a group prescheme over an arbitrary base, in the following way:

blocks.title: Définition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.1

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S, H an S-group prescheme, \(i : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) an S-monomorphism making H a subgroup of G. We shall say that H is a subtorus of G (resp. a maximal subtorus of G, a Cartan subgroup, a Borel subgroup, a parabolic subgroup) if:

i) H is smooth over S.

ii) For every geometric point \(\bar{s}\) over S, \(\mathrm{H}_{\bar{s}}\) is a subtorus of \((\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}})^0_{\text{réd}}\) (resp. a maximal subtorus, a Cartan subgroup, a Borel subgroup, a parabolic subgroup).

blocks.label: 6.1 bis
blocks.title: Remarques
blocks.html:
Remarks 6.1 bis

a) If the S-group H is a subtorus of G (resp. …), its fibres are connected, and consequently H is of finite presentation over S (Exp. VIB 5.3.3).

b) If H is a subtorus of G, then H is a torus in the sense of Exp. IX, as follows immediately from Exp. X 8.1. Moreover the monomorphism \(i : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) is an immersion (cf. 8.3 below).

c) If G is smooth over S, with connected fibres, and if H is a Cartan subgroup of G (resp. a Borel subgroup, a parabolic subgroup), the monomorphism \(i : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) is an immersion, so that our definitions coincide with those introduced in Exp. XII and Exp. XIV. Indeed, H is then identical to its connected normalizer (by XII 6.6 c), XIV 4.8 and 4.8 bis) and it suffices to apply 5.8.

blocks.label: 6.1 ter
blocks.title: Définition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.1 ter

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme locally of finite type, \(s\) a point of S. We call the nilpotent rank of G at the point \(s\), denoted \(\rho_n(s)\), the dimension of the Cartan subgroups of \((\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}})^0_{\text{réd}}\). One defines analogously the reductive rank \(\rho_r(s)\), the unipotent rank \(\rho_u(s)\), the abelian rank \(\rho_{ab}(s)\) (cf. Exp. X 8.7).

If now G is a smooth connected algebraic group defined over an algebraically closed field \(k\), recall that the radical of G, denoted \(\operatorname{rad}(\mathrm{G})\), is the largest algebraic subgroup of G which is normal, smooth, connected and solvable; \(\mathrm{G}/\operatorname{rad}(\mathrm{G})\) is then semi-simple (use Exp. XII 6.1 to reduce to the case of G affine). If G is moreover affine, one defines the unipotent radical \(\operatorname{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})\) of G as the largest algebraic subgroup of G which is normal, smooth, connected and unipotent: \(\mathrm{G}/\operatorname{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})\) is then reductive.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.2

Let S be a prescheme, G and H two S-group preschemes of finite presentation over S, \(i : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) an S-monomorphism of groups making H a subgroup of G, and let \(\mathrm{P}(s)\) be one of the following properties concerning the point \(s\) of S:

i) \((\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}})^0_{\text{réd}}\) is an abelian variety (resp. is affine, is a torus, is unipotent).

ii) \((\mathrm{H}_{\bar{s}})^0_{\text{réd}}\) is a maximal torus of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}}\).

iii) \((\mathrm{H}_{\bar{s}})^0_{\text{réd}}\) is the centralizer in \((\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}})^0_{\text{réd}}\) of a torus of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}}\) (resp. is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}}\)).

iv) \((\mathrm{H}_{\bar{s}})^0_{\text{réd}}\) is a Borel subgroup (resp. a parabolic subgroup) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}}\).

v) \((\mathrm{H}_{\bar{s}})^0_{\text{réd}}\) is the radical of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}}\) (resp. \((\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}})^0_{\text{réd}}\) is semi-simple).

vi) \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}}\) is affine and \((\mathrm{H}_{\bar{s}})^0_{\text{red}}\) is the unipotent radical of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}}\) (resp. \((\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}})^0_{\text{réd}}\) is reductive).

Then the set E of points \(s\) of S such that \(\mathrm{P}(s)\) is true is locally constructible (EGA \(0_{\text{III}}\) 9.1.11).

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 6.2.1

This proposition complements Exp. VIB §10. Moreover, one can further specify the structure of E, using semicontinuity theorems (cf. Exp. X 8.7); we shall see an example of this a little further on.
Proof of 6.2.

Note that if S is the spectrum of a field, E is invariant under extension of that field. A standard reduction (EGA IV 9) then allows us to reduce to the case where S is noetherian and integral, with generic point \(\eta\). We must show that E, or \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{S}) \setminus \mathrm{E}\), contains a neighbourhood of \(\eta\) (EGA IV 9.2.1). We may assume S affine with ring A and fraction field K. If L is a finite extension of K, it is immediate that there exists a sub-A-algebra B of L, finite over A, having L as fraction field. The canonical morphism \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\), where \(\mathrm{S}' = \operatorname{Spec} \mathrm{B}\), is dominant, of finite presentation, so the image of a non-empty open subset of \(\mathrm{S}'\) contains a non-empty open subset of S (EGA IV 1.8.4). From the point of view of interest to us, we may therefore replace S by \(\mathrm{S}'\), hence replace K by a finite extension L. Thus we may choose L so that \((\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{L})_{\text{réd}}\) and \((\mathrm{H}_\mathrm{L})_{\text{réd}}\) are smooth over L (EGA IV 4.6.6). After restricting \(\mathrm{S}'\) if necessary, we may assume that \(\mathrm{G}_{\text{réd}}\) and \(\mathrm{H}_{\text{réd}}\) are group preschemes smooth over S (Exp. VIB §10 and EGA IV 17). In view of the properties to be proved, we may replace G and H by their reduced identity components (Exp. VIB 10.9), hence assume G and H smooth over S, with connected fibres. Finally, we may assume that H is a closed subgroup prescheme of G (Exp. VIB 10.4).

Proof of i). After making a finite extension of K if necessary, we may assume that \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\) possesses a "Chevalley decomposition", i.e. is an extension of an abelian variety \(\mathrm{D}_\eta\) by a linear algebraic group \(\mathrm{F}_\eta\), smooth and connected (Séminaire Bourbaki 1956/57 No. 145). By Exp. VIB 10.16, there exists an open neighbourhood U of \(\eta\) such that this generic extension comes from an extension: \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{F} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}|_\mathrm{U} \longrightarrow \mathrm{D} \longrightarrow 1. \] We may moreover assume F and D smooth over U, with connected fibres, F affine over U and D proper over U (EGA IV 8, 9 and 17). For every point \(s\) of U, \((\mathrm{D}_s, \mathrm{F}_s)\) is then the "Chevalley decomposition" of \(\mathrm{G}_s\). Consequently, \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is an abelian variety (resp. is affine) if and only if \(\mathrm{F}_s\) (resp. \(\mathrm{D}_s\)) is the unit group, which is a constructible property (EGA IV 9.2.6.1).

To establish the last two assertions of i), we may, in view of the above, assume G affine over S. Let \(q\) be a prime number invertible on S and \({}_q\mathrm{G}\) the "kernel" of raising to the \(q^{\text{th}}\) power in G. It follows easily from the structure of affine algebraic groups that \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is a torus (resp. is unipotent) if and only if a) \({}_q\mathrm{G}_s\) is quasi-finite, which is a constructible property (EGA IV 9.3.2), and b) \({}_q\mathrm{G}_s\) has \(r^q\) geometric points, where \(r\) denotes the relative dimension of G over S (resp. \({}_q\mathrm{G}_s\) has a single point). Now the function \(s \mapsto\) (number of geometric points of \({}_q\mathrm{G}_s\)) is constructible (EGA IV 9.7.9). This completes the proof of (i).

Proof of iii). a) Case of a centralizer of a torus. Suppose that \(\mathrm{H}_\eta = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_\eta}(\mathrm{T}_\eta)\), where \(\mathrm{T}_\eta\) is a torus of \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\), and let us show that \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is the centralizer of a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) for \(s\) in a neighbourhood of \(\eta\). By i), after restricting S if necessary, we may assume that \(\mathrm{T}_\eta\) comes from a subtorus T of G. But then \(\mathrm{Z} = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})\) is representable (Exp. XI 6.11) by a subgroup prescheme of G. Since H and Z coincide generically, they coincide above a neighbourhood of \(\eta\). This proves that the set E of points \(s\) of S such that \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is the centralizer in \(\mathrm{G}_s\) of a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is ind-constructible (EGA IV 1), and this result will suffice for us to establish, in lemma 6.6 below, that E is an open part of S; a fortiori, E will indeed be a locally constructible part of S.

iii) b) Case of a Cartan subgroup. Suppose that \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\), and let us show that \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) at every point \(s\) in a neighbourhood of \(\eta\). The group \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) is the centralizer in \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\) of a torus of \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\) and is nilpotent (Exp. XII 6.6). By a) and Exp. VIB 8.4, \(\mathrm{H}_s\) has the same properties at every point \(s\) of a neighbourhood U of \(\eta\). For every point \(s\) of U, the group \(\mathrm{H}_s\) thus has the same reductive rank as \(\mathrm{G}_s\) and its unique maximal torus is central (Exp. XII 6.7); it is therefore the centralizer of a maximal torus of \(\mathrm{G}_s\), i.e. a Cartan group of \(\mathrm{G}_s\).

Suppose now that H is not a Cartan subgroup of G, and let us show that \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is not a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) for \(s\) in a neighbourhood U of \(\eta\). Taking into account the assertion provisionally granted in (a) above, we may restrict ourselves to the case where H is the centralizer in G of a subtorus T. But then \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) contains a Cartan subgroup \(\mathrm{C}_\eta\) of \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\). We have just seen that, after restricting S if necessary, \(\mathrm{C}_\eta\) extends to a Cartan subgroup C of G, which we may assume contained in H. By hypothesis \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) strictly dominates \(\mathrm{C}_\eta\), so \(\mathrm{H}_s\) strictly dominates \(\mathrm{C}_s\) for \(s\) in a neighbourhood of \(\eta\) (EGA IV 9.5.2); a fortiori, \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is not a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) for \(s\) in U.

Proof of ii). Suppose that \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) is a maximal torus of \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\), and let \(\mathrm{C}_\eta\) be its centralizer in \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\). By i) and iii), H is a torus above a neighbourhood U of \(\eta\), and \(\mathrm{C} = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}|_\mathrm{U}}(\mathrm{H}|_\mathrm{U})\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}|_\mathrm{U}\). To prove that H is a maximal torus of G on a neighbourhood of \(\eta\), we may then replace G by C, then by the linear component F of a Chevalley decomposition of C (cf. i)). Let \(q\) be an integer invertible on S, \({}_q\mathrm{F}\) the kernel of raising to the \(q^{\text{th}}\) power in F. Since \(\mathrm{F}_s\) is affine, nilpotent, smooth and connected, \(\mathrm{F}_{\bar{s}}\) is the direct product of its maximal torus \(\mathrm{T}_s\) with a unipotent group (BIBLE 6-04), so \({}_q\mathrm{F}_s = {}_q\mathrm{T}_s\). Since \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) is a maximal torus, \({}_q\mathrm{H}_\eta = {}_q\mathrm{F}_\eta\), and consequently \({}_q\mathrm{H} = {}_q\mathrm{F}\) above a neighbourhood V of \(\eta\). For every point \(s\) of V, \({}_q\mathrm{H}_s = {}_q\mathrm{T}_s\), so \(\mathrm{H}_s = \mathrm{T}_s\) is a maximal torus.

Suppose now that \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) is not a maximal torus of \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\). By i), we may restrict ourselves to the case where \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) is a torus, then assume that it is contained in a strictly larger torus \(\mathrm{T}_\eta\). The latter extends to a torus T which strictly dominates H on a neighbourhood U of \(\eta\). A fortiori, \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is not a maximal torus for \(s \in \mathrm{U}\).

Proof of iv). After restricting S if necessary, we may assume that the centre Z of G is representable (Exp. VIB 10.11) and flat over S, as well as the quotient \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\) (loc. cit.). The property "\(\mathrm{H}_s\) dominates \(\mathrm{Z}_s\)" is constructible (EGA IV 9.5.2), and every parabolic subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) contains \(\mathrm{Z}_s\) (Exp. XIV 4.9 a)); this allows us to replace G by \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\), hence to assume G affine over S (Exp. XII 6.1 and i)). We may further assume that \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}\) is representable; but then \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is a parabolic subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) if and only if \((\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H})_s\) is proper (BIBLE 6. Th.4 b)), which is an ind-constructible property (EGA IV 9.3.5). So E is ind-constructible, and this will suffice for us to prove that E is open (Lemma 6.6), hence locally constructible.

Let us examine the case of Borel subgroups. If \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) is a Borel subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\), i.e. a solvable parabolic subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\), the above and Exp. VIB 8.4 imply that these properties still hold at every point \(s\) of a neighbourhood of \(\eta\). If now \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) is not a Borel subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\), to prove that the same holds at the points \(s\) of a neighbourhood of \(\eta\), we may restrict ourselves (in view of the above) to the case where \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) is a parabolic subgroup, then assume that \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) contains a Borel subgroup \(\mathrm{B}_\eta\). We have just shown that the latter extends to a Borel subgroup B of G on a neighbourhood U of \(\eta\). Since \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) strictly dominates \(\mathrm{B}_\eta\), \(\mathrm{H}_s\) strictly dominates \(\mathrm{B}_s\) at every point of an open set V, and \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is not a Borel subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) for \(s \in \mathrm{V}\).

Proof of v). Suppose that \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) is the radical of \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\). The group \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) is thus normal in \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\), solvable (smooth and connected); the same therefore holds for \(\mathrm{H}_s\) for \(s\) belonging to a neighbourhood U of \(\eta\) (Exp. VIB 8 and 10), so, for \(s \in \mathrm{U}\), \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is contained in the radical of \(\mathrm{G}_s\). Replacing G by \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}\) (Exp. VIB 10), we must prove that if \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\) is semi-simple, then \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is semi-simple at every point of a neighbourhood V of \(\eta\). Thanks to i) and ii), we may assume that G is affine over S and possesses a maximal torus T. Let W be the Weyl group of T (Exp. XII 2), which is quasi-finite and étale over S, hence finite and étale on an open set V. It then follows from the elementary properties of roots (Exp. XIX 1.12) that G is semi-simple above V.

Suppose now that \(\mathrm{H}_\eta\) is not the radical of \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\). After replacing K by a finite extension L if necessary, we may assume that \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\) possesses a radical \(\mathrm{R}_\eta\). By the above, \(\mathrm{R}_\eta\) extends to a subgroup prescheme R of \(\mathrm{G}|_\mathrm{U}\) such that for every \(s \in \mathrm{U}\), \(\mathrm{R}_s\) is the radical of \(\mathrm{G}_s\). By hypothesis, \(\mathrm{R}_\eta \neq \mathrm{H}_\eta\). Hence \(\mathrm{R}_s \neq \mathrm{H}_s\) for \(s \in \mathrm{V}\). It remains to prove that if \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\) is not semi-simple, the same holds for \(\mathrm{G}_s\) at nearby points, but this is a special case of the above (take H = unit group).
Proof of vi). The proof is entirely analogous to that of v), taking i) into account, and is left to the reader.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.3

Let \(\mathrm{S}_0\) be a quasi-compact prescheme, \(\mathrm{S}_i\) \((i \in \mathrm{L})\) a projective system of \(\mathrm{S}_0\)-preschemas, affine over \(\mathrm{S}_0\), \(\mathrm{S} = \varprojlim \mathrm{S}_i\) (EGA IV 8.2), \(\mathrm{G}_0\) a group prescheme of finite presentation over \(\mathrm{S}_0\), \(\mathrm{G}_i = \mathrm{G}_0 \times_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathrm{S}_i\), \(\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{G}_0 \times_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathrm{S}\), H a subgroup of G. Then if H is a subtorus of G (resp. a maximal subtorus, a Cartan subgroup, a Borel subgroup, a parabolic subgroup), there exist an index \(i \in \mathrm{L}\) and a subgroup \(\mathrm{H}_i\) of \(\mathrm{G}_i\) such that \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{H}_i \times_{\mathrm{S}_i} \mathrm{S}\) and such that \(\mathrm{H}_i\) is a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_i\) (resp. …).

Indeed, H is smooth, with connected fibres, hence of finite presentation over S (Exp. VIB 5.3.3). By (Exp. VIB §10) there exist an \(i \in \mathrm{L}\) and a subgroup \(\mathrm{H}_i\) of \(\mathrm{G}_i\), smooth over S, such that \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{H}_i \times_{\mathrm{S}_i} \mathrm{S}\). Corollary 6.3 then follows from definition 6.1, from 6.2 and from EGA IV 9.3.3.

blocks.label: 6.3 bis
blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.3 bis

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S. Then the functions \(\rho_n\), \(\rho_r\), \(\rho_u\), \(\rho_{ab}\) (cf. 6.1 ter) are locally constructible functions on S.

It suffices for us to show (EGA IV 9.) that if S is an integral noetherian scheme with generic point \(\eta\), the functions in play are constant on a neighbourhood of \(\eta\). After replacing S by a scheme \(\mathrm{S}'\), finite over S, dominant, we may assume that \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\) possesses a Cartan subgroup \(\mathrm{C}_\eta\) having a Chevalley decomposition \(1 \to \mathrm{L}_\eta \to \mathrm{C}_\eta \to \mathrm{A}_\eta \to 1\). The argument made in 6.2 i) proves that this decomposition extends to a Chevalley decomposition on a neighbourhood of \(\eta\): \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{L} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C} \longrightarrow \mathrm{A} \longrightarrow 1. \] Moreover, we may assume that C is a Cartan subgroup of G (6.3) and that the maximal torus \(\mathrm{T}_\eta\) of \(\mathrm{L}_\eta\) extends to a maximal torus T of L (6.3). The corollary follows immediately from this and from the definitions.

6.4.0. Now let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S, \(\mathscr{L}\) the functor of subgroups of G, smooth, with connected fibres and equal to their connected normalizer (cf. §5); \(\mathscr{L}\) is representable (5.2 and 5.8). The remainder of this section is devoted to the study of certain subfunctors of \(\mathscr{L}\). More precisely, let us introduce the subfunctors of \(\mathscr{L}\), denoted \(\mathscr{LC}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{CT}\), resp. \(\mathscr{P}\)), defined in the following way: for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\), \(\mathscr{LC}(\mathrm{S}')\) (resp. \(\mathscr{CT}(\mathrm{S}')\), resp. \(\mathscr{P}(\mathrm{S}')\)) is the set of subgroups H of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), smooth over \(\mathrm{S}'\), with connected fibres, equal to their connected normalizer and such that for every point \(s\) of \(\mathrm{S}'\), \(\mathrm{H}_{\bar{s}}\) contains a Cartan subgroup (6.1) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}}\) (resp. is the centralizer in \((\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}})^0_{\text{réd}}\) of a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}}\), resp. is a parabolic subgroup (6.1) of \(\mathrm{G}_s\)).

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.4

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S; then the S-functors \(\mathscr{LC}\), \(\mathscr{CT}\), \(\mathscr{P}\) above (6.4.0) are representable by S-preschemas of finite presentation over S, quasi-projective over S.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 6.5

If G has smooth fibres, for example if G is smooth over S, or if the residue characteristics of S are zero (Exp. VIB 1.6.1), every subgroup H of G, smooth over S, with connected fibres, such that for every point \(s\) of S, \(\mathrm{H}_s\) contains a Cartan subgroup of \((\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}})^0\), is necessarily equal to its connected normalizer (and consequently is an element of \(\mathscr{LC}(\mathrm{S})\)). Indeed, thanks to 5.1 iii), we may assume that S is the spectrum of a field, in which case the property was pointed out at the end of the statement of Exp. XIII 2.1.

Note that we have natural monomorphisms: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{CT} \ar[dr] & \\ & \mathscr{LC} \ar[r] & \mathscr{L} \\ \mathscr{P} \ar[ur] & } \]

Let us show that these monomorphisms are open immersions of finite presentation (which will already prove, taking 5.2 into account, that \(\mathscr{LC}\), \(\mathscr{CT}\), \(\mathscr{P}\) are representable by S-preschemas, unions of an increasing sequence of open subpreschemas, quasi-projective and of finite presentation over S). Now this will follow, by the usual technique, from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.6

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite presentation, H a subgroup of G, smooth with connected fibres. Then the set of points \(s\) of S such that \(\mathrm{H}_{\bar{s}}\) contains a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}}\) (resp. is equal to the centralizer, in \((\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}})^0_{\text{réd}}\), of a torus of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}}\)) is an open subset of S. If moreover H is equal to its connected normalizer∗, the set of points \(s\) of S such that \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is a parabolic subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is also an open subset of S.

The assertion to be proved is local on S, which allows us to assume S affine, then, by EGA IV 8.1 and 6.3, S noetherian. Denote by E the set of points of S having the property in question. It follows from the assertions of 6.2 actually proved that E is ind-constructible. But S is noetherian, so to prove that E is open, it then suffices to show that E is stable under generizations (EGA IV 1.10.1). Using EGA II 7.1.9, we are finally led to prove that if S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, and if the closed point \(s\) belongs to E, then so does the generic point \(t\). We shall need the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.7

Let A be a complete noetherian local ring, \(\mathrm{S} = \operatorname{Spec} \mathrm{A}\), \(s\) the closed point of S, H an S-group prescheme, smooth with connected fibres, \(\mathrm{T}_s\) a subtorus of \(\mathrm{H}_s\). Then:

i) There exists a closed subgroup prescheme C of H, smooth, with connected fibres, such that \(\mathrm{C}_s = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{H}_s}(\mathrm{T}_s)\).

ii) For every point \(t\) of S, \(\mathrm{C}_t\) is the centralizer in \(\mathrm{H}_t\) of a subtorus \(\mathrm{T}_t\) of \(\mathrm{H}_t\).

Proof of 6.7. Let \(\mathrm{T}'\) be an S-torus such that there exists an isomorphism \(u_0 : \mathrm{T}'_s \to \mathrm{T}_s\) (Exp. X 4.6).
