Let \(\mathfrak{m}\) be the maximal ideal of A, \(\mathrm{A}_n = \mathrm{A}/\mathfrak{m}^n\), \(\mathrm{S}_n = \operatorname{Spec} \mathrm{A}_n\), \(\mathrm{H}_n = \mathrm{H} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_n\), etc. Since H is smooth over S, for every integer \(n > 0\) there exists an \(\mathrm{S}_n\)-morphism of groups: \[ u_n : \mathrm{T}'_n \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_n \] lifting \(u_0\) (Exp. IX 3.6), and we may assume by induction on \(n\) that \(u_n\) lifts \(u_{n-1}\). On the other hand let \(q\) be a prime number invertible on S; for every integer \(\ell\) equal to a power of \(q\), denote by \({}_\ell u_n\) the restriction of \(u_n\) to the subgroup \({}_\ell\mathrm{T}'_n\). For \(\ell\) fixed and \(n\) variable, the morphisms \({}_\ell u_n\) form a projective system, hence come from a unique S-morphism of groups \({}_\ell u : {}_\ell\mathrm{T}' \to \mathrm{H}\) (1.6 a)). Since H is separated (Exp. VIB 5.2) and \(u_0\) is a monomorphism, \({}_\ell u\) is a monomorphism (Exp. IX 6.8) and even a closed immersion, since it is finite, \({}_\ell\mathrm{T}'\) being finite over S. Denote by \(\mathrm{M}(\ell)\) the image group. It is clear that the family of subgroups of multiplicative type \(\mathrm{M}(\ell)\) is coherent in the sense of 4.1. Let \(\mathrm{C}_\ell = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{H}(\mathrm{M}(\ell))\), which is representable by a subgroup prescheme (2.5), closed (H is separated), smooth over S (Exp. XI 2.4). The \(\mathrm{C}_\ell\) form a filtered, decreasing family of closed subschemes, hence stationary, H being noetherian. The stationary value is a subgroup C, smooth and closed, such that \(\mathrm{C}_s = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{H}_s}(\mathrm{T}_s)\) by the density theorem (Exp. IX 4.7). It remains for us to show that for every point \(t\) of S, \(\mathrm{C}_t\) is the centralizer in \(\mathrm{H}_t\) of a subtorus \(\mathrm{T}_t\) (which will imply that \(\mathrm{C}_t\) is connected). But this will follow from the following more precise lemma, applied to the family \(\mathrm{M}(\ell)_t\) of subgroups of \(\mathrm{H}_t\):

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.8

Let G be a connected algebraic group defined over a field \(k\), \(r\) an integer \(> 0\), \(q\) an integer prime to the characteristic7 of \(k\), \(\mathrm{M}(\ell)\) (\(\ell\) running over the powers of \(q\)) a coherent family of subgroups of multiplicative type of G, of type \((\mathbb{Z}/\ell\mathbb{Z})^r\) (cf. 4.6), M the algebraic subgroup of G generated by the \(\mathrm{M}(\ell)\) (loc. cit.), T the unique maximal torus of M (cf. 3.4). Then we have \[ \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T}) = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{M}) = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{M}(\ell)) \qquad \text{for } \ell \text{ large enough.} \]

The last equality is quite clear. To prove the first, let us introduce the centre Z of G, \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\), \(\mathrm{M}'\) (resp. \(\mathrm{T}'\)) the image of M (resp. T) in \(\mathrm{G}'\), K the inverse image of \(\mathrm{T}'\) in G (i.e. the algebraic subgroup of G generated by T and Z). It obviously suffices to prove that \(\mathrm{K} \supset \mathrm{M}\), hence that \(\mathrm{T}' = \mathrm{M}'\). Now, M is smooth and connected (4.6) and \(\mathrm{G}'\) is affine (Exp. XII 6.1), so \(\mathrm{M}'\) is the direct product of its maximal torus \(\mathrm{T}'\) (Exp. XII 6.6 d)) and a unipotent group (BIBLE 4 Th. 4) (we may assume \(k\) algebraically closed). The image of \(\mathrm{M}(\ell)\) in \(\mathrm{M}'\) is therefore necessarily contained in \(\mathrm{T}'\). Hence the inverse image of \(\mathrm{T}'\) in M dominates \(\mathrm{M}(\ell)\) for every \(\ell\), hence equals M. Consequently \(\mathrm{M}' = \mathrm{T}'\). This proves 6.8 and hence 6.7.

This being so, let us prove 6.6. We have reduced ourselves to the case where S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring A, which we may moreover assume complete with algebraically closed residue field. After replacing A by its normalization in a finite extension of its fraction field, we may assume that \((\mathrm{G}_t)_{\text{réd}}\) is smooth8. It is clear that, to prove 6.6, we may replace G by the identity component of the schematic closure in G of \((\mathrm{G}_t)^0_{\text{réd}}\), hence assume that G is flat over S, with connected fibres, and that \(\mathrm{G}_t\) is smooth.

a) Suppose that \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is the centralizer in \((\mathrm{G}_s)_{\text{réd}}\) of a torus \(\mathrm{T}_s\), and let us show that \(\mathrm{H}_t\) is then the centralizer in \(\mathrm{G}_t\) of a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_t\). By lemma 6.7, there exists a subgroup scheme C of H, smooth over S, whose closed fibre is \(\mathrm{H}_s\) and such that \(\mathrm{C}_t = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{H}_t}(\mathrm{T}_t)\), where \(\mathrm{T}_t\) is a subtorus of \(\mathrm{H}_t\). Since H is smooth over S, with connected fibres, one concludes, for dimension reasons, that \(\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{H}\). Keeping the notation of 6.7, we have \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{C} = \mathrm{C}_\ell\) for \(\ell\) large. Consider likewise \(\mathrm{C}'_\ell = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{M}(\ell))\) (2.5), and let \(\mathrm{C}'\) be the stationary value of \(\mathrm{C}'_\ell\) for \(\ell\) large (2.5 bis). The group scheme \(\mathrm{C}'\) dominates H and is such that \(\mathrm{C}'_t = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_t}(\mathrm{T}_t)\) (6.8) and \(\mathrm{C}'_s = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_s}(\mathrm{T}_s)\). The hypothesis made on \(\mathrm{H}_s\) implies \(\dim \mathrm{H}_s = \dim \mathrm{C}'_s\). Moreover, \(\dim \mathrm{H}_s = \dim \mathrm{H}_t\) (since H is smooth over S) and \(\dim \mathrm{H}_t \leqslant \dim \mathrm{C}'_t\) (Exp. VIB 4.1), so we have \(\dim \mathrm{H}_t = \dim \mathrm{C}'_t\). But \(\mathrm{G}_t\) being smooth and connected, \(\mathrm{C}'_t = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_t}(\mathrm{T}_t)\) is smooth and connected, so finally we have \(\mathrm{H}_t = \mathrm{C}'_t = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_t}(\mathrm{T}_t)\).

b) Suppose that \(\mathrm{H}_s\) contains a Cartan subgroup \(\mathrm{C}_s\) of \(\mathrm{G}_s\), i.e. the centralizer in \((\mathrm{G}_s)_{\text{réd}}\) of a maximal torus \(\mathrm{T}_s\) of \(\mathrm{G}_s\). By 6.7, there exists a subgroup scheme C of H, smooth over S, with connected fibres, which lifts \(\mathrm{C}_s\). It obviously suffices to prove that \(\mathrm{C}_t\) contains a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_t\). Now by a) applied with \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{C}\), \(\mathrm{C}_t\) is the centralizer in \(\mathrm{G}_t\) of a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_t\), hence contains a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_t\).

c) Suppose that \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is a parabolic subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\). Let \(\mathrm{N} = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H})\). By hypothesis, H is equal to its connected normalizer, so \(\mathrm{N}_s\) is smooth, and consequently is equal to \(\underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{(\mathrm{G}_s)_{\text{réd}}}(\mathrm{H}_s) = \mathrm{H}_s\) (Exp. XIV 4.8 bis). But then N is flat over S. We shall see in Exp. XVI that under these conditions, \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\) is representable. Since \(\mathrm{H}_s = \mathrm{N}_s\) is a parabolic subgroup of G, \((\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N})_s\) is proper. Since \((\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N})\) has connected fibres and is flat over S, it follows from EGA III 5.5.1 that \((\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N})\) is proper over S. Hence \((\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N})_t = \mathrm{G}_t/\mathrm{N}_t\) is proper over \(t\), and the same holds for \(\mathrm{G}_t/\mathrm{H}_t\), since \(\mathrm{N}_t/\mathrm{H}_t\) is finite. It then suffices to apply the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.9

Let \(k\) be a field, G a \(k\)-algebraic group, H a smooth connected algebraic subgroup of G, \(\mathrm{N} = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G} \mathrm{H}\); then if \(\dim \mathrm{H} = \dim \mathrm{N}\) and if \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}\) is proper, H is a parabolic subgroup of G.

Indeed, we may assume \(k\) algebraically closed and G smooth and connected. The centre Z of G is contained in N, and the hypothesis \(\dim \mathrm{H} = \dim \mathrm{N}\) implies that \(\mathrm{Z}' = (\mathrm{Z})^0_{\text{réd}}\) is contained in H, so \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}'\) is affine (Exp. XIV 4.9). Replacing G by \(\mathrm{G}'\), and H by its image in \(\mathrm{G}'\), we are reduced to the case where G is affine (Exp. XIV 4.9), and lemma 6.9 then follows from BIBLE 6 Th. 4. We have thus proved lemma 6.6.

To finish proving 6.4, we must prove that the S-preschemas representing \(\mathscr{LC}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{CT}\), resp. \(\mathscr{P}\)) are of finite presentation over S. This assertion is local on S, which allows us to assume S affine, then, G being of finite presentation, S noetherian (EGA IV 8.9). We have just seen that the natural inclusions \(\mathscr{CT} \to \mathscr{L}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{P} \to \mathscr{L}\)) are immersions; the same therefore holds for the inclusions \(\mathscr{CT} \to \mathscr{LC}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{P} \to \mathscr{LC}\)), and consequently it suffices to prove that \(\mathscr{LC}\) is representable by an S-prescheme of finite presentation.

Let us take up again the notation introduced in 5.2. For every integer \(n \geqslant 0\), let \(\mathscr{L}^n\) then be the subfunctor of \(\mathscr{L}\) such that: \[ \mathscr{L}^n(\mathrm{S}') = \{\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{L}(\mathrm{S}') \text{ such that } \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H}) = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H}^{(n)})\}. \] The S-functor \(\mathscr{L}^n\) is thus representable by an open subprescheme of \(\mathscr{L}\), the sum of the \(\mathscr{L}^n_r\). Each \(\mathscr{L}^n_r\) is of finite presentation over S (5.3) and is empty for \(r > \operatorname{Sup}_{s \in \mathrm{S}} \dim \mathrm{G}_s\) (which is a finite number, S being quasi-compact), so \(\mathscr{L}^n\) is of finite presentation over S. It suffices to prove that \(\mathscr{LC}\) is contained in \(\mathscr{L}^n\) for \(n\) large enough.

For every point \(s\) of S, let \(d(s)\) be the smallest integer \(n\) (finite or infinite) such that \(\mathscr{LC}_{\mathrm{G}_s} \subset \mathscr{L}^n_{\mathrm{G}_s}\). It suffices to show that the function \(d\) is bounded on S, for if M is an upper bound, \(\mathscr{LC}\) will be set-theoretically contained in \(\mathscr{L}^\mathrm{M}\), hence \(\mathscr{LC}\) will be contained in \(\mathscr{L}^\mathrm{M}\), since the latter is an open subset of \(\mathscr{L}\). An immediate constructibility argument reduces us to proving that if S is noetherian and integral with generic point \(t\), then the function \(d\) is bounded on a neighbourhood of \(t\).
a) Reduction to the case where G is smooth over S.

Proceeding as in 6.2, one sees that, after changing S if necessary, we may assume that \((\mathrm{G})_{\text{réd}}\) is a group prescheme smooth over S, which we shall denote by \(\mathrm{G}'\). Set \(\mathrm{X} = \mathscr{LC}_\mathrm{G}\), \(\mathrm{X}' = \mathscr{LC}_{\mathrm{G}'}\), and let H (resp. \(\mathrm{H}'\)) be the subgroup prescheme of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{X}\) (resp. \(\mathrm{G}'_{\mathrm{X}'}\)) universal for the functor \(\mathscr{LC}_\mathrm{G}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{LC}_{\mathrm{G}'}\)). Since H is smooth over X, \(\mathrm{H} \times_\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{X}_{\text{réd}})\) is reduced, hence contained in \(\mathrm{G}'_{\mathrm{X}_{\text{réd}}}\), and it is an element of \(\mathscr{LC}_{\mathrm{G}'}(\mathrm{X}_{\text{réd}})\), whence a canonical morphism \(p : \mathrm{X}_{\text{réd}} \to \mathrm{X}'\). It is clear that \(p\) is a monomorphism; let us show that \(p\) is even an immersion. Let \(\mathrm{N}'\) be the normalizer of \(\mathrm{H}'\) in \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{X}'}\). The set of points \(s\) of \(\mathrm{X}'\) such that the immersion \(\mathrm{H}'_s \to \mathrm{N}'_s\) is an open immersion is an open set U, and \(\mathrm{H}'|_\mathrm{U} \to \mathrm{N}|_\mathrm{U}\) is an open immersion (Exp. VIB 2.5 and EGA IV 17.9.5). It follows from 5.1 iii) that U is the largest open subset of \(\mathrm{X}'\) above which \(\mathrm{H}'\) is equal to its connected normalizer in \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{X}'}\), hence \(\mathrm{H}'|_\mathrm{U} \in \mathscr{LC}(\mathrm{U})\). One immediately deduces that \(p\) is an isomorphism from \(\mathrm{X}_{\text{réd}}\) onto \(\mathrm{U}_{\text{réd}}\). If we know how to show that \(\mathscr{LC}_\mathrm{G}\) is of finite type when G is smooth, then \(\mathrm{X}'\) will be of finite type over S, so \(\mathrm{X}_{\text{réd}}\) will be of finite type (S is noetherian) and consequently will be contained in \(\mathscr{L}^\mathrm{M}_\mathrm{G}\) for M large enough, and the same will hold for X.
b) Case where S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field \(k\) of characteristic \(p > 0\) and G is an algebraic group smooth over \(k\).

Instead of using the infinitesimal neighbourhoods \(\mathrm{H}^{(n)}\) of a subgroup prescheme H of G, we shall use the radicial subgroups \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{H})\), kernels of the iterates of the Frobenius morphism in H (Exp. VIIA 4), which is legitimate here, given that \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{H})\) is contained in the infinitesimal neighbourhood of order \(p^n\) of the unit section of H. If T is a subtorus of G, \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{T}) = {}_{p^n}(\mathrm{T})\), and it is immediate by duality that \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}({}_{p^n}(\mathrm{T}))\) equals \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})\) for \(n\) large enough. We then have the following more precise proposition:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.10

Let \(k\) be a field of characteristic \(p > 0\), G a smooth \(k\)-algebraic group, T a maximal torus of \(\mathrm{G}_k\), \(m\) the smallest integer such that: \[ \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}({}_{p^m}(\mathrm{T}))^0 = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})^0. \] Then, for every \(k\)-prescheme S and every \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{LC}(\mathrm{S})\), we have: \[ \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{S}}(\mathrm{H}) = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{S}}({}_{\mathrm{F}^m}(\mathrm{H})). \] A fortiori, \(\mathscr{LC}\) is contained in \(\mathscr{L}^{p^m}\).

Since H is smooth over S, of finite presentation over S, and has constant nilpotent rank (namely that of G), we shall see in the next section (7.3) that the functor \(\mathscr{C}_\mathrm{H}\) of Cartan subgroups of H is representable by an S-prescheme, smooth over S (the reader will verify that the proof given of this property does not use the fact that \(\mathscr{LC}_\mathrm{H}\) is of finite type over S). It then follows from Exp. XIII 3.1 that one may consider the open set U of regular points of H.

Let \(\mathrm{S}'\) be an S-prescheme, \(g\) an element of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}')\) normalizing \({}_{\mathrm{F}^m}(\mathrm{H})_{\mathrm{S}'}\). To prove that \(g\) normalizes \(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), it suffices to prove that \(\operatorname{int}(g)\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is contained in \(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'}\); indeed, \(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'} \cap \operatorname{int}(g)\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) will then contain an open subgroup of \(\operatorname{int}(g)\mathrm{H}\), hence will be equal to \(\operatorname{int}(g)\mathrm{H}\), since the latter has connected fibres. After replacing \(\mathrm{S}'\) by a suitable \(\mathrm{S}''\), then \(\mathrm{S}''\) by S, we are reduced to proving that if \(g \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\) normalizes \({}_{\mathrm{F}^m}(\mathrm{H})\) and if \(u \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S})\), then \(\operatorname{int}(g)u \in \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{S})\).

Now let C be the unique Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{H}_\mathrm{S}\) which "contains" \(u\) (Exp. XIII 3.2). It suffices for us to show that \(\mathrm{C}' = \operatorname{int}(g)\mathrm{C} \subset \mathrm{H}\). Since \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{LC}(\mathrm{S})\), C is also a Cartan subgroup of G; but the latter possesses maximal tori, so (Exp. XII 7.1 (a)) C is the centralizer in \(\mathrm{G}^0_\mathrm{S}\) of its unique maximal torus T. It follows from the definition of \(m\) (and from the fact that two maximal tori of G are locally conjugate for fpqc (Exp. XII 7.1)) that we have: \[ \mathrm{C} = (\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T}))^0 = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}({}_{p^m}(\mathrm{T}))^0, \] whence by conjugation by \(g\): \[ \mathrm{C}' = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\operatorname{int}(g)({}_{p^m}(\mathrm{T})))^0. \] But \(\operatorname{int}(g)({}_{p^m}(\mathrm{T}))\) is a subgroup of multiplicative type of \(\operatorname{int}(g)({}_{\mathrm{F}^m}(\mathrm{H}))\), which equals \({}_{\mathrm{F}^m}(\mathrm{H})\) (\(g\) normalizes \({}_{\mathrm{F}^m}(\mathrm{H})\)), hence is contained in H. It then follows from Exp. XIII 2.1 (which is proved when the base is a field, but extends immediately to the case of an arbitrary base) that for \(\mathrm{C}'\) to be contained in H, it suffices that we have: \[ \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{C}' \subset \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}. \] Now, \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{C}' = \operatorname{int}(g)(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{C}) \subset \operatorname{int}(g)(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H})\).

On the other hand, if \(m \geqslant 1\), which we may assume, we have (using Exp. VIIA 4.1.2): \[ \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H} = \operatorname{Lie}({}_{\mathrm{F}^m}(\mathrm{H})) = \operatorname{Lie}(\operatorname{int}(g)({}_{\mathrm{F}^m}(\mathrm{H}))) = \operatorname{int}(g)(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}). \] Hence \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{C}' \subset \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}\), which completes the proof of 6.10.
We shall need another definition of the integer \(m\) introduced in 6.10.

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.11

Let G be a smooth algebraic group defined over an algebraically closed field \(k\) of characteristic \(p > 0\), T a maximal torus of G, \(\mathfrak{g} = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}\), and R the family of non-zero characters of T occurring in the representation of T in \(\mathfrak{g}\) induced by the adjoint representation of G. For every element \(r \in \mathrm{R}\), denote by \(e_r\) the largest integer \(n\) such that \(p^n\) divides \(r\) in the character group of T. Then \(m = \operatorname{Sup}_{r \in \mathrm{R}}(e_r + 1)\) if \(\mathrm{R} \neq \varnothing\), and \(m = 0\) otherwise.

Indeed, \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})\) is smooth and contained in \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}({}_{p^m}(\mathrm{T}))\), so: \[ (\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G} \mathrm{T})^0 = (\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}({}_{p^m}(\mathrm{T})))^0 \Longleftrightarrow \operatorname{Lie}(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G} \mathrm{T}) = \operatorname{Lie}(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}({}_{\mathrm{F}^m}(\mathrm{T}))) \] \[ \Longleftrightarrow \mathfrak{g}^\mathrm{T} = \mathfrak{g}^{{}_{p^m}(\mathrm{T})} \quad (\text{Exp. II 5.2.3}). \] Now with the usual notation, we have: \[ \mathfrak{g} = \mathfrak{g}_0 \oplus \coprod_{r \in \mathrm{R}} \mathfrak{g}^r. \] So \(\mathfrak{g}^\mathrm{T} = \mathfrak{g}_0\) and \(\mathfrak{g}^{({}_{p^m}(\mathrm{T}))} = \mathfrak{g}_0 + \coprod_{r \in \mathrm{R}'} \mathfrak{g}^r\), where \(\mathrm{R}'\) is the part of R formed by the characters of T whose restriction to \({}_{p^m}(\mathrm{T})\) is zero. But a non-zero character \(r\) of T has zero restriction to \({}_{p^m}(\mathrm{T})\) if and only if \(m \leqslant e_r\), whence the lemma.

c) Let us return to the proof of 6.4. We have reduced ourselves (by point a) and the paragraph preceding it) to the case where S is an integral noetherian scheme and G is smooth over S. We must show that the function \(d\) is bounded on a neighbourhood of the generic point \(t\) of S. After changing S if necessary, we may assume that G possesses a trivial maximal torus (6.2) \(\mathrm{T} = \mathrm{D}_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{M})\). Let then \(\mathfrak{g} = \coprod_{\lambda \in \mathrm{M}} \mathfrak{g}^\lambda\) be the decomposition of the Lie algebra of G according to the characters of T, and let R be the finite set of non-zero characters of M such that \(\mathfrak{g}^\lambda \neq 0\). Let us then distinguish two cases:

1st case: the point \(t\), and consequently all the points of S, have residue characteristic \(p > 0\). It is clear, in view of the above, that the function \(d\) is then bounded above by \(p^m\), where \(m\) is defined as in 6.11.

2nd case: the point \(t\) has residue characteristic zero. For every \(\lambda \in \mathrm{R}\), let \(n_\lambda\) be the largest integer dividing \(\lambda\) in the group M, and set \(n = \prod n_\lambda\), \(\lambda \in \mathrm{R}\). For every prime number \(q\) dividing \(n\), denote by \(\mathrm{S}_q\) the closed part consisting of the points of S whose residue characteristic equals \(q\), and let U be the non-empty open set (it contains \(t\)) complementary in S to the union of the \(\mathrm{S}_q\). If now \(s\) is a point of U, either \(s\) has residue characteristic zero, and then \(d(s) = 1\) (5.6), or \(s\) has residue characteristic \(p > 0\) not dividing \(n\), so the integer \(m\), relative to the group \(\mathrm{G}_s\), defined in 6.11, is less than or equal to one. Moreover, it follows from Exp. VII9 that we have: \[ \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}({}_{\mathrm{F}^1}(\mathrm{H})) = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}) = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H}^{(1)}). \] Finally, it follows from 6.10 that if \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{LC}_{\mathrm{G}_s}(\mathrm{S}')\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H}) = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{H}^{(1)})\), and consequently \(d(s) \leqslant 1\), so \(d\) is bounded by 1 on U.
This completes the proof of 6.4.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.12

Let S be a prescheme and G an S-group prescheme of finite presentation. Suppose that the nilpotent rank (resp. the dimension of the Borel subgroups) of the fibres of G is a locally constant function on S; then the functor \(\mathscr{C}\) of Cartan subgroups of G (resp. the functor \(\mathscr{B}\) of Borel subgroups of G) is representable by an S-prescheme of finite presentation over S.

Indeed, after restricting S if necessary, we may assume that the nilpotent rank \(\nu\) of the fibres is constant. But then it is clear that \(\mathscr{C}\) is represented by the open and closed subprescheme of the prescheme X representing \(\mathscr{LC}\) (6.4) above which the universal subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{X}\) relative to the functor \(\mathscr{LC}\) is of relative dimension \(\nu\). The proof is analogous for the functor \(\mathscr{B}\), taking into account the representability of the functor \(\mathscr{P}\).

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: the word "residue" has been removed.
