a hypothesis that is in fact superfluous, cf. XVII App. III 3.

pages.title: 7. Cartan subgroups of a smooth group

blocks.html: 7. Cartan subgroups of a smooth group

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.1

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite presentation, smooth over S, with connected fibres.

i) Let \(\mathscr{CT}\) be the S-functor \((\mathbf{Sch}/\mathrm{S})^\circ \to \mathbf{Ens}\) such that for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\) we have:

\(\mathscr{CT}(\mathrm{S}') =\) the set of subgroup preschemes H of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), smooth over \(\mathrm{S}'\), such that for every point \(s'\) of \(\mathrm{S}'\), \(\mathrm{H}_{\overline{s'}}\) is the centralizer in \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s'}}\) of a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s'}}\).

Then \(\mathscr{CT}\) is representable by an S-prescheme of finite presentation over S, smooth and quasi-projective over S.

ii) If S is artinian and \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{CT}(\mathrm{S})\), H is the centralizer in G of a maximal central subtorus of H (resp. If S is the spectrum of a henselian local ring, and if \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{CT}(\mathrm{S})\), H is the centralizer in G of a subgroup of multiplicative type of G, étale over S).

iii) If L is an S-group prescheme, smooth and of finite presentation over S, \(i : \mathrm{L} \to \mathrm{G}\) an S-monomorphism of groups, and H an element of \(\mathscr{CT}(\mathrm{S})\), then \(\underline{\operatorname{Transp}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{L})\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{Transpstr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{L})\) (cf. Exp. VIII 6.5 e)) are representable by closed subpreschemas of G, smooth over S.

iv) If \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{CT}(\mathrm{S})\), H is closed in G, \(\mathrm{N} = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H})\) is representable by a subgroup prescheme of G, closed and smooth over S; \(\mathrm{N}/\mathrm{H}\) is representable by an S-group prescheme, separated over S, étale and of finite type over S; \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\) is representable by an S-prescheme smooth and quasi-projective over S.

v) Let \(\mathrm{G}'\) be an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S and \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}'\) an S-morphism of groups, faithfully flat, so that \(\mathrm{G}'\) satisfies the same hypotheses as G (Exp. VIB 9). Then if \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{CT}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\), the image of H under \(u\) is representable by a subgroup prescheme \(\mathrm{H}'\) of \(\mathrm{G}'\) which is an element of \(\mathscr{CT}_{\mathrm{G}'}(\mathrm{S})\). Moreover \(\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{H}'\) is faithfully flat, and if H is the centralizer in G of a torus T, \(\mathrm{H}'\) is the centralizer in \(\mathrm{G}'\) of the torus \(\mathrm{T}' = u(\mathrm{T})\).

vi) Under the conditions of v), consider the S-morphism: \[ \tilde{u} : \mathscr{CT}_\mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \mathscr{CT}_{\mathrm{G}'}, \qquad \mathrm{H} \mapsto \mathrm{H}' = u(\mathrm{H}). \] Then \(\tilde{u}\) is a faithfully flat quasi-compact morphism; if moreover \(\operatorname{Ker} u\) is central, \(\tilde{u}\) is an isomorphism, the inverse isomorphism being \(\mathrm{H}' \mapsto u^{-1}(\mathrm{H}')\).

Proof of ii). For the first assertion, we may assume S local artinian with closed point \(s\). Let \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{CT}(\mathrm{S})\) and \(\mathrm{T}_{\bar{s}}\) the maximal central torus of \(\mathrm{H}_{\bar{s}}\), which is already defined over \(\kappa(s)\) (cf. 3.4). Since \(\mathrm{H}_s \in \mathscr{CT}(s)\), we have \(\mathrm{H}_s = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_s} \mathrm{T}_s\). The group H is smooth, so \(\mathrm{T}_s\) lifts (in a unique way) to a subtorus T of H, central in H (Exp. IX 3.6 bis and Exp. IX 5.6). But then \(\mathrm{H}' = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G} \mathrm{T}\) dominates H and has the same fibre as H; since H is flat over S, we have \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{H}'\) (Exp. VIB 2.5).

Suppose now that S is the spectrum of a henselian local ring, which we may assume noetherian by the usual reductions. Denote by \(s\) the closed point of S, \(\mathrm{T}_s\) the maximal central torus of \(\mathrm{H}_s\), \(q\) an integer invertible on S, \(\ell\) a power of \(q\), T an S-torus with closed fibre isomorphic to \(\mathrm{T}_s\) (Exp. X 4.6). On the other hand, let \({}_\ell\mathrm{H}\) be the "kernel" of raising to the \(\ell^{\text{th}}\) power in H, and let \(\mathrm{U}_\ell\) be the largest open subset of \({}_\ell\mathrm{H}\) which is étale over S. It then follows from 1.3 and from the fact that H is flat over S that \(\mathrm{U}_\ell\) dominates \({}_\ell\mathrm{T}_s\). Since S is henselian, there exists a unique S-morphism: \[ u_\ell : {}_\ell\mathrm{T} \longrightarrow \mathrm{U}_\ell \] which, on the closed fibre, induces the canonical immersion \({}_\ell\mathrm{T}_s \to (\mathrm{U}_\ell)_s\). Because of the uniqueness, one easily sees that \(u_\ell\) is a morphism of S-groups, central (Exp. IX 5.6 a)). Proceeding then as in 6.6 and 6.7, one shows that \(u_\ell\) is an immersion and that if \(\mathrm{M}_\ell\) is the image group \(u_\ell({}_\ell\mathrm{T})\), then \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{M}_\ell)\) equals H for \(\ell\) large enough (it is here that the hypothesis of S noetherian is used).

Proof of i). The group G is smooth over S, with connected fibres, so if \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{CT}(\mathrm{S})\), H has connected fibres (Exp. XII 6.6 b)) and is equal to its connected normalizer (6.5), so that the functor \(\mathscr{CT}\) defined in 7.1 i) coincides with the functor, also denoted \(\mathscr{CT}\), which was defined in 6.4.0. Hence, by theorem 6.4, \(\mathscr{CT}\) is representable by an S-prescheme of finite presentation and quasi-projective over S. It remains to show that this prescheme is smooth over S. We first reduce, by EGA IV 8, to the case where S is affine and noetherian. Thanks to Exp. XI 1.5, it then suffices to prove that if S is the spectrum of a local artinian ring, \(\mathrm{S}_0\) a subscheme defined by a nilpotent ideal, then an element \(\mathrm{H}_0\) of \(\mathscr{CT}(\mathrm{S}_0)\) lifts to a subgroup prescheme H of G, smooth over S. Now by ii), \(\mathrm{H}_0 = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_0} \mathrm{T}_0\), where \(\mathrm{T}_0\) is a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_0\). Since G is smooth, \(\mathrm{T}_0\) lifts to a subtorus T of G (Exp. IX 3.6 bis), and it suffices to take \(\mathrm{H} = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})\), which is indeed smooth over S (Exp. XI 2.4 and lemma 2.5).

Proof of iii). Since H is smooth, with connected fibres, then by Exp. XI 6.11, \(\underline{\operatorname{Transp}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{L})\) is representable by a closed subprescheme of G, of finite presentation over S. To show that this transporter is smooth, we reduce, as above, to proving that if S is local artinian, \(\mathrm{S}_0\) a closed subprescheme of S, \(g_0 \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}_0)\) such that \(\operatorname{int}(g_0)\mathrm{H}_0 \subset \mathrm{L}_0\), then \(g_0\) lifts to \(g \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\) such that \(\operatorname{int}(g)\mathrm{H} \subset \mathrm{L}\). The group G being smooth over S, there exists a section \(g_1\) of G lifting \(g_0\); let \(\mathrm{H}' = \operatorname{int}(g_1)\). It is thus an element of \(\mathscr{CT}(\mathrm{S})\) such that \(\mathrm{H}'_0 \subset \mathrm{L}_0\). By ii), \(\mathrm{H}'\) is the centralizer in G of a torus \(\mathrm{T}'\) of \(\mathrm{H}'\). Since L is smooth, the torus \(\mathrm{T}'_0\) of \(\mathrm{L}_0\) lifts to a torus \(\mathrm{T}''\) of L (Exp. XI 3.6 bis). The group \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{L} \mathrm{T}''\) is contained in \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G} \mathrm{T}''\), has the same fibre as the latter (namely \(\mathrm{H}'_0\)) and is smooth, hence equals \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G} \mathrm{T}'' = \mathrm{H}''\). The subtori \(\mathrm{T}'\) and \(\mathrm{T}''\) of G are two liftings of \(\mathrm{T}'_0\), hence are conjugate by an element \(h\) of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\) reducing to the unit section of \(\mathrm{G}_0\) (Exp. IX 3.3 bis); the same therefore holds for their centralizers \(\mathrm{H}'\) and \(\mathrm{H}''\) in G. The section \(g = hg_1\) lifts \(g_0\), and we indeed have \(\operatorname{int}(g)\mathrm{H} \subset \mathrm{L}\).

If now \(g \in \underline{\operatorname{Transp}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{L})(\mathrm{S})\), for \(\operatorname{int}(g)\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{L}\) it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(\dim \overline{\mathrm{H}}_s = \dim \mathrm{L}_s\). It follows that if U denotes the open and closed subprescheme of S above which the fibres of H have the same dimension as those of L, the strict transporter of H into L, \(\underline{\operatorname{Transpstr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{L})\), is representable by the S-prescheme: \[ \mathrm{U} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \underline{\operatorname{Transp}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{L}). \]

Proof of iv). To see that if \(\mathrm{H} \in \mathscr{CT}(\mathrm{S})\), H is closed in G, we may assume S affine noetherian, then S the spectrum of a complete local ring (EGA IV 8); but then H is the centralizer in G of a subgroup of multiplicative type (by ii)), hence is closed since G is separated over S (Exp. VIB 5.2).

By iii), \(\mathrm{N} = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H}) = \underline{\operatorname{Transpstr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{H})\) is representable by a subgroup prescheme smooth over S and closed in G. Consider the S-morphism: \[ \mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \mathscr{CT}, \qquad g \mapsto \operatorname{int}(g)\mathrm{H}. \] It follows from iii) that this morphism is smooth; its image is therefore an open subset U of \(\mathscr{CT}\). One then proves, as in Exp. XI 5.3, that \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\) is representable by U, hence in particular is quasi-projective.

Let us now study the quotient \(\mathrm{N}/\mathrm{H}\). Thanks to EGA IV 8, to prove that \(\mathrm{N}/\mathrm{H}\) is representable, we may assume S affine noetherian, then S the spectrum of a local ring A of finite dimension10. We shall proceed by increasing induction on the dimension of S. If \(\dim \mathrm{S} = 0\), the property follows from Exp. VIA §4. Note now that if \(\mathrm{N}/\mathrm{H}\) is representable, it is separated over S (since H is closed in N by iv)), of finite type and étale over S (since N is smooth over S, of finite type, and H is open in N), so \(\mathrm{N}/\mathrm{H}\) is necessarily quasi-affine over S (SGA1 VIII.6.2). By effective descent of quasi-affine schemes (loc. cit. 7.9), we may replace A by its completion, hence assume S is the spectrum of a complete noetherian local ring. Let \(s\) be its closed point and \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{S} \setminus s\). By the induction hypothesis, \((\mathrm{N}|_\mathrm{U})/(\mathrm{H}|_\mathrm{U})\) is representable by a U-group K. Then let \(\mathrm{T}_s\) be the maximal central torus of \(\mathrm{H}_s\), \(q\) an integer invertible on S, \(\ell\) a power of \(q\), \(\mathrm{M}_\ell\) the unique central subgroup of multiplicative type of H lifting \({}_\ell\mathrm{T}_s\) (cf. ii)). Choose \(\ell\) large enough so that \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{M}_\ell) = \mathrm{H}\), and let \(\mathrm{N}' = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{M}_\ell)\). Since \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{M}_\ell) = \mathrm{H}\), we have \(\mathrm{N}' \subset \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H})\). Moreover, one verifies immediately that \(\mathrm{T}_s\) (hence also \((\mathrm{M}_\ell)_s\)) is a characteristic subgroup of \(\mathrm{H}_s\) (i.e. stable under \(\underline{\operatorname{Aut}}_{\text{gr}}(\mathrm{H}_s)\)), so \(\mathrm{N}_s\) normalizes \((\mathrm{M}_\ell)_s\) and consequently \(\mathrm{N}_s = \mathrm{N}'_s\). The proof of Exp. XI 5.9 then shows that the quotient \(\mathrm{N}'/\mathrm{H} = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{M}_\ell)/\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{M}_\ell)\) is representable by an S-group \(\mathrm{K}'\). Since \(\mathrm{N}'\) is smooth over S and \(\mathrm{N}'_s = \mathrm{N}_s\), \(\mathrm{N}'\) is an open subgroup of N (Exp. VIB 2.5) containing H, so the image of \(\mathrm{N}'|_\mathrm{U}\) in K is an open subgroup, isomorphic to \(\mathrm{K}'|_\mathrm{U}\). Let L be the S-prescheme obtained by gluing K and \(\mathrm{K}'\) via the preceding isomorphism, and let \(p\) be the S-morphism \(\mathrm{N} \to \mathrm{L}\) obtained by gluing the canonical projections \(\mathrm{N}|_\mathrm{U} \to \mathrm{K}\) and \(\mathrm{N}' \to \mathrm{K}'\). It is clear that \((\mathrm{L}, p)\) represents the quotient \(\mathrm{N}/\mathrm{H}\).

Proof of v). Suppose first that S is the spectrum of a field \(k\). The image \(\mathrm{H}'\) of H is then a smooth subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}'\). We must show that \(\mathrm{H}' \in \mathscr{CT}_{\mathrm{G}'}(\mathrm{S})\), which will follow from the following more precise lemma:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.2

Let \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}'\) be an epimorphism of smooth connected \(k\)-algebraic groups, T a torus of G, \(\mathrm{T}'\) its image in \(\mathrm{G}'\); then: \[ u(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G} \mathrm{T}) = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}'} \mathrm{T}'. \]

Write \(\mathrm{H} = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G} \mathrm{T}\), \(\mathrm{H}' = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}'} \mathrm{T}'\), \(\mathrm{H}'' = u(\mathrm{H})\). We have \(\mathrm{H}'' \subset \mathrm{H}'\). To prove that \(\mathrm{H}' = \mathrm{H}''\), we may assume the base field algebraically closed, and it suffices to prove that every Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{H}'\) is contained in \(\mathrm{H}''\). Indeed, \(\mathrm{H}''\) will then contain the open set of regular points of \(\mathrm{H}'\), so \(\mathrm{H}''\) will be an open subgroup of \(\mathrm{H}'\), and consequently will equal \(\mathrm{H}'\), since the latter has connected fibres. So let \(\mathrm{C}'\) be a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{H}'\); \(\mathrm{C}'\) is also a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}'\), since \(\mathrm{H}'\) is the centralizer of a torus \(\mathrm{T}'\), hence has the same reductive rank and the same nilpotent rank as \(\mathrm{G}'\). Set \(\mathrm{K} = (u^{-1}(\mathrm{C}'))^0_{\text{réd}}\). Since \(\mathrm{T}'\) is in the centre of \(\mathrm{H}'\), \(\mathrm{C}'\) contains \(\mathrm{T}'\), so K contains T. Then let C be a Cartan subgroup of K containing T. The torus T is contained in the unique maximal torus of C, which is central in C (Exp. XII 6.6 c)), so C is contained in \(\mathrm{H} = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G} \mathrm{T}\). Using now the fact that two Cartan subgroups of G are conjugate and that the image of a Cartan subgroup of G is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}'\) (Exp. XII 6.6), one deduces that C is also a Cartan subgroup of G; its image is therefore a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}'\); since it is contained in \(\mathrm{C}'\), we have \(u(\mathrm{C}) = \mathrm{C}'\), so \(\mathrm{C}'\) is indeed contained in \(\mathrm{H}''\).

We have thus established v) when S is the spectrum of a field \(k\). Let us now study the general case. Since G, H and \(\mathrm{G}'\) are of finite presentation over S, to prove that \(u(\mathrm{H})\) is representable and is an element of \(\mathscr{CT}_{\mathrm{G}'}(\mathrm{S})\), we reduce by the usual technique to the case where S is affine noetherian, then to the case where S is the spectrum of a local ring. By fpqc descent of subpreschemas of \(\mathrm{G}'\), we may even assume that S is the spectrum of a complete noetherian ring A.

Let us take up again the notation of ii), i.e.: Let \(\mathrm{T}_s\) be the maximal central torus of \(\mathrm{H}_s\) (\(s\) is the closed point of S), \(\mathrm{M}_\ell\) a subgroup of multiplicative type of H lifting \({}_\ell\mathrm{T}_s\) and such that \(\mathrm{H} = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G} \mathrm{M}_\ell\). Let \(\mathrm{T}'_s\) be the image of \(\mathrm{T}_s\) in \(\mathrm{G}'_s\). Since \(\mathrm{G}'\) is separated over S (Exp. VIB 5.2), the image of \(\mathrm{M}_\ell\) under \(u\) is a subgroup of multiplicative type \(\mathrm{M}'_\ell\) of \(\mathrm{G}'\) (Exp. IX 6.8). Then set \(\mathrm{H}' = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}'} \mathrm{M}'_\ell\), which is a smooth subgroup prescheme of \(\mathrm{G}'\). For every integer \(\ell'\) equal to a power of \(q\), there exists \(\ell\) such that \((\mathrm{M}'_\ell)_s\) dominates \({}_{\ell'}\mathrm{T}'_s\); we may therefore assume \(\ell\) chosen large enough so that \(\mathrm{H}'_s = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}'_s} \mathrm{T}'_s = u_s(\mathrm{H}_s)\), where the last equality follows from 7.2. The restriction of \(u\) to H, say \(v\), obviously factors through \(\mathrm{H}'\). Let us prove that \(v : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{H}'\) is a flat morphism. Since H and \(\mathrm{H}'\) are flat over S and \(\mathrm{H}_s \to \mathrm{H}'_s\) is flat, \(v\) is flat on a neighbourhood of \(\mathrm{H}_s\) (EGA IV 11.3.10 and 11.3.1). The morphism \(v\) is therefore flat on an open subgroup of H (Exp. VIB 2.2), so \(v\) is flat, H having connected fibres. The set-theoretic image of H is therefore an open subset of \(\mathrm{H}'\) (necessarily equal to \((\mathrm{H}')^0\)) which, endowed with its induced structure, represents the image sheaf \(u(\mathrm{H})\) (for the fpqc topology). The fact that \(u(\mathrm{H})\) is an element of \(\mathscr{CT}_{\mathrm{G}'}(\mathrm{S})\) then follows from 7.2.

Suppose now that H is the centralizer in G of a torus T, and let \(\mathrm{T}'\) be the image of T under \(u\), which is a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}'\) (Exp. IX 6.8). The image of H under \(u\) is contained in \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}'}(\mathrm{T}')\), coincides fibre by fibre with the latter (7.2) and is smooth over S, so \(u(\overline{\mathrm{H}}) = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}'}(\mathrm{T}')\).

Proof of vi). To show that \(\tilde{u}\) is a faithfully flat S-morphism, knowing that \(\mathscr{CT}_\mathrm{G}\) and \(\mathscr{CT}_{\mathrm{G}'}\) are smooth over S (by i)), it suffices to verify it on the geometric fibres. We are therefore reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field \(k\). Let \(\mathrm{H}' \in \mathscr{CT}_{\mathrm{G}'}(k)\), \(\mathrm{T}'\) its maximal central torus, T a subtorus of G whose image is \(\mathrm{T}'\), \(\mathrm{H} = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})\), so that \(\mathrm{H}' = u(\mathrm{H})\) (7.2), \(\mathrm{N} = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{H})\), \(\mathrm{N}' = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}'}(\mathrm{H}')\). We showed in iv) that \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\) (resp. \(\mathrm{G}'/\mathrm{N}'\)) is canonically identified with open neighbourhoods of H in \(\mathscr{CT}_\mathrm{G}\) (resp. of \(\mathrm{H}'\) in \(\mathscr{CT}_{\mathrm{G}'}\)). By means of these identifications, the restriction of \(\tilde{u}\) to \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\) coincides with the natural morphism: \[ w : \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}'/\mathrm{N}' \] deduced from \(u\) by passage to the quotient. Now \(w\) is an epimorphism of homogeneous spaces under G, hence is faithfully flat. This proves that \(\tilde{u}\) is a flat morphism such that \(u(\mathscr{CT}_\mathrm{G})\), which is an open subset of \(\mathscr{CT}_{\mathrm{G}'}\), contains every point of \(\mathscr{CT}_{\mathrm{G}'}(k)\). Since \(\mathscr{CT}_{\mathrm{G}'}\) is of finite type over \(k\), one deduces that \(\tilde{u}\) is surjective, hence is faithfully flat.
The complementary assertions contained in vi) in the case where \(\operatorname{Ker} u\) is central follow from Exp. XII 7.12.
The following theorem generalizes theorem 7.1 of Exp. XII:

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.3

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S, smooth, with connected fibres, and consider the S-functor \(\mathscr{C} : (\mathbf{Sch}/\mathrm{S})^\circ \to \mathbf{Ens}\) such that:

\(\mathscr{C}(\mathrm{S}') =\) the set of Cartan subgroups of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\).

i) The following conditions are equivalent:

a) The functor \(\mathscr{C}\) is representable.

b) The functor \(\mathscr{C}\) is representable by an S-prescheme smooth, quasi-projective, of finite presentation over S, with affine fibres.

c) The group G possesses, locally for the étale topology, a Cartan subgroup.

d) The group G possesses, locally for the faithfully flat topology, a Cartan subgroup.

e) The nilpotent rank of the fibres of G is a locally constant function on S.

ii) If the preceding conditions are fulfilled, two Cartan subgroups of G are locally conjugate for the étale topology. The set of regular points of the fibres of G (Exp. XIII 2.7) is an open set \(\mathrm{G}_{\text{rég}}\), of finite presentation over S, and every section of \(\mathrm{G}_{\text{rég}}\) over S is contained in one and only one Cartan subgroup of G.

iii) Let \(\mathrm{G}'\) be an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S and \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}'\) a faithfully flat S-morphism of groups, so that \(\mathrm{G}'\) is smooth over S, with connected fibres. Then if C is a Cartan subgroup of G, \(u(\mathrm{C}) = \mathrm{C}'\) is representable by a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}'\), and \(\mathrm{C} \to \mathrm{C}'\) is faithfully flat.

iv) Under the conditions of i) and iii), the morphism: \[ \tilde{u} : \mathscr{C}_\mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \mathscr{C}_{\mathrm{G}'}, \qquad \mathrm{C} \mapsto u(\mathrm{C}) = \mathrm{C}' \] is faithfully flat. If moreover \(\operatorname{Ker}(u)\) is central, \(\tilde{u}\) is an isomorphism.

v) For any complementary information concerning transporters and the relations with maximal tori, one may consult 7.1 and Exp. XII 7.1.
Proof. i) We shall show that b) \(\Rightarrow\) c) \(\Rightarrow\) d) \(\Rightarrow\) e) \(\Rightarrow\) b) \(\Rightarrow\) a) \(\Rightarrow\) d).

b) \(\Rightarrow\) c). Let \(s\) be a point of S. Since \(\mathscr{C}_s\) is smooth over \(\kappa(s)\), there exist points of \(\mathscr{C}_s\) whose residue field is a finite separable extension of \(\kappa(s)\). Such a point provides a Cartan subgroup of G locally for the étale topology.
c) \(\Rightarrow\) d) is quite clear.

d) \(\Rightarrow\) e). Let \(s \in \mathrm{S}\). By hypothesis, there exists an S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\), flat over S, whose image contains \(s\), such that \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) possesses a Cartan subgroup. Let \(s'\) be a point of \(\mathrm{S}'\) above \(s\). The nilpotent rank of the fibres of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is thus constant on \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}',s'}\), and consequently the nilpotent rank of the fibres of G is constant on \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S},s}\), which is the image of \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}',s'}\) (EGA IV 2.3.4 ii)). Let \(r\) be its value. It follows from 6.3 bis that the set \(\mathrm{E}_r\) formed by the points \(x\) of S such that the nilpotent rank of \(\mathrm{G}_x\) equals \(r\) is an ind-constructible part of S, hence contains a neighbourhood of \(s\) (EGA IV 1.10.1).

e) \(\Rightarrow\) b). The assertion is local on S, so we may assume that the nilpotent rank of the fibres of G is constant and equal to \(r\). For every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\), there is then an identity between the Cartan subgroups of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) and the subgroup preschemas of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), smooth over \(\mathrm{S}'\), of relative dimension \(r\), whose geometric fibres are the centralizers of a torus. Since \(\mathscr{CT}\) is representable, by 7.1, \(\mathscr{C}\) is representable by the open and closed subprescheme of \(\mathscr{CT}\) representing the subfunctor of \(\mathscr{CT}\) formed by the groups of relative dimension \(r\). The other assertions appearing in b) are contained in 7.1 i), except for the fact that the fibres of \(\mathscr{C}\) are affine, which follows from Exp. XII 7.1 d).

It is clear that b) \(\Rightarrow\) a). Let us show that a) \(\Rightarrow\) d). It follows from 6.2 iii) that the functor \(\mathscr{C}\) commutes with filtered inductive limits of rings, so if \(\mathscr{C}\) is representable, it is necessarily representable by an S-prescheme locally of finite presentation (EGA IV 8.14.2). To prove that \(\mathscr{C}\) is smooth over S, we are reduced to showing that if S is affine and \(\mathrm{S}_0\) is the closed subscheme defined by a nilpotent ideal J, then every Cartan subgroup \(\mathrm{C}_0\) of \(\mathrm{G}_0 = \mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_0\) lifts to a Cartan subgroup C of G. But the existence of \(\mathrm{C}_0\) implies that condition e) is satisfied for \(\mathrm{S}_0\), hence for S, which has the same underlying space, and one concludes from the fact that d) \(\Rightarrow\) b). Since \(\mathscr{C}\) is smooth over S and \(\mathscr{C} \to \mathrm{S}\) is surjective, we see that a) \(\Rightarrow\) d).

ii) Let C and \(\mathrm{C}'\) be two Cartan subgroups of G. Then \(\underline{\operatorname{Transp}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{C}')\) is representable by a prescheme smooth over S (7.1 iii)), with non-empty fibres (see Exp. XII 6.6 a) and c)). The fact that C and \(\mathrm{C}'\) are locally conjugate for the étale topology on S is then a consequence of Hensel's lemma (Exp. XI 1.10).
The other assertions of ii) are consequences of XIII 3.1 and XIII 3.2, taking i) into account.

iii) Let C be a Cartan subgroup of G. We know (7.1 v)) that \(u(\mathrm{C})\) is representable by a smooth subgroup \(\mathrm{C}'\) of \(\mathrm{G}'\). Since the fibres of \(\mathrm{C}'\) are Cartan subgroups of the fibres of \(\mathrm{G}'\) (Exp. XII 6.6 d)), \(\mathrm{C}'\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}'\).
iv) To prove that the morphism \(\tilde{u}\) is faithfully flat, one proceeds as in 7.1 vi).

If now \(\operatorname{Ker} u\) is central and \(\mathrm{C}'\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}'\), then \(u^{-1}(\mathrm{C}') = \mathrm{C}\) is smooth, with connected fibres (7.1 vi)), and its fibres are Cartan subgroups (Exp. XII 6.6 f)), so C is a Cartan subgroup of G, which completes the proof of iv), taking 7.1 vi) into account.

pages.title: 8. Criterion for representability of the functor of subtori of a smooth group

blocks.html: 8. Criterion for representability of the functor of subtori of a smooth group

8.0. In this section, if S is a prescheme and G an S-group prescheme, \(\mathscr{T}_\mathrm{G}\) (or simply \(\mathscr{T}\) if there is no ambiguity) denotes the S-functor \((\mathbf{Sch}/\mathrm{S})^\circ \to \mathbf{Ens}\) such that, for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\), we have: \[ \mathscr{T}(\mathrm{S}') = \text{the set of subtori of } \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}. \] We define likewise \(\mathscr{TC}\) as the functor of central subtori of G.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.1

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme and G an S-group prescheme of finite type. Then the following conditions are equivalent:

i) The functor \(\mathscr{T}_\mathrm{G}\) "commutes with adic limits of artinian local rings", i.e. for every S-prescheme of the form \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{A}) = \mathrm{S}'\), where A is a noetherian local ring complete for the topology defined by its maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\), the canonical map: \[ \mathscr{T}(\mathrm{S}') \longrightarrow \varprojlim_n \mathscr{T}(\mathrm{S}'_n) \quad (\text{where } \mathrm{S}'_n = \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{A}/\mathfrak{m}^n)) \] is bijective.

ii) As in i), but restricting to the case where A is a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field.

iii) As in ii), but restricting to the subfunctor \(\mathscr{T}^{(1)}\) of \(\mathscr{T}\) relative to the subtori of G of relative dimension 1.

i bis) For every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\) as in i) and every \(\mathrm{S}'\)-torus T, the canonical map: \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}'\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}) \longrightarrow \varprojlim_n \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}'_n\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}'_n}, \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'_n}) \] is bijective.

ii bis) As in i bis), but restricting to the case where A is a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field.

iii bis) As in ii bis), but restricting to the case where T is the multiplicative group \(\mathbb{G}_m\).

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 8.2

There is an analogous proposition on restricting to the central subtori of G and to central homomorphisms of a torus into G.
Proof. We shall use the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.3

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, T an S-torus, \(u : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{G}\) an S-morphism of groups. Suppose moreover that G is of finite presentation over S, or else that S is locally noetherian and G locally of finite type. Then:

a) \(\operatorname{Ker} u\) is a subgroup of multiplicative type of T.

b) The quotient \(\mathrm{T}' = \mathrm{T}/\operatorname{Ker} u\) is a torus.

c) The canonical monomorphism \(\mathrm{T}' \to \mathrm{G}\), deduced from \(u\) by passage to the quotient, is an immersion.

This lemma is a consequence of Exp. IX 6.8 when G is separated over S. In the general case, we reduce as usual to the case where S is noetherian. Let us first show that \(\mathrm{K} = \operatorname{Ker} u\) is flat. We may assume that S is the spectrum of an artinian local ring (EGA \(0_{\text{III}}\) 10.2.6), in which case G is separated (Exp. VIB 5.2), so K is of multiplicative type (Exp. IX 6.8) and a fortiori flat over S. Let us now prove that \(\operatorname{Ker} u\) is closed in T, which reduces us to the case where S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring (EGA II 7.2.1). Since T is flat, \(u\) factors through the identity component of the schematic closure in G of the generic fibre of G. We may therefore assume G flat with connected fibres, but then G is separated (Exp. VIB 5.2), so K is closed. This being so, it follows from Exp. X 4.8 b) that K is a subgroup of multiplicative type of T. The quotient \(\mathrm{T}' = \mathrm{T}/\mathrm{K}\) is then representable, and \(\mathrm{T}'\) is a group of multiplicative type (Exp. IX 2.7) whose fibres are tori, hence is a torus. The fact that the monomorphism \(\mathrm{T}' \to \mathrm{G}\) is an immersion then follows from Exp. VIII 7.9.
Proof of 8.1.

i) \(\Rightarrow\) i bis). Set \(\mathrm{T}_n = \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}'_n}\), \(\mathrm{G}_n = \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'_n}\) and let \((u_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}\) be an element of \(\varprojlim_n \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}'_n\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}_n, \mathrm{G}_n)\). For every integer \(n\), \(u_n(\mathrm{T}_n)\) is thus a subtorus \(\mathrm{T}'_n\) of \(\mathrm{G}_n\) (lemma 8.3). By hypothesis, there exists a unique subtorus \(\mathrm{T}'\) of G lifting \(\mathrm{T}'_n\) for every \(n\). Since \(\mathrm{T}'\) has affine fibres, one concludes thanks to 4.4.

i bis) \(\Rightarrow\) i). Let \((\mathrm{T}_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}\) be an element of \(\varprojlim_n \mathscr{T}(\mathrm{S}_n)\). By Exp. X 4.6, there exist an \(\mathrm{S}'\)-torus \(\mathrm{T}'\) and an \(\mathrm{S}_0\)-isomorphism: \[ u_0 : \mathrm{T}'_0 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{T}_0. \] Since \(\mathrm{T}_n\) is smooth over \(\mathrm{S}'_n\), \(u_0\) lifts to an \(\mathrm{S}'_n\)-morphism \[ u_n : \mathrm{T}'_n \longrightarrow \mathrm{T}_n \quad (\text{Exp. IX 3.6}) \] and we may assume the family \((u_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}\) coherent, hence coming from a morphism \(u : \mathrm{T}' \to \mathrm{G}\). The image of \(\mathrm{T}'\) under \(u\) is then a subtorus of G (lemma 8.3) which lifts \(\mathrm{T}_n\) for every \(n\).

The implications i) \(\Rightarrow\) ii) \(\Rightarrow\) iii) on the one hand, and i bis) \(\Rightarrow\) ii bis) \(\Rightarrow\) iii bis) on the other hand, are obvious. The implication iii) \(\Rightarrow\) iii bis) is proved like i) \(\Rightarrow\) i bis). It therefore suffices for us to prove: iii bis) \(\Rightarrow\) ii bis) \(\Rightarrow\) i bis).

ii bis) \(\Rightarrow\) i bis). With the terminology introduced in 4.3, assertion i bis) is true if and only if every element \((u_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}\) of \(\varprojlim_n \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}_n\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}_n, \mathrm{G}_n)\) is "admissible". For every point \(t\) of \(\mathrm{S}'\) distinct from the closed point \(s\) of \(\mathrm{S}'\), there exists an S-scheme \(\mathrm{S}''\), the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field, whose generic point projects onto \(t\) and whose closed point projects onto \(s\) (EGA II 7.1.9). One immediately deduces a valuative criterion for a family of morphisms to be admissible. That is to say, ii bis) \(\Rightarrow\) i bis).

iii bis) \(\Rightarrow\) ii bis). Let T be an S-torus, where S is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field. The torus T is then trivial (Exp. X 4.6), i.e. isomorphic to \((\mathbb{G}_{m,\,\mathrm{S}})^r\) for a suitable integer \(r\). Let \((u_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}\) be an element of \(\varprojlim_n \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}_n\text{-gr}}((\mathbb{G}_m)^r_{\mathrm{S}_n}, \mathrm{G}_n)\). By hypothesis, the restrictions of the \(u_n\) to each factor of \((\mathbb{G}_m)^r_\mathrm{S}\) come from a group morphism: \[ \mathbb{G}_{m,\,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}. \] Whence a product morphism \((\mathbb{G}_m)^r_\mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{G}^r\) which, composed with the morphism \(\mathrm{G}^r \to \mathrm{G}\) defined by the composition law in G, provides a morphism: \[ v : (\mathbb{G}_m)^r_\mathrm{S} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}. \] Given the existence of the group morphism \(u_n\), it is clear that \(u_n = v_n\). It remains to see that \(v\) is a group morphism, which translates into the fact that two obvious morphisms \(f, g : \mathrm{X} = (\mathbb{G}_m)^r_\mathrm{S} \times_\mathrm{S} (\mathbb{G}_m)^r_\mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{G}\) coincide. Let Z be the coincidence subscheme of \(f\) and \(g\). Since \((\mathbb{G}_m)^r_\mathrm{S}\) has connected fibres and \(v((\mathbb{G}_m)^r_\mathrm{S})\) contains the unit section, one sees as in 8.3 that \(v\) factors through the identity component of G, which allows us to assume G separated (Exp. VIB 5.2), so Z is closed. Moreover, since \(v_n\) is a group morphism, we have \(f_n = g_n\), so Z contains a neighbourhood of the closed fibre of X (EGA I 10.9.4), hence is schematically dense in X, X having smooth and irreducible fibres (Exp. IX 4.6), and consequently \(\mathrm{Z} = \mathrm{X}\), so \(f = g\).

blocks.title: Définition
blocks.html:
Definition 8.4

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite type, and \(\mathscr{S}\) the set of S-schemes \(\mathrm{S}'\), spectra of complete discrete valuation rings with algebraically closed residue field, with generic point \(t\), such that \((\mathrm{G}_t)^0_{\text{réd}}\) is smooth and possesses a Chevalley decomposition, i.e. is an extension of an abelian variety by a linear algebraic subgroup, smooth and connected, \(\mathrm{L}_t\) (this decomposition is then unique). If \(\mathrm{S}' \in \mathscr{S}\), denote by \(\mathrm{G}'\) (resp. \(\mathrm{L}'\)) the schematic closure in \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) of \(\mathrm{G}_t\) (resp. \(\mathrm{L}_t\)).

Under these conditions, we shall say that

"the abelian part of G does not degenerate into a toric part", or more briefly that G satisfies property AT,

if for every \(\mathrm{S}' \in \mathscr{S}\), \(\mathrm{L}_s\) has the same reductive rank as \(\mathrm{G}'_s\). (Intuitively, suppose that the quotient \(\mathrm{A} = \mathrm{G}'/\mathrm{L}\) is representable, in which case A is a flat group prescheme such that \((\mathrm{A}_t)^0_{\text{réd}}\) is an abelian variety. The condition "AT" then means that \(\mathrm{A}_s\) has zero reductive rank, hence that \((\mathrm{A}_s)^0_{\text{réd}}\) is an extension of an abelian variety by a unipotent group.)

Likewise, assuming moreover that G has connected fibres, we shall say that

G satisfies property ATC

if for every \(\mathrm{S}' \in \mathscr{S}\), the schematic closure Z of the centre \(\mathrm{Z}_t\) of \(\mathrm{G}_t\) satisfies AT.
These technical definitions are justified by the following proposition:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.5

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite type. Then:

a) For the functor \(\mathscr{T}\) of subtori of G to "commute with adic limits of artinian local rings" (cf. 8.1), it is necessary and sufficient that G satisfy property AT (8.4).

b) If G has connected fibres, for the functor \(\mathscr{TC}\) of central subtori of G to commute with adic limits of artinian local rings, it is necessary and sufficient that G satisfy property ATC (8.4).
We shall need the following technical lemma:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.6

Let S be the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, \(s\) the closed point of S, G an S-group prescheme flat and of finite type, \(\mathrm{T}_s\) a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_s\). Then there exist an S-scheme \(\mathrm{S}'\), the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, faithfully flat over S, with closed point \(s'\), and a subgroup scheme C of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), flat over \(\mathrm{S}'\), commutative, with connected fibres, such that \(\mathrm{C}_{s'}\) dominates \(\mathrm{T}_s \times_s s'\).

After replacing S by \(\mathrm{S}'\), the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring faithfully flat over S, we may assume that \(\mathrm{T}_s\) equals \((\mathbb{G}_m)^r_s\) and that the transcendence degree of \(\kappa(s)\) over the prime field is \(\geqslant r\) (EGA \(0_{\text{III}}\) 10.3.1 and EGA II 7.1.9). There then exists an element \(\bar{x}\) of \(\mathrm{T}_s(s)\) such that every algebraic subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{s}}\) which "contains" \(\bar{x}\) dominates \(\mathrm{T}_{\bar{s}}\) (cf. Exp. XIII proof of 2.1 (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (vii)). Since G is flat over S, through \(\bar{x}\) there passes a quasi-section (EGA IV 14.5.8), and consequently, after replacing S by the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring faithfully flat over S, we may assume that there exists a section \(x\) of G over S lifting \(\bar{x}\). Let \(\mathrm{C}_t\) be the commutative algebraic subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_t\) generated by \(x_t\) (Exp. VIB 7) and let C be the schematic closure of \(\mathrm{C}_t\) in G. It is clear that \(\mathrm{C}_s\) contains \(\bar{x}\), hence dominates \(\mathrm{T}_s\), and consequently the "identity component" of C will be a flat and commutative group scheme answering the question.

Proof of 8.5 a). Suppose that the functor \(\mathscr{T}\) commutes with adic limits of artinian rings, and let us show that G satisfies property AT. So let \(\mathrm{S}' \in \mathscr{S}\), and let \(\mathrm{T}_s\) be a maximal torus of \(\mathrm{G}'_s\). We must prove that \(\mathrm{T}_s\) is contained in \(\mathrm{L}_s\). The formation of L and \(\mathrm{G}'\) obviously commutes with faithfully flat extensions \(\mathrm{S}'' \to \mathrm{S}'\) of discrete valuation rings. After changing \(\mathrm{S}'\), we may therefore, by lemma 8.6, assume that there exists a subgroup scheme C of \(\mathrm{G}'_{\mathrm{S}'}\), flat and commutative, such that \(\mathrm{C}_s\) dominates \(\mathrm{T}_s\). But then \(\mathrm{T}_s\) is a central subtorus of \(\mathrm{C}_s\), and consequently lifts infinitesimally to a central subtorus (cor. 2.3). In view of the hypothesis made on G, \(\mathrm{T}_s\) lifts to a subtorus T of G. Obviously \(\mathrm{T}_t\) is contained in the linear component \(\mathrm{L}_t\) of \(\mathrm{G}_t\), so T is contained in L.

Suppose now that G satisfies property AT, and let us show that condition 8.1 iii) bis is satisfied. So let S be the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring A, complete, with algebraically closed residue field, \(\mathfrak{m}\) the maximal ideal of A, \(\mathrm{S}_n = \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{A}/\mathfrak{m}^n)\), and \(u_n\), \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), a coherent system of group morphisms \(u_n : (\mathbb{G}_{m,\,\mathrm{S}})_n \to \mathrm{G}_n = \mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}_n\). Let \(q\) be a prime number invertible on S. The integer \(\ell\) running over the powers of \(q\), there exists a unique S-morphism of groups: \[ {}_\ell u : {}_\ell\mathbb{G}_{m,\,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] lifting \(u_n|_{({}_\ell\mathbb{G}_m)_n}\) for every \(n\) (prop. 1.6 a)). Consequently, if there exists an S-morphism of groups \(u : \mathbb{G}_{m,\,\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{T}\) lifting \(u_n\) for every \(n\), its restriction to \({}_\ell\mathbb{G}_{m,\,\mathrm{S}}\) is uniquely determined. Taking into account the density theorem (Exp. IX 4.8 (a)), this proves the uniqueness of \(u\) and the fact that, to prove the existence of \(u\), we may allow ourselves a faithfully flat extension of the base. Now \((\mathrm{G}_{\bar{t}})^0_{\text{réd}}\) possesses a Chevalley decomposition, and this is already defined over a finite extension L of the fraction field K of A. After replacing S by the normalization of S in L, we may therefore assume that \(\mathrm{S} \in \mathscr{S}\). The morphism \({}_\ell u_t\) factors through \((\mathrm{G}_t)_{\text{réd}}\), so \({}_\ell u\) factors through the schematic closure \(\mathrm{G}''\) of \((\mathrm{G}_t)_{\text{réd}}\) in \(\mathrm{G}'\). Again thanks to the density theorem, one deduces that \(u_n\) factors through \(\mathrm{G}''\) for every \(n\). Since G has property AT, every subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}'_s\), and a fortiori of \(\mathrm{G}''_s\), is contained in \(\mathrm{L}_s\), so \(u_s\) factors through \(\mathrm{L}_s\). I claim that for every \(n\), \(u_n\) factors through \(\mathrm{L}_n\). Indeed, since \(\mathrm{L}_t\) is normal in \((\mathrm{G}'')_t\), L is normal in \(\mathrm{G}''\); on the other hand L is flat over S, so for every integer \(n\), the quotient \(\mathrm{H}_n = \mathrm{G}''_n/\mathrm{L}_n\) is representable (Exp. VIA §4). The image of \((\mathbb{G}_{m,\,\mathrm{S}})_n\) in \(\mathrm{H}_n\) is a subtorus of \(\mathrm{H}_n\) (Exp. IX 6.8) whose closed fibre is zero, so this image is zero and consequently \(u_n\) factors through \(\mathrm{L}_n\). But since \(\mathrm{L}_t\) is affine, one deduces that the family \(u_n\) is admissible (prop. 4.3), which completes the proof.
The proof of 8.5 b) is entirely analogous to that of 8.5 a), taking into account 8.2 and Exp. IX 5.6 a).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.7

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite type. Then:

i) If G has connected fibres and satisfies AT, it satisfies ATC.

ii) If G satisfies AT, every subgroup prescheme of G satisfies AT.

iii) If G is flat over S and the abelian rank of the fibres of G is a locally constant function on S, then G satisfies AT.
i) follows immediately from 8.5 and from Exp. IX 5.6 a).

ii) Let H be a subgroup prescheme of G. To prove that H satisfies property AT, we may assume that S is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring, and we must show that every coherent family of \(\mathrm{S}_n\)-morphisms of groups \[ u_n : (\mathbb{G}_{m,\,\mathrm{S}})_n \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_n \] is admissible (8.5 and 8.1 iii)). Now by hypothesis, G satisfies property AT, so there exists an S-morphism of groups \[ u : (\mathbb{G}_m)_\mathrm{S} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] lifting \(u_n\) for every \(n\). Proceeding as in the proof of 8.5, one sees that \(u|_{{}_\ell\mathbb{G}_{m,\,\mathrm{S}}}\) (where \(\ell\) runs over the powers of a prime number \(q\) invertible on S) factors through H. By density, one deduces that on the generic fibre, \(u_t\) factors through \(\mathrm{H}_t\). Since \((\mathbb{G}_m)_\mathrm{S}\) is reduced, it indeed follows that \(u\) factors through H.

iii) Let \(\mathrm{S}' \in \mathscr{S}\) (notation of 8.4). The group L is flat over S, its generic fibre \(\mathrm{L}_t\) is affine; under these conditions, one can show that \(\mathrm{L}_s\) is necessarily affine (XVII App. III, 2). Since G and L are flat, we have \(\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{G}'\), and the dimension of the fibres of G and L is constant on S (Exp. VIB 4), so that we have the inequalities: \[ \operatorname{abelian rank}\ \mathrm{G}_s \leqslant \dim \mathrm{G}_s - \dim \mathrm{L}_s = \dim \mathrm{G}_t - \dim \mathrm{L}_t = \operatorname{abelian rank}\ \mathrm{G}_t. \] The hypothesis implies that these inequalities are equalities. It follows that \((\mathrm{G}_s/\mathrm{L}_s)^0_{\text{réd}}\) is an abelian variety, hence has zero reductive rank, and consequently \(\mathrm{L}_s\) has the same reductive rank as \(\mathrm{G}_s\).
We are now in a position to prove the main theorems of this section:

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 8.8

Let G be a group prescheme of finite type over a locally noetherian prescheme S. Suppose G flat over S with connected fibres. Then:

a) For the functor \(\mathscr{TC}\) of central subtori of G to be representable, it is necessary and sufficient that G possess property ATC (8.4). Moreover, in this case, \(\mathscr{TC}\) is representable by an S-prescheme étale and separated over S.

b) Under the conditions of a), for every S-torus, the functor \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom} \text{cent}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G})\) of central homomorphisms of T into G is representable by an S-prescheme étale and separated over S.

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 8.9

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme and G an S-group prescheme of finite type, smooth over S. Then:

a) For the functor \(\mathscr{T}\) of subtori of G to be representable, it is necessary and sufficient that G possess property AT (8.4). Moreover, in this case, \(\mathscr{T}\) is representable by an S-prescheme smooth and separated over S; more precisely, the structural morphism \(\mathscr{T} \to \mathrm{S}\) admits a canonical factorization: \[ \mathscr{T} \xrightarrow{\ u\ } \mathrm{Y} \xrightarrow{\ v\ } \mathrm{S} \] where \(v\) is a smooth and quasi-projective morphism (hence of finite type) and \(u\) is a separated étale morphism.

b) Under the conditions of a), for every S-torus T, the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G})\) of homomorphisms of T into G is representable by an S-prescheme smooth and separated over S.

Proof of 8.8 a). If the functor \(\mathscr{TC}\) is representable, it commutes with adic limits of artinian rings, and consequently (8.2 and 8.5 b)) G possesses property ATC. To establish the converse, we shall use the following result, which will be proved in EGA VI and is also found in Murre's exposé: Sém. Bourbaki, May 1965, No. 294, Theorem 1, corollary 2.

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.10

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme, and \(\mathscr{F}\) a contravariant functor defined on \(\mathbf{Sch}/\mathrm{S}\) with values in the category of sets. For \(\mathscr{F}\) to be representable by an S-prescheme étale and separated, it (is necessary and) suffices that \(\mathscr{F}\) satisfy the five following properties:

i) \(\mathscr{F}\) is a sheaf for the fpqc topology (Exp. IV).

ii) \(\mathscr{F}\) commutes with inductive limits of rings (Exp. XI 3.2).

iii) \(\mathscr{F}\) commutes with adic limits of artinian local rings (8.1 i)).

iv) \(\mathscr{F}\) satisfies the "valuative criterion of separation", i.e. for every S-scheme \(\mathrm{S}'\) which is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, with generic point \(t\), the canonical map: \[ \mathscr{F}(\mathrm{S}') \longrightarrow \mathscr{F}(t) \] is injective.

v) \(\mathscr{F}\) is infinitesimally étale (Exp. XI 1.8).
Let us show that the functor \(\mathscr{TC}\) of 8.8 satisfies the five conditions of 8.10.

i) The functor \(\mathscr{T}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{TC}\)) is a sheaf for the fpqc topology as soon as G is of finite presentation over S. Indeed, every monomorphism from a torus into G is then an immersion (Exp. VIII 7.9), and the property follows from the fpqc descent of subpreschemas.

ii) Corollary 6.3 proves that the functor \(\mathscr{T}\) commutes with inductive limits of rings if G is of finite presentation over S; one immediately deduces that the same holds for \(\mathscr{TC}\).
iii) By virtue of 8.5, condition iii) is precisely equivalent to property ATC.

iv) follows simply from the fact that if S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, two subtori of G having the same generic fibre coincide, and more precisely coincide with the identity component of the schematic closure in G of their generic fibre.
v) follows from 2.3, since G is flat over S.

Proof of 8.8 b). Proceeding as in Exp. XI 4.2, one sees that it suffices to prove that the product group \(\mathrm{T} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{G}\) still satisfies property ATC, which is immediate from the definition.

Proof of 8.9 a). After replacing G by its identity component (Exp. VIB 3.10), we may assume G has connected fibres. If T is a subtorus of G, its centralizer \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})\) is then representable (Exp. XI 6.11) by a subgroup prescheme of G, smooth over S (Exp. XI 2.4), with connected fibres (Exp. XII 6.6 b)), hence is an element of \(\mathscr{CT}(\mathrm{S})\), where \(\mathscr{CT}\) is the functor defined in 7.1 i). It is clear that the map: \[ \mathrm{T} \longmapsto \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T}) \] defines an S-morphism: \[ u : \mathscr{T} \longrightarrow \mathscr{CT}. \] Since \(\mathscr{CT}\) is representable by an S-prescheme smooth and quasi-projective over S (7.1 i)), it suffices for us to prove that the morphism \(u\) is representable by a separated étale morphism.

After a suitable base change \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) (with \(\mathrm{S}' = \mathscr{CT}\), hence \(\mathrm{S}'\) locally noetherian), we are reduced to the following problem: let S be a locally noetherian prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, smooth and of finite type over S, with connected fibres, H a subgroup of G, smooth over S and with connected fibres. Consider the subfunctor X of \(\mathscr{TC}_\mathrm{H}\) such that, for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\), \(\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\) is the set of central subtori T of \(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) such that \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{T}) = \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'}\). We are going to show that X is representable by an S-prescheme étale and separated over S.

Indeed, by hypothesis, G satisfies property AT; hence so does H (8.7 ii)), and since H has connected fibres, H also satisfies property ATC (8.7 i)). On the other hand H is smooth over S, hence flat. By 8.8 a), \(\mathscr{TC}_\mathrm{H}\) is representable by an S-prescheme étale and separated over S. It then suffices for us to show that the canonical monomorphism \(\mathrm{X} \to \mathscr{TC}_\mathrm{H}\) is representable by an open immersion.

Set \(\mathrm{S}' = \mathscr{TC}_\mathrm{H}\) and let K be the centralizer in \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) of the "universal" central torus of \(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'}\). The group K is a group scheme smooth over \(\mathrm{S}'\), with connected fibres, which dominates \(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'}\). By definition, we have \(\mathrm{X} = \coprod_{\mathrm{K}/\mathrm{S}'} \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'}/\mathrm{K}\), which is indeed representable by the open and closed subprescheme of \(\mathrm{S}'\) above which \(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) and K have the same relative dimension.
One proves 8.9 b) in a way analogous to 8.8 b).

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.11

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth over S and of finite presentation. Then, if the abelian rank of the fibres of G is a locally constant function on S (in particular if the fibres of G are affine), the functor of subtori of G is representable by an S-prescheme, smooth and separated over S, and the same holds for the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G})\), for every S-torus T.

Indeed, the assertion is local on S, so we may assume S affine and the abelian rank of the fibres of G constant. One can then find (Exp. VIB §10) a noetherian affine scheme \(\mathrm{S}_0\) and an \(\mathrm{S}_0\)-group prescheme \(\mathrm{G}_0\), smooth, of finite type over \(\mathrm{S}_0\), such that G is S-isomorphic to \(\mathrm{G}_0 \times_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathrm{S}\). Moreover, the abelian rank of the fibres of an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S is an ind-constructible function (6.3 bis). By a standard argument (EGA IV 8), one concludes that in the present case we may assume the abelian rank of the fibres of \(\mathrm{G}_0\) constant on \(\mathrm{S}_0\). But then \(\mathrm{G}_0\) possesses property AT (8.7 iii)), and consequently (8.10) the functor of subtori of \(\mathrm{G}_0\) is representable by an \(\mathrm{S}_0\)-prescheme smooth and separated over \(\mathrm{S}_0\), whence the announced property for G. As for the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G})\), one proceeds analogously.
Generalization of 8.9.
The functor \(\mathscr{T}\) of subtori of a smooth group G not being necessarily representable, we are going to state sufficient conditions for a subfunctor of \(\mathscr{T}\) to be representable.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.12

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, smooth over S, with connected fibres, and \(\mathscr{F}\) a sub-S-functor of the functor \(\mathscr{T}\) of subtori of G, satisfying the following properties:

i) \(\mathscr{F}\) is a sheaf for the fpqc topology (Exp. IV).

ii) \(\mathscr{F}\) commutes with inductive limits of rings (Exp. XI 3.2).

iii) \(\mathscr{F}\) commutes with adic limits of artinian local rings (Exp. XI 3.3).

iv) \(\mathscr{F}\) is infinitesimally smooth over S (Exp. XI 1.8).

v) \(\mathscr{F}\) is stable under inner automorphisms of G, i.e. for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\), we have: \[ \mathrm{T} \in \mathscr{F}(\mathrm{S}') \text{ and } g \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}') \Rightarrow \operatorname{int}(g)\mathrm{T} \in \mathscr{F}(\mathrm{S}'). \] Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is representable by an S-prescheme, smooth and separated over S.

blocks.label: 8.12 bis
blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.12 bis

Let S and G be as above, T an S-torus and \(\mathscr{F}\) a subfunctor of \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G})\), satisfying the following properties:

i), ii), iii), iv) as above.

v) \(\mathscr{F}\) is stable under inner automorphisms of G, i.e. for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\), we have: \[ u \in \mathscr{F}(\mathrm{S}') \text{ and } g \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}') \Rightarrow \operatorname{int}(g)u \in \mathscr{F}(\mathrm{S}'). \] Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is representable by an S-prescheme smooth and separated over S.
Proof of 8.12. (The proof of 8.12 bis is entirely analogous and is left to the reader.)

Let \(u : \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{CT}\) (7.1 i)) be the S-morphism which to every element T of \(\mathscr{F}(\mathrm{S}')\) associates the element \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{T})\) of \(\mathscr{CT}(\mathrm{S}')\). Since \(\mathscr{CT}\) is representable by an S-prescheme smooth and quasi-projective (7.1 i)), we are reduced to proving the representability of \(u\). After the base change \(\mathscr{CT} \to \mathrm{S}\), we are reduced to the following problem: given S and G as above, H a smooth subgroup of G with connected fibres, we must represent the functor \(\mathscr{F}_\mathrm{H}\) such that \(\mathscr{F}_\mathrm{H}(\mathrm{S}') =\) the set of elements \(\mathrm{T} \in \mathscr{F}(\mathrm{S}')\) such that: \[ \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}'} = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{T}). \]
We are going to show that \(\mathscr{F}_\mathrm{H}\) is étale and separated over S. For this, it suffices to verify the five conditions of 8.10.

Conditions i), ii) and iii) of 8.10 follow easily from 8.12 i), ii) and iii). We have already remarked that \(\mathscr{TC}_\mathrm{H}\) is a separated and infinitesimally étale functor, so \(\mathscr{F}_\mathrm{H}\), which is a subfunctor of \(\mathscr{TC}_\mathrm{H}\), is separated and infinitesimally unramified (Exp. XI 1.8). It therefore suffices for us to show that \(\mathscr{F}_\mathrm{H}\) is infinitesimally smooth (loc. cit.). Let S be the spectrum of an artinian local ring, \(\mathrm{S}_0\) a subscheme of S defined by a nilpotent ideal, \(\mathrm{T}_0\) an element of \(\mathscr{F}_\mathrm{H}(\mathrm{S}_0)\); let us prove that \(\mathrm{T}_0\) lifts to an element T of \(\mathscr{F}_\mathrm{H}(\mathrm{S})\). By hypothesis (8.12 iv)), \(\mathrm{T}_0\) lifts to an element \(\mathrm{T}'\) of \(\mathscr{F}(\mathrm{S})\). On the other hand, H being smooth, \(\mathrm{T}_0\) lifts to a subtorus \(\mathrm{T}''\) of \(\mathrm{H}_\mathrm{S}\) (Exp. IX 3.6 bis) which is conjugate to \(\mathrm{T}'\) by an element \(g \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\) (loc. cit.), so \(\mathrm{T}'' \in \mathscr{F}(\mathrm{S})\) (8.12 v)). Since \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T}'')\) is smooth over S and coincides with \(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}_0}\) above \(\mathrm{S}_0\), \(\mathrm{T}''\) is in the centre of \(\mathrm{H}_\mathrm{S}\) (Exp. IX 5.6 a)) and its centralizer in G equals \(\mathrm{H}_\mathrm{S}\); in short \(\mathrm{T}'' \in \mathscr{F}_\mathrm{H}(\mathrm{S})\), and we take \(\mathrm{T} = \mathrm{T}''\).

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.13

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth and of finite presentation over S, with connected fibres, and let \(\mathscr{TD}\) be the subfunctor of \(\mathscr{T}\) whose set of points with values in an S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\) is the set of subtori T of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) such that, for every point \(s'\) of \(\mathrm{S}'\), \(\mathrm{T}_{s'}\) is contained in the derived subgroup (Exp. VIB 7) of \(\mathrm{G}_{s'}\). Then \(\mathscr{TD}\) is representable by an S-prescheme smooth and separated over S.

blocks.label: 8.13 bis
blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.13 bis

Let S and G be as above, T an S-torus, and let \[ \underline{\operatorname{Hom} \text{der}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G}) \] be the subfunctor of \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G})\) whose set of points with values in \(\mathrm{S}'\) is the set of \(\mathrm{S}'\)-morphisms \(u : \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}'} \to \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) such that, for every point \(s'\) of \(\mathrm{S}'\), \(u_{s'}\) factors through the derived group of \(\mathrm{G}_{s'}\). Then this functor is representable by an S-prescheme smooth and separated over S.

Corollary 8.13 and corollary 8.13 bis are proved in an analogous way; let us prove, for example, 8.13 bis. By the usual procedure, we reduce to the case where S is noetherian. Let us verify that the five conditions of 8.12 bis are satisfied:

Conditions i) and iv) follow immediately from the corresponding properties of the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G})\). Condition v) is satisfied because the derived group of an algebraic group is normal (Exp. VIB §7). To establish ii), we are reduced by a standard reduction (EGA IV 8) to proving that if S is an integral noetherian scheme with generic point \(\eta\) and if \(u : \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{G}\) is an S-morphism of groups which on the generic fibre factors through the derived group of \(\mathrm{G}_\eta\), then there exists a neighbourhood U of \(\eta\) such that, for every point \(s\) of U, \(u_s\) factors through the derived group of \(\mathrm{G}_s\). But this follows immediately from Exp. VIB 10.12. To establish iii), let us take up again the notation of 4.3. To show that an element \((u_m)_{m \in \mathbb{N}}\) of \(\varprojlim_m \underline{\operatorname{Hom} \text{der}}_{\mathrm{S}_m\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}_m, \mathrm{G}_m)\) is "admissible", in the sense of 4.3, and comes from an element of \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom} \text{der}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G})\), we reduce immediately to the case where S is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring (cf. 8.1). Let \(t\) be the generic point and \(s\) the closed point of S, D the schematic closure in G of the derived group \(\mathrm{D}_t\) of \(\mathrm{G}_t\).

a) \(\mathrm{D}_s\) contains the derived group of \(\mathrm{G}_s\). Indeed, since \(\mathrm{D}_t\) is normal in \(\mathrm{G}_t\) and G flat over S, D is normal in G. Moreover the morphism: \[ \mathrm{G} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}, \qquad (x, y) \mapsto xyx^{-1}y^{-1} \] factors through \(\mathrm{D}_t\) on the generic fibre, hence factors through D. Consequently the algebraic group \(\mathrm{G}_s/\mathrm{D}_s\) is commutative, whence assertion a).

b) If \(u_m \in \underline{\operatorname{Hom} \text{der}}_{\mathrm{S}_m\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}_m, \mathrm{G}_m)\), then \(u_m\) factors through \(\mathrm{D}_m\). Indeed, by hypothesis, \(u_s\) factors through the derived group of \(\mathrm{G}_s\), hence a fortiori factors through \(\mathrm{D}_s\), by a). Since \(\mathrm{D}_m\) is flat over \(\mathrm{S}_m\) and normal in \(\mathrm{G}_m\), the quotient group \(\mathrm{H}_m = \mathrm{G}_m/\mathrm{D}_m\) is representable (Exp. VIA §4). Since the image of \(\mathrm{T}_m\) in \(\mathrm{H}_m\) is a torus (Exp. IX 6.8) whose closed fibre is zero, the image of \(\mathrm{T}_m\) is zero; that is to say, \(u_m\) factors through \(\mathrm{D}_m\).

c) The family \((u_m)_{m \in \mathbb{N}}\) is "admissible" and lifts to a morphism \(\mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{G}\) belonging to \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom} \text{der}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{G})\). In view of the above and 4.1 bis, it suffices to prove that \(\mathrm{D}_t\) is an affine algebraic group, which follows from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.14

Let G be a smooth connected algebraic group defined over a field \(k\). Then the derived group D of G is affine.

Since the formation of D commutes with extension of the base field (Exp. VIB 7), we may assume \(k\) algebraically closed. But then G is an extension of an abelian variety A by a linear group L. Since A is commutative, D is necessarily contained in L, hence is affine.
Maximal tori.

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 8.15

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme and G an S-group prescheme, smooth and of finite type over S. Then the following conditions are equivalent:

i) The S-functor \(\mathscr{TM}\), whose set of points with values in an S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\) equals the set of maximal tori of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) (Exp. XII 1.3), is representable.

ii) The preceding functor \(\mathscr{TM}\) is representable by an S-prescheme smooth and quasi-projective over S, with affine fibres.

iii) The group G possesses, locally for the étale topology, a maximal torus.

iv) The group G possesses, locally for the faithfully flat topology, a maximal torus.

v) The group G possesses property AT (8.4), and the reductive rank of its fibres is a locally constant function on S.
Proof: ii) \(\Rightarrow\) i) is clear.

i) \(\Rightarrow\) iii). Indeed, since G is of finite presentation over S, it follows from 6.3 that \(\mathscr{TM}\) commutes with inductive limits of rings, hence is locally of finite presentation if it is representable (EGA IV 8.14). Moreover \(\mathscr{TM}\) is formally smooth (Exp. XI 2.1 bis). So if it is representable, it is representable by a prescheme smooth over S, and iii) then follows from Hensel's lemma (Exp. XI 1.10).
iii) \(\Rightarrow\) iv) is clear.

iv) \(\Rightarrow\) v). Let \(s\) be a point of S. By hypothesis, there exists an S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\), flat over S, whose image contains \(s\), such that \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) possesses a maximal torus \(\mathrm{T}'\). Let \(s'\) be a point of \(\mathrm{S}'\) above \(s\). The reductive rank of the fibres of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is thus constant on \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}',s'}\), and consequently the reductive rank of the fibres of G is constant on the image of \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}',s'}\), which is \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S},s}\) (EGA IV 2.3.4 ii)). Now the reductive rank of the fibres of G is a locally constructible function on S (6.3 bis), so this rank is constant on a neighbourhood of \(s\) (EGA IV 1.10.1).

To see that G possesses property AT, consider an S-scheme \(\mathrm{S}_1\), the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, the closed point \(s_1\) of \(\mathrm{S}_1\) projecting onto the preceding point \(s\). The prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'_1 = \mathrm{S}_1 \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{S}'\) is faithfully flat over \(\mathrm{S}_1\) and \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'_1}\) possesses a maximal torus. Let A (resp. \(\mathrm{A}'\)) be the ring of \(\mathrm{S}_1\) (resp. \(\mathrm{S}'_1\)). Regarding \(\mathrm{A}'\) as an inductive limit of its sub-A-algebras of finite type, it follows from 6.3 that there exists an \(\mathrm{S}_1\)-scheme \(\mathrm{S}''_1\) such that \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}''_1}\) possesses a maximal torus and such that the structural morphism \(\mathrm{S}''_1 \to \mathrm{S}_1\) is of finite type and surjective. Using now EGA II 7.1.9, we may assume that \(\mathrm{S}'_1\) is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring. But then it is clear that \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'_1}\), hence also \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}_1}\), possesses property AT. Since this is true for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}_1\) that is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, G possesses property AT.

v) \(\Rightarrow\) i). Indeed, by 8.9, the functor \(\mathscr{T}\) of subtori of G is representable, and it is clear that \(\mathscr{TM}\) is representable by the open and closed subprescheme of \(\mathscr{T}\) representing the subfunctor of tori of rank \(r\), where \(r\) denotes the reductive rank of G (which we may assume constant).

iii) \(\Rightarrow\) ii). Indeed, if condition iii) is fulfilled, we can use the results of Exp. XII 7.1. The functor \(\mathscr{TM}\) is then canonically isomorphic to the functor of Cartan subgroups of G, and it suffices to apply 7.3 i).

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 8.16

One can show that the prescheme \(\mathscr{TM}\) of maximal tori of G is affine over S∗, which generalizes Exp. XII 5.4.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.17

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, smooth and of finite presentation over S. Suppose that the abelian rank and the reductive rank of the fibres of G are locally constant functions on S; then G satisfies the (equivalent) properties i) to iv) of 8.15.

We may assume that the abelian rank and the reductive rank of the fibres of G are constant. Proceeding as in 8.11, and taking 6.3 bis into account, we reduce to the case where S is noetherian. But then G possesses property AT (8.7) and one concludes by 8.15 v).

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.18

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, smooth over S. Then the following conditions are equivalent:

a) The unipotent rank \(\rho_u\) and the abelian rank \(\rho_{ab}\) (6.1 ter) of the fibres of G are locally constant functions on S.

b) The unipotent rank \(\rho_u\) is locally constant and G possesses, locally for the fpqc topology, maximal tori.

c) The reductive rank \(\rho_r\) (6.1 ter) and the abelian rank \(\rho_{ab}\) of the fibres of G are locally constant functions on S.

blocks.title: Remarques
blocks.html:
Remarks 8.19

Under the hypotheses of 8.18, a more precise argument, using the lower semicontinuity of the abelian rank (announced in Exp. X 8.7), allows one to show that if two of the three ranks \(\rho_u\), \(\rho_r\), \(\rho_{ab}\) are locally constant, then so is the third.
Proof of 8.18. After replacing G by its identity component, we may assume G of finite presentation over S (Exp. VIB 5.3.3).

a) \(\Rightarrow\) c). Let \(s\) be a point of S. Since \(\rho_{ab}\) is locally constant, it follows from 8.11 that, after making an étale extension covering S, we may assume that there exists a subtorus T of G whose fibre \(\mathrm{T}_s\) is a maximal torus of \(\mathrm{G}_s\). Let \(\mathrm{C} = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{T})\), which is a subgroup prescheme of G, smooth over S, with connected fibres. For every point \(t\) of S, \(\mathrm{C}_t\) obviously contains a Cartan subgroup \(\mathrm{C}'_t\) of \(\mathrm{G}_t\). After restricting S if necessary, we may assume \(\rho_u\), \(\rho_{ab}\) and the relative dimension of C over S constant. We then have the inequalities: \[ \dim \mathrm{C}_s = \dim \mathrm{C}_t \geqslant \dim \mathrm{C}'_t \geqslant \rho_u(t) + \rho_{ab}(t) + \dim \mathrm{T}_t = \rho_u(s) + \rho_{ab}(s) + \dim \mathrm{T}_s = \rho_n(s) = \dim \mathrm{C}_s. \] One deduces that \(\mathrm{C}_t = \mathrm{C}'_t\), hence that T is a maximal torus of G, and a fortiori \(\rho_r(t) = \rho_r(s)\).
c) \(\Rightarrow\) b) by 8.17.

b) \(\Rightarrow\) a). Indeed, since G possesses maximal tori locally for the fpqc topology, it possesses Cartan subgroups locally for the fpqc topology, and consequently (Exp. XII 7.3) the nilpotent rank \(\rho_n = \rho_u + \rho_r + \rho_{ab}\) is locally constant. Since \(\rho_r\) and \(\rho_{ab}\) are locally constant, \(\rho_u\) is locally constant.

footnotes.html: cf. M. Raynaud, Faisceaux amples sur les schémas en groupes et les espaces homogènes (thesis, to appear) (N.D.E.: see Lecture Notes Math. 119 (1970), Springer, in particular IX 2.9).
